question: $A B$ is a horizontal diameter of a ball of mass $m=0.4 {~kg}$ and radius $R=0.10 {~m}$. At time $t=0$, a sharp impulse is applied at $B$ at angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal, as shown in figure so that the ball immediately starts to move with velocity ${V_0} = 10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. Calculate the angular velocity of ball   just after impulse provided.
explanation:
Since, line of action of impulse does not pass through centre of mass of the sphere, therefore, just after application of impulse. the sphere starts to move, both translationally and rotationally. Translation motion is produced by horizontal component of the impulse, while rotational motion is produced by moment of the impulse. Let the impulse applied be $J$.https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954244/preptracker/questions/q_37077_explanation_1764954244032.png Then its horizontal component provides horizontal motion.
$J \cdot \cos 45^\circ $ where gives $J = 4\sqrt 2 \,kg\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Moment of inertial of ball about centroidal axis is
$l=\dfrac{2}{4} m R^{2}=1.6 \times 10^{-3} {~kg} {~m}^{2}$
Initial angular momentum of ball (about centre), $ = J\left. {\left( {R\sin 45^\circ } \right.} \right)$
Angular impulse will change angular momentum of the ball
$J \cdot R \cdot \sin 45^\circ = \Delta L = \left( {I{\omega _0} - 0} \right)$
which gives $\omega_{0}=250\,\,{rad} {s}^{-1}$ (clockwise)
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: Consider a body shown in the figure, consisting of two identical balls, each connected by a light rigid rod. If an impulse $J=M v(v=$ linear velocity of imparted to the body at one of its ends, what would be its angular velocity?
explanation:
System of balls will rotate about its entre of mass.
Initial angular momentum $=M v\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)$
Final angular momentum $=2 I \omega=2 M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2} \omega$
By conservation of momentum,
$M v\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)=2 M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2} \omega$
$\Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{v}{L}$
So, option (3) is correct.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: A solid sphere rests on a horizontal surface. A horizontal impulse is applied at a height h above the centre. The sphere starts pure rolling just after the application of impulse. The value of $\dfrac{h}{r}$ is
explanation:
From linear impulse
$J=m v$
From angular impulse
$\begin{aligned}& J h=I \omega=\dfrac{2}{5} m R^{2} \cdot\left(\dfrac{v}{R}\right) \\& \dfrac{h}{R}=\dfrac{2}{5}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: The two uniform discs rotate separately on parallel axles. The upper disc (radius $a$ and momentum of inertia $I_{1}$ ) is given an angular velocity $\omega_{0}$ and the lower disc of (radius $b$ and momentum of inertia $I_{2}$ ) is at rest. Now the two discs are moved together so that their rims touch. Final angular velocity of the upper disc is
explanation:
The two discs exert equal and opposite forces on each other when in contact. The torque due to these forces changes the angular momentum of each disc. Let $\omega_{1}$ and $\omega_{2}$ are the angular velocities of the two discs. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956900/preptracker/questions/q_35485_explanation_1764956899475.png The angular impulse on the two discs are
$fa\Delta t = {I_1}\left( {{\omega _0} - {\omega _1}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
and $fb\,\Delta t = {I_2}{\omega _2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eqns. (1) and (2), we get
$\frac{a}{b} = \frac{{{I_1}\left( {{\omega _0} - {\omega _1}} \right)}}{{{I_2}{\omega _2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
When slipping ceases between the discs, the contact points of the two discs have the same linear velocity, i.e.,
$a{\omega _1} = b{\omega _2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
On substituting $\omega_{2}$ in eq's (3), we get
$\omega_{1}=\dfrac{\left(I_{1} \omega_{0}\right)}{\left[I_{1}+\left(a^{2} I_{2} / b^{2}\right)\right]}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: A uniform solid sphere of radius $r$ is rolling on a smooth horizontal surface with velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega(v=\omega r)$. The sphere collides with the wall as shown in the figure. The coefficient of friction between the sphere and the edge $\mu=1 / 5$. Just after the collision the angular velocity of the sphere becomes zero. The linear velocity of the sphere just after the collision is equal to
explanation:
Impulse provided by the edge in the horizontal direction:
$\int N dt = m{v^\prime } - ( - mv)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Angular impulse by friction in the vertical direction
$fR = \mu R\int N dt = \frac{2}{5}m{R^2}\left( {\frac{v}{R}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955898/preptracker/questions/q_35482_explanation_1764955897939.png From eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\int N d t=2 m V \text { and } v^{\prime}=v$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: A thin uniform rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ is kept on a smooth horizontal surface such that it can move freely. At what distance from centre of rod should a particle of mass $m$ strike on the rod such that the point $\mathrm{P}$ at a distance $l /$ 3 from the end of the rod is instantaneously at rest just after the elastic collision?
explanation:
Let the impulse due to the particle be J
$J = m{v_{cm}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{CM}} = \frac{J}{m}({v_{CM}} - $ velocity of C.M. of the rod)
Angular impulse about centre of mass is
$Jx = \frac{{m{l^2}}}{{12}}\omega \Rightarrow \omega \frac{{12Jx}}{{m{l^2}}}$
For point A to be at rest instantaneously,
${\overrightarrow v _{trans}} - {v_{rot}} = 0$
or ${v_{cm}} = \frac{{\omega l}}{6}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955896/preptracker/questions/q_35476_explanation_1764955896431.png Thus $\frac{J}{m} = \frac{J}{6} \times \frac{{12Jx}}{{m{l^2}}}$
which gives, $x = \frac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: Consider a body, shown in the figure, consisting of two identical balls, each of mass $M$ connected by a light rigid rod. If an impulse $J = Mv$ is imparted to the body at one of its ends, what would be its angular velocity?
explanation:
Given system of two particles will rotate about its centre of mass.
Initial angular momentum $=M v\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)$
Final angular momentum $=2 M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2} \omega$
From conservation of angular momentum $M v\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)=2 M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2} \omega \Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{v}{L}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: An impulse $J$ is applied on a ring of mass $m$ along a line passing through its centre $O$. The ring is placed on a rough horizontal surface. The linear velocity of centre of ring once it starts rolling without slipping is
explanation:
Let $v$ be the velocity of $COM$ of ring just after the impulse is applied and $v$ ' its velocity when pure rolling starts. Angular velocity $\omega$ of the ring at this instant will be $\omega=\dfrac{v^{\prime}}{r}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956880/preptracker/questions/q_34889_explanation_1764956879646.png From impulse $=$ change in linear momentum, we have
$J = mv \Rightarrow v = J/m$
Between the two positions shown in the figure, force of friction on the ring acts backwards.
Angular momentum of the ring about bottom most point will remain conserved
$\therefore L_{i}=L_{f}$
$m v r=m v^{\prime} r+I \omega$
$m v r=m v^{\prime} r+\left(m r^{2}\right)\left(v^{\prime} / r\right)=2 m v^{\prime} r$
$v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{2}=\dfrac{J}{2 m}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: Two particles of equal mass $m$ at $A$ and $B$ are connected by a rigid light rod $AB$ lying on a smooth horizontal table. An impulse $J$ is applied at $A$ in the plane of the table and perpendicular to $A B$. The velocity of particle at $A$ is:
explanation: Let $l$ be the length of the rod.  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951382/preptracker/questions/q_34885_explanation_1764951382074.png $\begin{aligned}& v=\dfrac{J}{2 m} \\& J\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)=\left[m\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^{2}+m\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^{2}\right] \omega \\& \omega=\dfrac{J}{m l} \\& \therefore v_{A}=v+\dfrac{l}{2} \omega=\dfrac{J}{2 m}+\dfrac{J}{2 m}=\dfrac{J}{m}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: An impulse $J$ is exerted on a rod of mass $m$ and length $L$ at a distance $x$ from the $C.M.$ Find the value of $x$ at which the net velocity of point $P$ is zero.
explanation:
From linear impluse
$1pt(v{\rm{ - velocity}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,CM)$
From angular impluse
$J x=\dfrac{m L^{2}}{12} \omega$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951307/preptracker/questions/q_34668_explanation_1764951306904.png As the point $P$ velocity is zero.
$\begin{aligned}& v_{P}=v-\dfrac{l}{2} \omega=0 \\& v=\dfrac{l}{2} \omega\end{aligned}$
From the above three eq's $x=\dfrac{L}{6}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

question: A solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface. It is struck by a horizontal cue stick at a height $h$ above the surface. The value of $h$ so that the sphere performs pure rolling motion immediately after it has been struck is
explanation:
Let $v$ be the velocity of the centre of mass of the sphere and $\omega$ be the angular velocity of the body about an axis passing through the centre of mass.
The linear impulse is
$J = Mv$
The angular impulse is
$J(h-R)=\dfrac{2}{5} M R^{2} \times \omega$
From the above two equations, $v(h-R)=\dfrac{2}{5} R^{2} \omega$
From the condition of pure rolling, $v=R \omega$
$h-R=\dfrac{2 R}{5} \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{7 R}{5}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: A uniform rod $A B$ of mass $m$ and length $l$ at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. An impulse $P$ is applied to the end $B$ in a direction which is perpendicular to the length. The time taken by rod to turn through a right angle is :
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951303/preptracker/questions/q_34660_explanation_1764951302403.png Angular impulse about center of mass
$=\dfrac{p \times l}{2}$
Angular impulse $=$ Change in angular momentum
$\begin{aligned}& P \times \dfrac{l}{2}=\dfrac{m l^{2}}{12} \omega \\& \omega=\dfrac{6 P}{m l}\end{aligned}$
Time taken by the rod to turn by $90^{\circ}$ is
$t=\dfrac{T}{4}=\dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\dfrac{\pi}{2 \omega}=\dfrac{\pi m l}{12 P}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Angular Impulse

question: A cube of mass $m$ and edge length $l=1 {~m}$ is sliding without friction on a smooth horizontal floor with velocity $v_{0}=8 {~m} / {s}$ it strikes a long obstruction $P P$ of small height The obstruction is parallel to the leading bottom edge of the cube. The leading bottom edge of the cube gets pivoted with the obstruction and the cube starts rotating about the edge. Determine angular velocity of the cube immediately after the impact.
explanation:
Before the impact, there is no external force in the horizontal direction and the cube slides with uniform velocity $v_{0}$, and during the impact reaction forces of the obstruction stops its leading bottom edge and cause it to rotate about
its leading bottom.Let the velocity of the mass center and angular velocity of the cube immediately after the impact are $v_{C_{0}}$ and $\omega_{0}$.
These velocities are shown in the adjacent figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954190/preptracker/questions/q_37050_explanation_1764954190480.png We denote the angular momentum of the cube about axis coincident with the obstruction edge before and after the impact by $L_{P_{1}}$ and $L_{P_{2}}$.
Applying principle of conservation of angular momentum about an axis coincident with the obstruction, we have
${\vec L_{{P_1}}} = {\vec L_{{P_2}}} \Rightarrow {\vec r_{C/P}} \times m{\vec v_o} = {I_P}\vec \omega $
Using theorem of parallel axes for moment of inertia $I_{P}$ about the leading bottom edge, we get ${I_P} = {I_C} + mr_{C/P}^2 = \frac{2}{3}m{l^2}.$
Substituting this in the above equation, we have Angular velocity immediately after the impact
${\omega _o} = \frac{{3{v_o}}}{{4l}} = \frac{{3 \times 8}}{{4 \times 1}} = 4\,\,rad/s$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
topic: Angular Momentum and its Conservation for a Rigid Body
subtopic: Conservation of Angular Momentum of a Rigid Body about a Fixed Axis

question: A small block of mass $4\,kg$ is attached to a cord passing through a hole in a horizontal frictionless surface. The block is originally revolving in a circle of radius $0.5\,m$ about the hole, with a tangential velocity of ${4 {~m} / {s}}$. The cord is then pulled slowly from below, shortening the radius of the circle in which the block revolves. The breaking strength of the cord is $600\,N$. What will be the radius  of the circle when the cord breaks?
explanation:
The tension of the rope is the only net force on the block and it does not exert any torque about the axis of rotation. Hence, the angular momentum of the block about the axis should remain conserved. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951724/preptracker/questions/q_36914_explanation_1764951723863.png $ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,mvr = $constant
Let ${r_1} = 0.5\,m$ and ${v_1} = 4\,m/s$
Let ${r_{2}, v_{2}, T_{2}}$ be the radius, velocity and tension, respectively, when the string breaks.
$ \Rightarrow \,\,{T_2} = 600\,N$
$m{v_1}{r_1} = m{v_2}{r_2}{\text{ and }}{T_2} = mv_2^2/r$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{ }}m{v_1}{r_1} = m\sqrt {\frac{{{r_2}{T_2}}}{m}{r_2}} $
${r_2} = {\left( {\frac{{mv_1^2r_1^2}}{{{T_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}} = {\left( {\frac{{4 \times 16 \times 0.25}}{{600}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
$ = {\left( {\frac{{16}}{{600}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}} = 3.0\,m$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
topic: Angular Momentum and its Conservation for a Rigid Body
subtopic: Miscellaneous Problems on Angular Momentum
parent_topic: Angular Momentum and its Conservation for a Rigid Body

question: A man sits on a freely rotating stool holding two dumbbells, each of mass $2.0\,kg$. When his arms are extended horizontally [Fig. (a)], the dumbbells are $1.0\,m$ from the axis of rotation and the man rotates with an angular speed of $0.50\,rad/s$. The moment of inertia of the man plus stool is ${5.0 {~kg} \cdot {m}^{2}}$ and is assumed to be constant. The man pulls the dumbbells inward horizontally to a position $0.50\,m$ from the rotation axis [Fig. (b)]. Find the new angular speed of the man
explanation:
A man is seated on a stool that can rotate freely about a vertical axis with his arms stretched horizontally with equal weights in each hand. As no net external torque acts on the system that consists of the 'student, stool and dumbbells'. It means, the angular momentum of that system about the rotation axis must remain constant. The total angular momentum of the system of the student, the stool is conserved then,
$\vec{L}_{i}=\vec{L}_{f} \text { or } I_{i} \omega_{i}=I_{f} \omega_{f}$
Moment of inertia of the system,
$I_{\text {total }}=I_{\text {student }}+I_{\text {weights }}=5.0+2\left({mr}^{2}\right)$
Initial moment of inertia, ${r_{\text {initial }}=1.0 {~m}}$
Thus, ${I_{i}=5.0+2 \times(2.0)(1.0)^{2}=9.0 {~kg} \cdot {m}^{2}}$
Final moment of inertia, ${r_{\text {final }}=0.50 {~m}}$
Thus, ${I_{f}=5.0+2(2.0)(0.50)^{2}=5.0 {~kg} \cdot {m}^{2}}$
We now using conservation of angular momentum ${I_{f} \omega_{f}=I_{i} \omega_{i}}$
or ${\omega _f} = \left( {\frac{{{I_i}}}{{{I_f}}}} \right){\omega _i} = \left( {\frac{{9.0}}{{5.0}}} \right)(0.50) = 0.90\,rad/s$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic: Conservation of Angular Momentum of a Rigid Body about a Fixed Axis

question: An ice skater starts a spin with her arms stretched out to the sides. She balances on the tip of one skate to turn without friction. She then pulls her arms in so that her moment of inertia decreases by a factor of 2 . In the process of her doing so, her kinetic energy becomes ${n}$ times the initial kinetic energy.  Find  the value of ${n}$ is
explanation: Her angular momentum stays constant as ${I}$ is cut in half and ${\omega}$ doubles. Then ${\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}}$ doubles. So $n=2$

question: A small block of mass $3\,kg$ is attached to a cord passing through a hole in a horizontal frictionless surface. The block is originally revolving in a circle of radius $0.5\,m$ about the hole with a tangential velocity of $6\,m{\rm{/}}s.$ The cord is then pulled slowly from below, shortening the radius of the circle in which the block revolves. The breaking strength of the cord is $1000\,N.$ What will be the radius  of the circle when the cord breaks?
explanation:
The tension of the rope is the only net force on the block and it does not exert any torque about the axis of rotation. Hence, the angular momentum of the block about the axis should remain conserved.
$ \Rightarrow mvr = \,$ constant
Let $r_{2}, \mathrm{v}_{2}, \mathrm{~T}_{2}$ be the radius, velocity and tension respectively when the string breaks.According to law of conservation of momentum,
$\mathrm{mv}_{1} \mathrm{r}_{1}=\mathrm{mv}_{2} \mathrm{r}_{2}$
$\therefore \quad m{v_1}{r_1} = m\sqrt {\frac{{{r_2}{T_2}}}{m}{r_2}} $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because {T_2} = \frac{{mv_2^2}}{{{r_2}}}} \right)$
${m^2}v_1^2r_1^2 = {m^2}\frac{{{r_2}{T_2}}}{m}r_2^2$
${r_2} = {\left( {\frac{{mv_1^2r_1^2}}{{\,{T_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}} = {\left( {\frac{{3 \times 36 \times 0.25}}{{1000}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
$ = {\left( {\frac{{27}}{{1000}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}$
$\therefore {r_2} = 0.3\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A spherical body of mass ${m}$ and radius ${r}$ is released from rest along a smooth inclined plane of inclination ${\theta}$. The angular momentum of the body about the instantaneous point of contact after a time ' ${t}$ ' from the instant of release is
explanation:
The component of acceleration of the body after time ${t}$ is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951699/preptracker/questions/q_36653_explanation_1764951699489.png Component of acceleration along the plane is $g\sin \theta $and perpendicular to the plane is $g\cos \theta $.
Velocity of the body after time ${t}$ will be$v = u + at = gt\sin \theta {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (u = 0)$
Now, angular momentum of the particle is$L = mv{r_1} = m(gt\sin \theta )r$
$\therefore \,\,\,L = mgrt\sin \theta $.
So correct option is (2)

explanation:

For the given planets in solar system, ${\tau_{\text {ext }}=0}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{d L}{d t}=0 \Rightarrow L=}$ constant.

question: A thin uniform circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is rotating in a horizontal plane about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to the plane with angular velocity $\omega$. Another disc of same mass but half the radius is gently placed over it coaxially. The angular speed of the composite disc will be :
explanation:
Apply conservation of angular momentum.
${L_i} = {L_f}$
$\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}\omega = \left( {\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2} + \frac{{M{{(R/2)}^2}}}{2}} \right){\omega ^1}$
$ \Rightarrow {\omega ^1} = \frac{{4\,\omega }}{5}$

question: A particle performs uniform circular motion with an angular momentum $L$. If the frequency of particle motion is doubled and its kinetic energy is halved, the angular momentum becomes
explanation:
$\because$ Angular momentum,
$L = mvr = m{r^2}\omega \quad \quad (1)$
Also, kinetic energy, $K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
${{\rm{or }}}$ ${K = \frac{1}{2}m{{(r\omega )}^2} = \frac{1}{2}m{r^2}{\omega ^2}}$
$ \Rightarrow $ ${K = \frac{1}{2}\left( {m{r^2}\omega } \right)\omega = \frac{{L\omega }}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,[\therefore \,\,{\rm{from}}\,\,{\rm{eq}}\,.(1)]}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{ }}L = \frac{{2K}}{\omega }$
Given, $\quad \omega^{\prime}=2 \omega$
or $\quad K^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2} K$
Hence, new angular momentum,
$L^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 K^{\prime}}{\omega^{\prime}}=\dfrac{2\left(\dfrac{1}{2} K\right)}{2 \omega}=\dfrac{L}{4}$

question: A disc of mass $2\,kg$ and radius $0.2\,m$  is rotating with angular velocity $30{\kern 1pt} \,rad{s^{ - 1}}.$ What is angular velocity, if a mass of  $0.25\,kg$  is put on periphery of the disc?
explanation:
If no external torque acts on a system of particles, then angular momentum of the system remains constant, i.e. $\tau=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d L}{d t}=0 \Rightarrow L=I \omega=$ constant
$\Rightarrow I_{1} \omega_{1}=I_{2} \omega_{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}M{r^2}{\omega _1} = \frac{1}{2}(M + 2m){r^2}{\omega _2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Given, $M = 2\,kg,\,\,m = 0.25\,kg,$
$r = 0.2\,\,{m^2}$ and ${\omega _1} = 30\,rad{s^{ - 1}}.$
Hence, after putting the given values in Eq. (1), we get
$\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {(0.2)^2} \times 30$
$\quad = \frac{1}{2} \times (2 + 2 \times 0.25)\,{(0.2)^2} \times {\omega _2}{\text{ }}$
or $1.2 = 0.05\,{\omega _2}$ or ${\omega _2} = 24\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A cockroach of mass ' ${m}$ ' is moving with velocity ${v}$ in anticlockwise sense on the rim of a disc of radius R. The M.I. of the disc about the axis is ' ${I}$ ' and it is rotating in clockwise direction with an angular velocity ' ${\omega}$ '. If the cockroach stops then calculate the angular velocity of disc.
explanation:
The phenomenon occurs in the absense of external torque, so applying law of COAM, we have
${I_{\text {disc }} \omega-m v R=\left(I_{\text {disc }}+m R^{2}\right) \omega^{\prime}\left(I_{\text {disc }}=I\right)}$
${\Rightarrow \omega^{\prime}=\dfrac{I \omega-m v R}{I+m R^{2}}}$.

question:

Reason: When diver pulls his limbs, the moment of inertia decreases and on account of conservation of angular momentum his angular speed increases.
explanation: When a diver dives and performs several somersaults, the rotational kinetic energy of the diver increases due to the conservation of angular momentum as they pull their limbs. So correct option is (1).

question:

Reason: Angular momentum does not depend upon moment of inertia of the body.
explanation:
If $\tau=0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{d L}{d t}=0$
$\Rightarrow L=$ constant . Assertion is correct is stating that angular momentum $(L)$ remains conserved when the moment of inertia ( $I$ ) of a rotating body changes, $\left(\because E_{K, r o t}=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}\right)$ but its kinetic energy changes. Kinetic energy depends on both angular velocity $(\omega)$ and moment of inertia. So, reason is not correct statement as $L=I \omega$. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: Torque is equal to rate of change of angular momentum.

Reason: Angular momentum depends on moment of inertia.
explanation:
Torque $\tau=I \alpha$
But $\alpha=\dfrac{d \omega}{d t}$ and $L=I \omega$
$\Rightarrow \tau=\dfrac{d L}{d t}=\dfrac{d(I \omega)}{d t}=I \alpha$
So correct option is (2).

question: A thin horizontal circular disc is rotating about a vertical axis passing through its centre. An insect is at rest at a point near the rim of disc. The insect now moves along a diameter of the disc to reach its other end. During the journey of the insect, the angular speed of the disc
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the insect disc system,
$I=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}+m x^{2}$
where, $m=$ mass of insect
and $x=$ distance of insect from centre.
Clearly, as the insect moves along the diameter of the disc. Moment of inertia first decreases and then increases.
By conservation of angular momentum, angular speed first increases and then decreases.

question: A cubical block of side $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ is moving with velocity $2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ on a smooth horizontal surface. The surface has a bump at a point $\mathrm{O}$ as shown in figure. The angular velocity (in $\mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ ) of the block immediately after it hits the bump, is :
explanation:
Angular momentum of the block is conserved about point $\mathrm{O}$. The moment of inertia of the cube about the coner is
$\begin{aligned}& I_{0}=\left[\dfrac{m a^{2}}{6}+m\left(\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}\right]=\dfrac{2 m a^{2}}{3} \\& m v \dfrac{a}{2}=\dfrac{2}{3} m a^{2} \omega \Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{3 v}{4 a}=5 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

question: A ring of mass $\mathrm{M}$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is rotating about its axis with angular velocity $\omega$. Two identical bodies each of mass $m$ are now gently attached at the two ends of diameter of the ring. Because of this, the kinetic energy loss will be:
explanation:
As there is no external force angular momentum of the system is conserved.
$L_{i}=L_{f}$
Let $\omega^{\prime}$ is the new angular velocity of the system
${\rm{M}}{{\rm{R}}^2}\omega = \left( {{\rm{M}}{{\rm{R}}^2} + 2m{{\rm{R}}^2}} \right)\omega '$
$\omega^{\prime}=\dfrac{M \omega}{(M+2 m)}$
The loss is
$\begin{aligned}\Delta(\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}) & =\dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^{2} \omega^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\left(\mathrm{MR}^{2}+2 m \mathrm{R}^{2}\right) \mathrm{M}^{2} \omega^{2}}{(\mathrm{M}+2 m)^{2}} \\& =\dfrac{m \mathrm{M}}{(\mathrm{M}+2 m)}\left(\omega^{2} \mathrm{R}^{2}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $r$ slips on a rough horizontal surface. At some instant, it has translational velocity $v_{0}$ and angular velocity $\omega\left(=\dfrac{v_{0}}{2 \mathrm{r}}\right)$ about its centre as shown in figure. The translational velocity after the sphere starts pure rolling motion is
explanation:
Angular momentum about point of contact will be conserved
$\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \left(\dfrac{2}{5} M r^{2}\right) \dfrac{v_{0}}{r}+M r v_{0}=\left(\dfrac{2}{5} M r^{2}\right) \dfrac{v}{r}+M r v \\& \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{6}{7} v_{0}\end{aligned}$

question: A ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is rotating with angular speed $\omega$ about a fixed vertical axis passing through its centre $\mathrm{O}$ with two point masses each of mass $\dfrac{M}{8}$ at rest at $\mathrm{O}$. These masses can move radially outwards along two massless rods fixed on the ring as shown in the figure. At some instant the angular speed of the system is $\dfrac{8}{9} \omega$ and one of the masses is at a distance of $\dfrac{3}{5} R$ from O. At this instant the distance of the other mass from $\mathrm{O}$ is
explanation:
By conservation of angular moment https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951428/preptracker/questions/q_35470_explanation_1764951426441.png $M{R^2}\omega = \left[ {M{R^2} + \frac{M}{8}\frac{{9{R^2}}}{{25}} + \frac{{M{d^2}}}{8}} \right]\frac{{8\omega }}{9}$
${R^2} = \left( {\frac{{200{R^2} + 9{R^2} + 25{d^2}}}{{8 \times 25}}} \right)\frac{8}{9}$
$225{R^2} - 209{R^2} = 25{d^2}$
$ \Rightarrow d = \frac{{4R}}{5}$

question: A uniform circular disc of radius $r$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface and given a linear velocity $v_{0}$ and angular velocity $\omega_{0}$ as shown in the figure. The disc comes to rest after moving some distance to the right. It follows that
explanation:
Since the disc comes to rest, then $v=0$ and $\omega=0$. That means, the angular momentum about the instantaneous point of contact just after the time of stopping is zero. We know the angular momentum of the disc about $P$ remains constant because frictional force $f$, pass through point $P$ and net torque produced by $mg$ and N must be zero. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951532/preptracker/questions/q_35464_explanation_1764951531726.png $ \Rightarrow {L_{initial}} = {L_{Final}} \Rightarrow m{v_0}r - {I_0}{\omega _0} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow m{v_0}r = \frac{1}{2}m{r^2}{\omega _0} \Rightarrow 2{v_0} = {\omega _0}r$

question:
A uniform disc of mass $m$ and radius $R$ is rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ on a smooth horizontal surface. Another identical disc is moving translationally with velocity $v$ as shown.
When they touch each other, they stick together. The angular velocity of centre of mass of the system after contact will be
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& m v R-\dfrac{3 m R^{2} \omega}{2}=3 m R^{2} \omega^{\prime} \\& \Rightarrow \omega^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 v-3 R \omega}{6 R}\end{aligned}$

question: A disc is freely rotating with an angular speed $\omega$ on a smooth horizontal plane. If it is hooked at a rigid point $\mathrm{P}$ and rotates without bouncing about a point on its circumference. Its angular speed after the impact will be equal to
explanation:
The angular momentum of the disc about $\mathrm{P}$, just before and after the impact, remains the same
$\Rightarrow L_{2}=L_{1}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951530/preptracker/questions/q_35458_explanation_1764951529954.png where $L_{1}=$ angular momentum of the disc about $\mathrm{P}$ just before the impact
$L_{1}=I_{0} \omega=\dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} \omega$
$L_{2}=$ angular momentum of the disc about $\mathrm{P}$ just after the impact
$L_{2}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}+m r^{2}\right) \omega^{\prime}$
From eq's (1) & (2)
Just before the impact, the disc rotates about O. But just after the impact, the disc rotates about $\mathrm{P}$.
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} \omega=\dfrac{3}{2} m r^{2} \omega^{\prime} \Rightarrow \omega^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{3} \omega$

question: A body of mass $m$ and radius $r$ is released from rest along a smooth inclined plane of angle of inclination $\theta$. The angular momentum of the body about the instantaneous point of contact after a time $t$ from the instant of release is equal to
explanation:
Since surface is frictionless, the body does not rotate about its centre of mass. Only it slides parallel to the surface, i.e., $\omega=0$ and $v=(g \sin \theta) t$
The angular momentum after time $\mathrm{t}$ :
$L=m v r=m(g \sin \theta t) r=m g r t \sin \theta$

question: A man stands on a rotating platform with his arms stretched holding a $5\,kg$ weight in each hand. The angular speed of the platform is 1.2 rev ${s^{ - 1}}$. The moment of inertia of the man together with the platform may be taken to be constant and equal to $6\,kg\,{m^2}$. If the man brings his arms close to his chest with the distance of each weight from the axis changing from $100\,cm$ to $20\,cm$. The new angular speed of the platform is
explanation:
Initial moment of inertia,
$I_{1}=6+\left[2 \times 5 \times(1)^{2}\right]=16 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
Initial angular velocity, $\omega_{1}=1.2 \mathrm{rev} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$
Initial angular momentum, $L_{1}=I_{1} \omega_{1}$
Final moment of inertia,
$I_{2}=6+\left[2 \times 5 \times(0.2)^{2}\right]=6.4 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
Final angular speed $=\omega_{2}$
Final angular momentum, $L_{2}=I_{2} \omega_{2}$
According to law of conservation of angular momentum,
$L_{1}=L_{2}$ or $I_{1} \omega_{1}=I_{2} \omega_{2}$
${\omega _2} = \frac{{{I_1}{\omega _1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{\left( {16\,kg\,{m^2}} \right)\left( {1.2rev{s^{ - 1}}} \right)}}{{6.4\,kg\,{m^2}}} = 3rev{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A child is standing with his arms outstretched at the centre of a turntable that is rotating about its central axis with an angular speed $\omega_{0}$. Now, the child folds his hands back so that moment of inertia becomes 3 times the initial value. The new angular speed is
explanation:
Here,
Initial angular speed, $\omega_{i}=\omega_{0}$
Initial moment of inertia $=I_{i}$
Final moment of inertia $I_{f}=3 I_{i}$
According to the law of conservation of angular momentum, we get
${L_i} = {L_f}\,\,or\,\,{I_i}{\omega _i} = {I_f}{\omega _f}$
${\omega _f} = \frac{{{I_i}{\omega _i}}}{{{I_f}}} = \left( {\frac{{{I_i}}}{{{I_f}}}} \right){\omega _i} = \left( {\frac{{{I_i}}}{{3{I_i}}}} \right){\omega _0} = \frac{{{\omega _0}}}{3}$

question: A solid cylinder of mass $20\,kg$ and radius $20\,cm$ rotates about its axis with an angular speed of $100{\mkern 1mu} \,rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$. The angular momentum of the cylinder about its axis is
explanation:
Here, $M = 20\,kg$
$R = 20\,cm = 20 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m,\omega = 100\,{\mkern 1mu} rad{s^{ - 1}}$
Moment of inertia of the solid cylinder about its axis is
$I = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2} = \frac{{(20\,kg){{\left( {20 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\,m} \right)}^2}}}{2} = 0.4\,kg\,{m^2}$
Angular momentum of the cylinder about its axis is
$\left. {L = I\omega = \left( {0.4\,kg\,{m^2}} \right)100{\mkern 1mu} \,rad{s^{ - 1}}} \right) = 40\,Js$

question: A disc of moment of interia $'{I_1}'$ is rotating in horizontal plane about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its plane with constant angular speed $'{\omega _1}'$. Another disc of moment of interia $'{I_2}'$ having zero angular speed is placed co - axially on a rotating disc. Now, both the discs are rotating with constant angular speed $'{\omega _2}'$. The energy lost by the initial rotating disc is
explanation:
Net external torque on the system is zero.
Therefore, angular momentum of the system will remain same.
$ \Rightarrow {I_1}{\omega _1} = \left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right){\omega _2}$
$\frac{{{\omega _2}}}{{{\omega _1}}} = \frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_1} + {I_2}}}$
Energy lost $=E_{1}-E_{2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}{I_1}\omega _1^2 - \frac{1}{2}\left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right)\omega _2^2$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\omega _1^2\left[ {{I_1} - \left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right)\frac{{\omega _2^2}}{{\omega _1^2}}} \right]$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\omega _1^2\left[ {{I_1} - \left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right)\frac{{I_1^2}}{{{{\left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right)}^2}}}} \right][{\rm{using}}\,\,{\rm{eq}}.\,({\rm{1}})]$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\omega _1^2\left[ {\frac{{{I_1}{I_2}}}{{{I_1} + {I_2}}}} \right]$
subject: Physics

question: The moment of inertia of a ring about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to its plane is $I$. It is rotating with angular velocity $\omega$. Another identical ring is gently placed on it, so that their centres coincide. If both the rings are rotating about the same axis, then loss in kinetic energy is
explanation:
According to the law of conservation of angular momentum,
$I \omega=$ constant
Therefore for the given problem,
$\begin{aligned}& I \omega=I^{\prime} \omega^{\prime}=(I+I) \omega^{\prime} \\& \Rightarrow \omega^{\prime}=\dfrac{\omega}{2}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Loss in kinetic energy $\left(K_{L}\right)=K-K^{\prime}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} I^{\prime} \omega^{\prime 2}=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2}(2 I)\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{I \omega^{2}}{4}$
subject: Physics

explanation: As radius increases, moment of inertia also increases but angular momentum remains same.

question:
Assertion: The speed of spinning wind in a tornado is very high.


Reason: If no external torque acts on a body, its angular velocity remains conserved.
explanation:
In a tornado, the air from nearby regions gets concentrated in a small space there by decreasing the value of its moment of inertia. Since, $I \omega=$ constant, so due to decrease in momentof inertia of the air, its angular speed increases to a high value.
If no external torque acts, then
$I \omega=$ constant $I$ and $\omega$ may change.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

If the total force on the body is zero, then the total linear momentum of the body does not change with time.
If the total torque on the rigid body is zero, the total angular momentum of the body does not change with time.
explanation:
$F = \frac{{dP}}{{dT}} = 0 \Rightarrow P = $ constant
$\tau = \frac{{dL}}{{dt}} = 0 \Rightarrow L = {\rm{ }}$ constant

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: A merry-go-round, made of a ring-like platform of radius $R$ and mass $M$, is revolving with angular speed $\omega$. A person of mass $M$ is standing on its rim. At one instant, the person jumps off the round, radially away from the centre of the round (as seen from the round).The speed of the round afterward is
explanation:
When the person jumps off radially away from the centre, no torque is exerted i.e., $\tau=0$.
According to the principle of conservation of angular momentum, $I \omega=$ constant. As mass reduces to half From $2M\,\,to\,\,M)$, moment of inertia I becomes half. therefore, $\omega$ must become twice

question: A circular platform $s$ free to rotate in a horizontal plane about a vertical axis passing through its centre. A tortoise is sitting at the edge of the platform. Now, the platform is given an angular velocity $\omega_{0}$. When the tortoise moves along a chord of the platform with a constant velocity (w.r.t the platform), the angular velocity of the platform $\omega$ with time $t$ as
explanation:
Let $ACD$ be a chord as shown in the figure.
Since, there is no extrenal torque, angular momentum will remian conserved. The moment of inertia will first decrease till the tortoise moves from $A$ to $C$ and then increase as it moves from $C$ to $D$. Therefore, $\omega$ will initially increase and then decreases. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951390/preptracker/questions/q_34910_explanation_1764951389840.png So option correct option is (2).

question: A ring of mass $m$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is rolling down a rough inclined plane of angle $\theta$ with horizontal. Plot the angular momentum of the ring about the point of contact of ring and the plane as a function of time :
explanation:
Let $v$ and $\omega$ be the linear and anugular velocities of the ring which is in pure rolling on the inclined plane. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956886/preptracker/questions/q_34901_explanation_1764956885818.png The anugular momentum of the ring about point $O$ is
$L_{O}=I \omega+m v r$
$L_{O}=m r^{2}\left(\dfrac{v}{r}\right)+m v r=2 m v r$
Let $a$ be the linear acceleration of the centre of the ring.
$L_{O}=2 m r(a t)$ where $a=\dfrac{g \sin \theta}{\left(1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \quad L_{O} \propto t(a=$ constant $)$
So graph between $L$ and $t$ is a stargiht line passing through origin. So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question:
A disc of mass ' $m$ ' and radius ' $R$ ' is free to rotate in a horizontal plane about a vertical smooth fixed axis passing through its centre. There is a smooth groove along the diameter of the disc and two small balls of mass $m/2$ each are placed in it on either side of the centre of the disc as shown in the figure. The disc is given an initial angular velocity $\omega_{0}$ and released.
The angular speed of the disc when the balls reach the end of the disc is
explanation:
Let the angular speed of the disc when the balls reach the end be $\omega$. From conservation of angular momentum,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} m R^{2} \omega_{0}=\dfrac{1}{2} m R^{2} \omega+\dfrac{m}{2} R^{2} \omega+\dfrac{m}{2} R^{2} \omega \\& \Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{\omega_{0}}{3}\end{aligned}$

question: A hoop of radius $r$ and mass $m$ rotating with an angular velocity $\omega_{0}$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface. The initial velocity of the centre of the hoop is zero. What will be the velocity of the centre of the hoop when it ceases to slip?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955813/preptracker/questions/q_34877_explanation_1764955812874.png By conserving angular momentum about $P$
$\begin{aligned}& L_{i}=L_{f} \\& m r^{2} \omega_{0}=m v r+m r^{2} \times \dfrac{v_{0}}{r} \\& \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{\omega_{0} r}{2}\end{aligned}$

$L_{1}=L_{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& m v_{0} R=m v R+\dfrac{2}{5} m R^{2}\left(\dfrac{v}{R}\right) \\& \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{5 v_{0}}{7}\end{aligned}$

question: A uniform rod $AB$ mass $m$ and length $2 a$ is falling freely without rotation under gravity with $A B$ horizontal. Suddenly the end $A$ is fixed when the speed of the rod is $v$. The angular speed with which the rod begins to rotate is
explanation:
Let $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the rod. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955810/preptracker/questions/q_34869_explanation_1764955810530.png Angular momentum about $A$ will be conserved,
$\begin{aligned}& \text { i.e., } \quad L_{i}=L_{f} \\& m v a=I \omega \\& \Rightarrow m v a=\dfrac{m(2 a)^{2}}{3} . \omega \Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{3 v}{4 a}\end{aligned}$

question: A girl of mass $M$ stands on the rim of a frictionless merry-go-round of radius $R$ and rotational inertia $I$ that is not moving. She throws a rock of mass $m$ horizontally in a direction that is tangent to the outer edge of the merry-go-round. The speed of the rock, relative to the ground, is $v$, afterwards the linear speed of the girl is
explanation:
The initial angular momentum of the system is zero. The final angular momentum of the girl-plus-merrygo-round is $\left(I+M R^{2}\right) \omega$, which we will take to be positive. The final angular momentum we associate with the throw rock is negative: $ - mRv$, where $v$ is the speed of the rock relative to the ground.
From angular momentum conservation
$0=\left(I+M R^{2}\right) \omega-m R v \Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{m R v}{I+M R^{2}}$
The girl's linear speed is $R \omega=\dfrac{m v R^{2}}{I+M R^{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A sphere of mass $M$ rolls without slipping on a rough surface with centre of mass has constant speed $v_{0}$. If mass of the sphere is $m$ and its radius be $R$, then the angular momentum of the sphere about the point of contact is
explanation:
Since $\vec{L}_{P}=\vec{L}_{c m}+\vec{r} \times \vec{p}_{c m}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951340/preptracker/questions/q_34860_explanation_1764951340503.png $=I_{c m} \omega_{0}(-\hat{k})+M v_{0} R(-\hat{k})$
Since sphere is in pure rolling motion hence
$\begin{aligned}& \omega=v_{0} / R \\& \Rightarrow \vec{L}_{P}=\left[\dfrac{2}{5} M R^{2}\left(\dfrac{v_{0}}{R}\right)(-\hat{k})+M v_{0} R(-\hat{k})\right] \\& =\dfrac{7}{5} M v_{0} R(-\hat{k})\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: If the earth is treated as a sphere of radius $R$ and mass $M$. Its angular momentum about the axis of rotation with period $T$ is
explanation:
Angular momentum of earth about its axis of rotation,
$L=I \omega=\dfrac{2}{5} M R^{2} \times \dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\dfrac{4 \pi M R^{2}}{5 T}$

question:
If radius of earth is reduced to half without changing its mass, then match the following columns
A. Angular momentum of earth
B. Time period of rotation of earth
C. Rotational kinetic energy of earth
P. Will become two times
Q. Will become four times
R. Will remain constant
S. None
explanation:
In the absence of external torque, angular momentum remains constant.
$L=I \omega=\text { constant }$
$I \propto R^{2}$
$\therefore$ I will remain $\frac{1}{4}th$ or $\omega$ will become 4 times.
Therefore, time $\left( T \right)$ will becomes $\dfrac{T}{4}$.
Further, $K=\dfrac{L^{2}}{2 I}$
Since, angular momentum is constant and $I$ has become $\dfrac{1}{4} t h$.
Therefore, kinetic energy will become 4 times.

options.text: A - R, B - S, C - Q
A - P, B - Q, C- R
A - S, B - Q, C - P
A - Q, B - R, C - S

subject: Physics

question: Two disc are rotating about their axes, normal to the discs and passing through the centres of the discs. Disc $D_{1}$ has $2\,kg$ mass and $0.2{\rm{ }}m$ radius and initial angular of $50 \mathrm{rads}^{-1}$. Disc $D_{2}$ has $4\,kg$ mass $0.1\,m$ radius and initial angular velocity of $200\,\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$. The two disc are brought in contact face to face, with their axes of rotation coincident. The final angular velocity (in $\mathrm{rads}^{-1}$ ) of the system is
explanation:
Consider the two disc as a system. As there is no external torque the angular momentum is conserved about the central axis.
${I_1}{\omega _1} = \frac{{(2){{(0.2)}^2}}}{2}(50)$
${I_2}{\omega _2} = \frac{{(4){{(0.1)}^2}}}{2}(200)$
${L_i} = {L_j}{I_1}{\omega _1} + {I_2}{\omega _2} = \left( {{I_1} + {I_2}} \right)\omega $
$\omega = \frac{{\frac{{2{{(0.2)}^2}}}{2}(50) + \frac{{4{{(0.1)}^2}}}{2}(200)}}{{\frac{{2{{(0.2)}^2}}}{2} + \frac{{4{{(0.1)}^2}}}{2}}}$
$\omega = 100\,\,rad/{\rm{sec}}$

question: If the earth suddenly changes its radius $x$ times the present value, the new period of rotation would be
explanation:
As no torque is applied, angular momentum
$L = l\omega = {\rm{ constant}}$
$ = \left( {\frac{2}{5}M{R^2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right) = {\rm{ constant i}}{\rm{.e}}{\rm{., }}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{R^{2}}{T}=\text { constant } \Rightarrow \dfrac{R_{1}^{2}}{T_{1}}=\dfrac{R_{2}^{2}}{T_{2}} \\& \therefore \quad T_{2}=\dfrac{R_{2}^{2}}{R_{1}^{2}} T_{1}=\left(\dfrac{x R_{1}}{R_{1}}\right)^{2} \times 24 h=24 x^{2} h\end{aligned}$

explanation:

question: A circular disc is mounted on a vertical frictionless axle. Its radius is $r = 4\,m$ and moment of inertial $I = 100\,kg\,{m^2}$. Intially the disc is at rest. A $30\,kg$ boy stands on the edge and begins to walk along the edge at a speed of $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ relative to the ground. Find the angular velocity of the disc.
explanation:
As the system is at rest intially $L_{i}=0$.
According conservation of angular momentum
$\begin{aligned}& L_{i}=L_{f}=0 \\& L_{f}=I \omega-m v r\end{aligned}$
Where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the disc. $v$ is the velocity of the boy w.r.to ground, $r$ is the radius of the disc.
$\omega = \frac{{mvr}}{I} = \frac{{30 \times 2 \times 4}}{{100}} = 2.4\,rad/{s^2}$

explanation:

At some instant of time a viscous fluid of mass $m$ is dropped at the centre and is allowed to spread out and finally falls from the disc. The angular velocity during this period:
explanation:

explanation:

question: Calculate the angular momentum of a body whose rotational energy is 10 joule. If the angular momentum vector coincides with the axis of rotation and its moment of inertia about this axis is $\,8 \times {10^{ - 7}}kg{m^2}$
explanation:
Angular momentum is given by
$L = I\omega \left( {K = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow L = I\sqrt {\frac{{2K}}{I}}  = \sqrt {2KI} $
$L = \sqrt {2KI}  = \sqrt {2 \times 10 \times 8 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}  = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}kg{m^2} {s^{ - 1}}$

question: Which of the following statements are correct?
explanation:
Centre of gravity of a body is the point at which the total gravitational torque on body is zero. Centre of mass and centre of gravity coincides only for symmetrical bodies.
Hence statements (1) and (2) are incorrect.
A couple of a body produces rotational motion only.
Hence statement (3) is incorrect.
Mechanical advantage greater than one means that the system will require a force that is less than the load in order to move it.
Hence only statemnet (4) is correct.

options.text: Centre of mass of a body always coincides with the centre of gravity of the body.
Centre of mass of a body is the point at which the total gravitational torque on the body is zero.
A couple on a body produces both translational and rotational motion in a body.
Mechanical advantage greater than one means that small effort can be used to lift a large load.

subject: Physics

question: From a uniform disc of radius $R$, a circular section of radius $\dfrac{R}{2}$ is cut out. The centre of the hole is at $\dfrac{R}{2}$ from the centre of the original disc. Locate the centre of gravity of the resulting flat body.
explanation:
Let mass per unit area of the disc be $\sigma$.
$\therefore$ Mass of the disc $(M) = \pi {R^2}\sigma $.
Mass of the portion removed from the disc $\left(M^{\prime}\right)$
$=\pi\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2} \sigma=\dfrac{M}{4}$
The position of centre of mass of the remaining portion is
$x = \frac{{ - \frac{M}{4} \times \frac{R}{2}}}{{M - \frac{M}{4}}}$
$ = - \frac{{MR}}{8} \times \frac{4}{{3M}}$
$x = \frac{{ - R}}{6}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Center of Gravity

question: A small card board is balanced on the tip of a pencil. The centre of mass coincides with centre of gravity $G$. When the card board is in equilibrium (translational and rotational) then predict the correct option.
explanation:
As the card board is in translational equilibrium the net vertical force should be zero.
$\vec{R}=M \vec{g}$
For rotational equilibrium the net torque should be zero about any point. The torque about $G$ is
$\vec{\tau}_{G}=\sum \vec{r}_{i} \times \vec{F}_{g}=\sum \vec{r}_{i} \times \vec{m}_{i} \vec{g}=0$
Where $\vec{r}_{i}$ is the position vector of the $p$ article $m_{i}$ is the mass of the $i^{\text {th }}$ particle.
$\vec{g}$ is the acceleration vector.
For a small object $\vec{g}$ is same for all the particles
so $\vec{g}$ can be taken out of summation.
$\vec{g} \sum \vec{r}_{i} m_{i}=0 \Rightarrow \sum m_{i} \vec{r}_{i}=\dfrac{\vec{r}_{C M}}{\sum m_{i}}=0$
So the $CM$ coincides with centre of gravity
So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Center of Gravity

question: If linear density of a rod of length $3 m$ varies as $\lambda=2+x$, then the position of the centre of gravity of the rod is
explanation:
Linear density of the rod varies with distance as
$\begin{aligned}& \lambda=2+x \\& \dfrac{d m}{d x}=\lambda \text { [given] } \therefore d m=\lambda d x\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951309/preptracker/questions/q_34672_explanation_1764951309314.png Position of centre of mass
${x_{cm}} = \frac{{\int d m \times x}}{{\int d m}}$
$ = \frac{{\int\limits_0^3 {\left( {\lambda dx} \right) \times x} }}{{\int\limits_0^3 {\lambda dx} }} = \frac{{\int\limits_0^3 {\left( {2 + x} \right)} \times xdx}}{{\int\limits_0^3 {\left( {2 + x} \right)} \times dx}}$
$ = \frac{{\left[ {{x^2} + \frac{{{x^3}}}{3}} \right]_0^3}}{{\left[ {2x + \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}} \right]_0^3}} = \frac{{9 + 9}}{{6 + \frac{9}{2}}} = \frac{{12}}{7}m.$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Center of Gravity

question: An irregular shaped body like a cardboard is suspended by a string. The body is in equilibrium about the lines $A{A_1},B{B_1}\,\& \,C{C_1}$. Then the centre of gravity of the body lies
explanation: Centre of gravity is the intersection point of all the three lines. So it lies on all the three lines. So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

subtopic: Center of Gravity

question: A spring is compressed between two toy carts of mass $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$. When the toy carts are released, the springs exert equal and opposite average forces for the same time on each toy cart. If $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ are the velocities of the toy carts and there is no friction between the toy carts and the ground, then:
explanation:
Applying law of conservation of linear momentum
${m_1}{v_1} + {m_2}{v_2} = 0$
$\frac{{{m_1}}}{{\,{m_2}}} = - \frac{{{v_2}}}{{{v_1}}}{\rm{ or }}\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = - \frac{{{m_2}}}{{\,{m_1}}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: A massless spring of force constant $1000 {Nm}^{-1}$ is compressed a distance of 20 $cm$ between discs of 8 $kg$ and 2 $kg$ , spring is not attached to discs. The system is given an initial velocity $3 {~ms}^{-1}$ perpendicular to length of spring as shown in the figure. What is ground frame velocity of 2 $kg$ block  when spring regains its natural length.
explanation:
Let $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ are the velocities along the spring of $A$ and $B$ when spring regains it natural length then
$2{v_1} = 8{v_2}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\frac{1}{2}2v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}8v_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}1000{\left( {\frac{{20}}{{100}}} \right)^2}\quad \quad \quad \quad \,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2), ${v_1} = 4\,m{s^{ - 1}},{v_2} = 1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Ground frame velocity of $A$$=\sqrt{v^{2}+v_{1}^{2}}=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}=5 {~ms}^{-1}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: A child of mass 4 $kg$ jumps from cart $B$ to cart $A$ and then immediately back to cart $B$. The mass of each cart is 20 $kg$ and they are initially at rest. In both the cases the child jumps at $6 {~m} / {s}$ relative to the cart. If the cart moves along the same line with negligible friction with the final velocities of $v_{B}$ and $v_{A}$, respectively, find the ratio of $6 v_{B}$ and $5 v_{A}$.
explanation:
All the velocities shown in diagrams are w.r.t. ground.
After first jump: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956912/preptracker/questions/q_36967_explanation_1764956911601.png $20 v_{1}=4 v_{2} \text { and } v_{1}+v_{2}=6 \text { (given) }$
Solve to get $v_{1}=1 {~m} / {s}, v_{2}=5 {~m} / {s}$
When child arrives on $A$ : https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956912/preptracker/questions/q_36967_explanation_1764956912352.png $(20+4) v_{3}=4 v_{2} \Rightarrow v_{3}=5 / 6 {~m} / {s}$
After the second jump:https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956913/preptracker/questions/q_36967_explanation_1764956913451.png $v_{4}+v_{A}=6,24 v_{3}=20 v_{A}-4 v_{4}$
Solve to get $v_{A}=\dfrac{11}{6} {~m} / {s}, v_{4}=\dfrac{25}{6} {~m} / {s}$
when child arrives on $B$ :https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956914/preptracker/questions/q_36967_explanation_1764956914225.png $24{v_B} = 4{v_4} + 20{v_1}$
$ \Rightarrow 24{v_B} = 4\left( {\frac{{25}}{6}} \right) + 20 \times 1 \Rightarrow {v_B} = \frac{{55}}{{36}}m/s$
Now $\dfrac{6 v_{B}}{5 v_{A}}=\dfrac{6 \times 55 \times 6}{36 \times 5 \times 11}=1$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: A small ball of mass $m$ is projected with a minimum horizontal velocity $v_{0}$ on a smooth wedge of mass $M$ so that it will reach the highest point $P$ of the wedge. If $\dfrac{m}{M}=\dfrac{1}{4}$ and $R=0.60 {~m}$, then find the value of $v_{0}$. Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951726/preptracker/questions/q_36959_explanation_1764951726447.png If the ball reaches at point P,
the velocity of the ball with respect to wedge should be $\sqrt{g R}$.
Using work-energy theorem from centre of mass frame at $A$ and the highest point $P$
${W_{{\rm{ext}}}} + {W_{{\rm{int }}}} = {W_{{\rm{ext}}}} + 0 = {(\Delta K)_{{\rm{cm}}}}$
${W_{{\rm{gravity}}}} = {(\Delta K)_{{\rm{CM}}}}$
$ - mg(2R) = {\left[ {\frac{1}{2}\mu v_{{\rm{rel}}}^2} \right]_{{\rm{final}}}} - {\left[ {\frac{1}{2}\mu v_{{\rm{rel}}}^2} \right]_{{\rm{initial}}}}$
$ - mg2R = \frac{1}{2}\mu {(V + v)^2} - \frac{1}{2}\mu v_0^2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\mu=\left(\dfrac{m M}{m+M}\right)$ and $v=\sqrt{g R}$
As there is no external force acting on the system
in horizontal direction.
The linear momentum of the system should be conserved.
$i.\,e.$ $m v_{0}-m(V-v)+M V$
$ \Rightarrow V = \frac{{m\left( {{v_0} + v} \right)}}{{(m + M)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$ \Rightarrow \quad {v_0} = \sqrt {\left( {5 + \frac{{4m}}{M}} \right)gR} $
$ = \sqrt {\left( {5 + \frac{4}{4}} \right) \times 10 \times 0.6} = 6\,m/s$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: Figure shows a block $A$ of mass $9\,\,m$ having a smooth semicircular groove of radius $R$ placed on a smooth horizontal surface. A block $B$ of mass $m$ is released from a position in groove where its radius is horizontal. If $m=2 {~kg}$ and $R=2 {~m}$ then, find the speed  of bigger block when smaller block reaches its bottommost position. Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$.
explanation:
Conserving linear momentum in horizontal direction.https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956883/preptracker/questions/q_36955_explanation_1764956882661.png $m{v_1} = 9m{v_2}\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,{v_1} = \frac{{{v_0}}}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Now applying work energy theorem from centre of mass frame
${W_{{\rm{total}}}} = {(\Delta K)_{cm}}$
$ = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}\mu v_{{\rm{rel }}}^2} \right)_{{\rm{final }}}} - {\left( {\frac{1}{2}\mu v_{{\rm{rel }}}^2} \right)_{{\rm{inital }}}}$
$mgR = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{m \cdot 9m}}{{(m + 9m)}}$${\left( {{v_1} + {v_2}} \right)^2} = \frac{{9m}}{{20}}{\left( {\frac{4}{3}{v_2}} \right)^2}$
${v_2} = \sqrt {\frac{5}{4}gR} = \sqrt {\frac{5}{4} \times 10 \times 2} = 5\,m/s$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: Two balls $A$ and $B$ of equal masses are projected upward simultaneously, one from the ground with speed $50\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and other from height  $40\,m$ above the first ball high tower, with initial speed $30 {~ms}^{-1}$. Find the maximum height  attained by their centre of mass. Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
explanation:
Let us take balls '$A '$ and '$B$ ' as system the initial height of centre of mass from ground,
$y_{ic}=\dfrac{m \times 0+m \times 40}{2 m}=20 {~m}$
Initial velocity of centre of mass
${u_{cm}} = \frac{{m \times 50 + m \times 30}}{{2\,m}} = 40\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Acceleration of centre of mass, $a_{{cm}}=-g$
Using kinematics equation: $v_{{cm}}^{2}=u_{{cm}}^{2}+2 a_{{cm}} H$
Here $H$ is the maximum height reach by centre of mass of two balls from initial level.
$\therefore 0^{2}=40^{2}-2 \times 10 \times H \Rightarrow H=\dfrac{1600}{20}=80 {~m}$
Hence, maximum height reach by centre of mass from ground level will be
$h_{\max }=\left(h_{{cm}}\right)_{\text {initial }}+H=20+80=100 {~m}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: Two blocks $A$ and $B$ each of equal masses $m$ are released from the top of a smooth fixed wedge as shown in the figure. Find the magnitude of the acceleration of the centre of mass of the two blocks.  Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
explanation:
Let us consider incline surfaces of wedge as
$x$-axis and $y$-axis respectively. We can write accelerations of blocks $A$ and $B$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951728/preptracker/questions/q_36938_explanation_1764951727991.png ${{\vec a}_A} = g\sin 30\hat i$ and ${{\vec a}_B} = g\sin 60^\circ \hat j$
The acceleration of centre of mass of two blocks can be written as
${{\vec a}_{cm}} = \frac{{{m_A}{{\vec a}_A} + {m_B}{{\vec a}_B}}}{{{m_A} + {m_B}}}$
$ = \frac{{mg\sin {\text{ }}30^\circ {\text{ }}\hat i + mg\sin {\text{ }}60^\circ \hat j{\text{ }}}}{{m + m}}$
$ = \frac{{g\sin 30^\circ \hat i + g\sin 60^\circ \hat j}}{2}$
$ = \frac{g}{4}\hat i + \frac{{\sqrt 3 g}}{4}\hat j$
$ \Rightarrow \left| {{{\vec a}_{cm}}} \right| = \frac{g}{2} = 5.0\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Acceleration of Centre of Mass

question:
Both the blocks shown in the given arrangement are given together a horizontal velocity towards right. If ${x {~ms}^{-2}}$ be the subsequent acceleration of centre of mass of the system of blocks, find the minimum value of ${x}$
(Given, ${m_{A}=2 m_{B}}$ and ${g=10 {~ms}^{-2}}$, all in SI units)
explanation:
${F_{\text {ext }} \geq(0.2)(2 m+m)(10)=6 m}$
$\begin{aligned}& \bar{a}_{{COM}}=\dfrac{F_{{exI}}}{2 m+m}=\dfrac{6 m}{2 m+m}=2 {~ms}^{-2} \\& \therefore \quad x=2\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A circular plate of uniform thickness has a diameter of $112\,cm.$ A circular portion of diameter $84\,cm$ is removed from one edge as shown in the figure. The distance of centre of mass of the remaining portion from the centre of plate will be
explanation:
Let $m$ be mass of disc removed,
$\therefore \quad m = \frac{M}{{\pi {{(56)}^2}}} \times \pi {(42)^2}$
$ = \frac{{9M}}{{16}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{ (Surface}}\,{\text{density}}\,{\text{is constant)}}$
$\therefore $ Remaining mass $ = M - \frac{{9M}}{{16}} = \frac{{7M}}{{16}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{{7M}}{{16}} \times O{O_2} = \frac{{9M}}{{16}} \times O{O_1}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\therefore \quad O{O_1} = (56 - 42)cm = 14\,cm$
Substituting in equation (1) we get,
$O{O_2} = \frac{9}{7} \times 14\,cm = 18\,cm$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Center of Mass of Bodies having Cavities

question: A system consists of three particles, each of mass $m$ and located at $(1,1),(2,2)$ and $(3,3)$. The coordinates of the centre of mass are
explanation:
The coordinates of $C.M$ of three particle are
$x = \frac{{{m_1}{x_1} + {m_2}{x_2} + {m_3}{x_3}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}}}\,\& $
$y = \frac{{{m_1}{y_1} + {m_2}{y_2} + {m_3}{y_3}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}}}$
here ${m_1} = {m_2} = {m_3} = m$
so $x = \frac{{\left( {{x_1} + {x_2} + {x_3}} \right)m}}{{m + m + m}} = 2,$
$y = \frac{{\left( {{y_1} + {y_2} + {y_3}} \right)m}}{{m + m + m}} = 2$
so coordinates of $C.M.$ of three particle are $(2,2)$
$V = 300\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: A small disc of radius $2\,cm$ is cut from a disc of radius  $6\,cm.$ If the distance between their centres is  $3.2\,cm,$  what is the shift in the centre of mass of the disc?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955969/preptracker/questions/q_36494_explanation_1764955969231.png Let radius of complete disc is $a$ and that of small disc is $b$. Also, let centre of mass now shifts to $O_{2}$ at a distance $x_{2}$ from original centre.The position of new centre of mass is given by
$x_{C M}=\dfrac{-\sigma \pi b^{2} x_{1}}{\sigma \pi a^{2}-\sigma \pi b^{2}}$
where, $\sigma=$ mass per unit area.
Given, $a = 6\,cm,b = 2\,cm$
and ${x_1} = 3.2\,cm$
Hence,
$x_{C M}=\dfrac{-\sigma \times \pi(2)^{2} \times 3.2}{\sigma \times \pi \times(6)^{2}-\sigma \times \pi \times(2)^{2}}$
$ = - \frac{{12.8\pi }}{{32\pi }} = - 0.4\,cm$
Negative sign indicates the shift from the centre.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Displacement of Centre of Mass

question: A block of mass $m$ slides with velocity $v$ along a frictionless level surface towards a block of mass $4 m$ initially at rest. The velocity of centre of mass is
explanation:
Given, $m_{1}=m, v_{1}=v$
$\begin{aligned}& m_{2}=4 m \text {, and } v_{2}=0 \\& \text { As } \quad v_{C M}=\dfrac{m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \\& \text { So, } v_{C M}=\dfrac{m \times v+4 m \times 0}{m+4 m}=\dfrac{v}{5}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: Two masses of 6 and 2 unit are at positions $(6 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j})$ and $(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k})$, respectively. The coordinates of the centre of mass are
explanation:
Given, masses, $m_{1}=6$ unit and $m_{2}=2$ unit
Positions $=6 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}$ and $2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}$
To find centre of mass,
$\begin{aligned}x_{C M} & =\dfrac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \\& =\dfrac{6 \times 6+2 \times 2}{6+2} \\& =\dfrac{36+4}{8}=5 \hat{i} \\y_{C M} & =\dfrac{m_{1} y_{1}+m_{2} y_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \\& =\dfrac{6 \times(-7)+2 \times(+5)}{6+2} \\& =\dfrac{-42+10}{8}=-4 \hat{j} \\z_{C M} & =\dfrac{m_{1} z_{1}+m_{2} z_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{6 \times(0)+2 \times(-8)}{2+6} \\& =\dfrac{-16}{8}=-2 \hat{k}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Centre of mass lies on $5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Center of Mass of a Group of Particles

question: Four particles of mass ${m_{1}=3 m, m_{2}=4 m}$ ${m_{3}=3 m}$, and ${m_{4}}$ are placed at four corners of a square. What should be the value of ${m_{4}}$ so that the centre of mass of all the four particles are exactly at the centre of the square?
explanation:
As ${m_{1}=m_{3}}$, the C.O.M. of the two particles be at the centre of the square. If ${m_{2}=m_{4}}$ then the $C.M.$ of the two lie at the centre and hence total $C.M.$ the system also lies at centre.
${\Rightarrow m_{2}=m_{4}=4 m}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform square plate ${A B C D}$ has a mass of $5\,kg$. If two point masses of $1\,kg$, each are placed at the corners ${C}$ and ${D}$, as shown in the figure, then the centre of mass shifts to the point which lies on
explanation: As two equal masses are added on both side of X-axis, so C.O.M. will shifts towards the negative ${Y}$. Correct Option is (3).

question: In a gravity free room a man of mass ${m_{1}}$ is standing at a height ${h}$ above the floor. He throws a ball of mass ${m_{2}}$ vertically downward with a speed ${u}$. Find the distance of the man from the floor when the ball reaches the ground.
explanation:
Time taken by ball to reach the ground ${t=\dfrac{h}{u}}$
By conservation of linear momentum, speed of ${\operatorname{man} v=\left(\dfrac{m_{2} u}{m_{1}}\right)}$.
Therefore, the man will move upward by a distance ${=v t=\left(\dfrac{h}{u}\right)\left(\dfrac{m_{2} u}{m_{1}}\right)=\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} h}$
Total distance of the man from the floor
${=h+\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} h=\left(1+\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}\right) h}$.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: Two particles of masses $4\,kg$ and $6\,kg$ are approaching towards each other with accelerations of ${2 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$ and ${3 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$ respectively, on a smooth horizontal surface. Find the magnitude of acceleration of centre of mass of the system.
explanation:
The acceleration of centre of mass of the system ${\vec{a}_{c m}=\dfrac{m_{1} \vec{a}_{1}+m_{2} \vec{a}_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}}$
Since ${\vec{a}_{1}}$ and ${\vec{a}_{2}}$ are anti-parallel, so
${=\dfrac{|(4)(2)-(6)(3)|}{6+4}=1 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Acceleration of Centre of Mass

question: Two uniform rods of different materials ${M_{1}}$ and ${M_{2}}$ have lengths ${2 m}$ and ${4 m}$, respectively. The mass per unit length of rods ${M_{1}}$ and ${M_{2}}$ are ${1 {~kg} / {m}}$ and ${2 {~kg} / {m}}$, respectively. If the rods are arranged, as shown, the position of the centre of mass relative to ${O}$ point is
explanation:
Here,
Mass per unit length of rod ${M_{1}, \mu_{1}=1 {~kg} / {m}}$
Mass per unit length of ${\operatorname{rod} M_{2}, \mu_{2}=2 {~kg} / {m}}$
Length of ${\operatorname{rod} M_{1} L_{1}=2 m}$
Length of ${\operatorname{rod} M_{2}, L_{2}=4 m}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955965/preptracker/questions/q_36408_explanation_1764955965378.png Mass of ${\operatorname{rod} M_{1}, m_{1}=\mu_{1} L_{1}=(1 {~kg} / {m})(2 {~m})}$
${=2 {~kg}}$ (situated at ${C_{1}}$ )
Mass of ${\operatorname{rod} M_{2}, m_{2}=\mu_{2} L_{2}=(2 {~kg} / {m})(4 {~m})}$
${=8 {~kg}}$ (situated at ${C_{2}}$ )
From figure, ${x_{1}=1 m}$ and ${x_{2}=4 m}$
Thus, the position of centre of mass of the given system relative to point ${O}$ is,
$\begin{aligned}X_{C M} & =\dfrac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \\& =\dfrac{(2 {~kg})(1 {~m})+(8 {~kg})(4 {~m})}{2 {~kg}+8 {~kg}}=3.4 {~m}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: When a man jumps from a boat to the shore, the boat slightly moves away from the shore.

Reason: The total momentum should remain conserved.
explanation: When a man jumps from a boat to the shore, the boat does indeed slightly move away from the shore. This is because, according to the law of conservation of momentum, the total momentum of the system (man + boat) must remain conserved. When the man jumps, he gains momentum in the opposite direction, causing the boat to move slightly in the other direction. So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The centre of gravity of a body coincides with its centre of mass only if the gravitational field does not vary from one part of the body to the other.

Reason: Centre of gravity is independent of the gravitational field.
explanation:
Assertion is true for system of uniform mass density. The center of gravity of a body coincides with its center of mass only if the gravitational field does not vary from one part of the body to the other. But, clearly, centre of gravity is associated only when there is gravitational field.
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is false
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

explanation: The center of mass of a body such as ring may indeed lie where there is no mass. But reason is definition for COM. So correct option is (2).

explanation: The center of mass of a system does not move under the action of internal forces. The reason is also true, as internal forces like spring force is conservative. So correct option is (2).

Assertion: The centre of mass of uniform triangular lamina is centroid.

Reason: Centroid is centre of symmetry of mass of the triangular lamina.
explanation: As rigid body is stated of uniform density. The center of mass of a uniform triangular lamina is indeed its centroid. The reason is also true, as the centroid is indeed the center of symmetry of the mass distribution of the triangular lamina. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Acceleration due to gravity is zero at the centre of earth.

Reason: At the centre of earth, a body has centre of mass, but no centre of gravity.
explanation: At the centre of the Earth, all the mass surrounds the object equally, so that the attraction of particle on one side of ${\rm{COM}}$ conceals that of particle on the other side. Hence, there's no effective gravity acting on an object at the Earth's center, even though it still has mass. So correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: The centre mass of an electron and proton, when released remains at rest.

Reason: Proton is heavier than electron.
explanation: The centre of mass will remain at rest. As there are no external forces, but only internal coulomb forces due to charges. Reason is about masses (not charges). Here $\left(m_{p} r_{p}\right)=\left(m_{e} r_{e}\right)$$\Rightarrow$ Reason is true. So correct option is (2).

question:
Assertion: Position of centre of mass is independent of the reference frame.

Reason: Centre of mass is same for all bodies.
explanation: The centre of mass ${\rm{(COM)}}$ is an intrinsic property of a system and is independent of the reference frame. It is calculated based on the positions and masses of the particles within the system. ${\rm{COM}}$ of different bodies is different. So correct option is (3).

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: The centre of mass of a proton and an electron remains at rest if they are initially at rest relative to each other.

Reason: The centre of mass remain at rest, if no external force is applied on the system.
explanation: The centre of mass of a proton and an electron will remain at rest if they are initially at rest relative to each other. Coulomb attractive force between them is 'internal force' and is due to charges. So centre of mass remains at rest. So correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: The centre of mass of system of $n$ particles is the average of the position of all particles, weighted according to their masses.

Reason: The position of the centre of mass of a system is independent of co-ordinate system.
explanation: The centre of mass is calculated as the weighted average of the position vectors of the particles. Also, the centre of mass is an intrinsic property of the system and so is independent of the choice of coordinate system coordinates changes, but not position. So correct option is (2).

question: Find the velocity of centre of mass of the system shown in the figure.
explanation:
Here, $m_{1}=0.1 \mathrm{~kg}, v_{1}=\hat{i}$
Taking component of velocity along $x$ and $y$ axis
$v_{2}=2 \cos 30^{\circ} \hat{i}-2 \sin 30^{\circ} \hat{j}$

$=\dfrac{1 \times 2 \hat{i}+2\left(2 \cos 30^{\circ} \hat{i}-2 \sin 30^{\circ} \hat{j}\right)}{1+2}$

question: Figure shows a square plate of uniform thickness and side length $\sqrt 2 \,m$. One fourth of the plate is removed as indicated. The distance of centre of mass of the remaining portion from the centre of the original square plate is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956906/preptracker/questions/q_36019_explanation_1764956905529.jpg Shift in the position of centre of mass from the centre of the frame is $\Delta r_{C M}=\dfrac{-m_{\text {removed }} \times d}{M_{\text {initial }}-m_{\text {removed }}}$
Let $4 m_{0}$ be the initial mass of the frame

$\Rightarrow\left(\Delta r_{C M}\right)=\dfrac{1}{6} m$.

question: The centre of mass of three particles of mass $1\,kg,2\,kg$ and $3\,kg$ is at $(3,3,3)$ with reference to a fixed coordinate system. Where should be fourth particle of mass $4\,kg$ be placed, so that the centre of mass of the system of all particles shifts to a point $(1,1,1)$ ?
explanation:
We know that centre of mass of a solid body is given by
$\begin{aligned}& X_{C M}=\dfrac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}+m_{3} x_{3}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}} \\& Y_{C M}=\dfrac{m_{1} y_{1}+m_{2} y_{2}+m_{3} y_{3}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}} \\& Z_{C M}=\dfrac{m_{1} z_{1}+m_{2} z_{2}+m_{3} z_{3}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}}\end{aligned}$
Coordinates of centre of mass,
$\left(X_{C M}, Y_{C M}, Z_{C M}\right)=(3,3,3)$
Then
$1 \times {x_1} + 2 \times {x_2} + 3 \times {x_3} = (1 + 2 + 3)3\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When centre of mass shifts to $(1,1,1)$
$X_{C M}=Y_{C M}=Z_{C M}=1$ (given)
$1(1 + 2 + 3 + 4) = 1 + 2{\rm{ }}{x_2} + 3{\rm{ }}{x_3} + 4{\rm{ }}{x_4}\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Solving Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$4 x_{4}=10-18 \Rightarrow x_{4}=-2$
Similarly, $y_{4}=-2, z_{4}=-2$
The fourth particle must be placed at the point $(-2,-2,-2)$.
subject: Physics

question: The mass per unit length of a non-uniform rod of length $L$ varies as $m=\lambda x$ where $\lambda$ is constant. The centre of mass of the rod will be at;
explanation:
Given:
$m=\lambda x$
differentiating both sides
$d m=\lambda x d x$
We know that:
$x_{c m}=\dfrac{\int_{0}^{L} x d m}{\int_{0}^{L} d m}=\dfrac{\int_{0}^{L} x \cdot x \lambda d x}{\int_{0}^{L} \lambda x d x}$
$\Rightarrow x_{c m}=\dfrac{\lambda \int_{0}^{L} x^{2} d x}{\lambda \int_{0}^{L} x d x}$
$\Rightarrow x_{c m}=\dfrac{\left[\dfrac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{L}}{\left[\dfrac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{L}}=\dfrac{L^{3}}{3} \times \dfrac{2}{L^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow x_{c m}=\dfrac{2}{3} L$
So, option (1) is correct.

question: A uniform thin $\operatorname{rod} \mathrm{AB}$ of length $\mathrm{L}$ has linear mass density $\mu(x)=a+\dfrac{\mathrm{b} x}{\mathrm{~L}}$, where $\mathrm{x}$ is measured from A. If the $\mathrm{CM}$ of the rod lies at a distance of $\left(\dfrac{7}{12}\right) \mathrm{L}$ from $\mathrm{A}$, then $a$ and $\mathrm{b}$ are related as:
explanation:
Centre of mass of the rod is given by
${x_{cm}} = \frac{{\int\limits_0^L {\left( {ax + \frac{{b{x^2}}}{L}} \right)dx} }}{{\int\limits_0^L {\left( {a + \frac{{bx}}{L}} \right)dx} }}$
$=\dfrac{\dfrac{a \mathrm{~L}^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{\mathrm{bL}^{2}}{3}}{a \mathrm{~L}+\dfrac{\mathrm{bL}}{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{L}\left(\dfrac{a}{2}+\dfrac{\mathrm{b}}{3}\right)}{a+\dfrac{\mathrm{b}}{2}}$
Now $\dfrac{7 \mathrm{~L}}{12}=\dfrac{\mathrm{L}\left[\dfrac{a}{2}+\dfrac{\mathrm{b}}{3}\right]}{a+\dfrac{\mathrm{b}}{2}}$
On solving we get, $\mathrm{b}=2 a$

question: A thin bar of length $L$ has a mass per unit length $\lambda$, that increases linearly with distance from one end. If its total mass is $\mathrm{M}$ and its mass per unit length at the lighter end is $\lambda_{0}$, then the distance of the centre of mass from the lighter end is :-
explanation:
Mass per unit length $ = {\lambda _0} + kx$
$M = \int\limits_0^L {\left( {{\lambda _0} + kx} \right)dx} $
$M = {\lambda _0}\,L + \frac{{K \times {L^2}}}{2}$
$\frac{{2\left( {M - {\lambda _0}\,L} \right)}}{{{L^2}}} = K$
$\frac{{2M}}{{{L^2}}} - \frac{{2{\lambda _0}}}{{\,L}} = K$
${r_{cm}} = \frac{{\int {dm} (r)}}{{\int {dm} }} = \frac{{\int {(\lambda dx)} x}}{M} = \frac{{\int_0^L {\left( {{\lambda _0}x + k{x^2}} \right)} dx}}{M}$
$ = \frac{1}{M}\left[ {\frac{{{\lambda _0}\,{L^2}}}{2} + \frac{{K{L^3}}}{3}} \right]$
substitute $'k'$
${r_{cm}} = \frac{{2\,L}}{3} - \frac{{{\lambda _0}\,{L^2}}}{{6M}}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: COM of Continuous Mass Distribution

question: In a vertical plane inside a smooth hollow thin tube a block of same mass as that of tube is released as shown in the figure. When it is slightly disturbed it moves towards right. By the time the block reaches the right end of the tube, displacement of the tube will be (where ' $R$ ' is mean radius of tube). Assume that the tube remains in vertical plane.
explanation:
Let $m$ is the mass of the block and the tube. As there is no external force along horizontal direction then the C.M should not move along horizontal direction.
${m_1}{{\vec s}_1} + {m_2}{{\vec s}_2} = 0$
${m_1} = {m_2} = m$
$ \Rightarrow {\vec s_1} + {\vec s_2} = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Given that
${\vec s_{12}} = R\hat i = {\vec s_1} - {\vec s_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From the above two equations we get
${\vec s_2} = \frac{{ - R}}{2}\hat i$

question: In a system of two particles of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ the first particle is moved by a distance $d$$ towards the centre of mass. To keep the centre of mass unchanged, the second particle will have to be moved by a distance
explanation:
Let $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$ be the position of masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ respectively.
The position of centre of mass is
$x_{C M}=\dfrac{x_{1} m_{1}+x_{2} m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$
If $\Delta x_{1}$ and $\Delta x_{2}$ be the changes in positions of $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ respectively, the change in the position of centre of mass,
$\Delta x_{C M}=\dfrac{\Delta x_{1} m_{1}+\Delta x_{2} m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}$
Given the centre of mass remains unchanged i.e. $\Delta x_{C M}=0$ and $\Delta x_{1}=d$ (towards centre of mass)
$\Rightarrow 0=\dfrac{d m_{1}+\Delta x_{2} m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \Rightarrow \Delta x_{2}=-\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}} d$
$\therefore$ The displacement of the second mass should also be towards the centre of mass as these two masses are located on two opposite sides of their common centre of mass and their displacements have opposite signs.

options.text: $\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} d$ towards the centre of mass
$\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}} d$, away from the centre of mass
$\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}} d$, towards the centre of mass
$\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} d$, away from the centre of mass
