question: Consider a two particle system with particles having masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$. If the first particle is pushed towards the centre of mass through a distance $d$, by what distance should the second particle be moved, so as to keep the centre of mass at the same position?
explanation:
To keep the centre of mass at the same position, velocity of centre of mass is zero, so
$\dfrac{m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}=0$
[where $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ are velocities of particles 1 and 2 respectively.]
$\Rightarrow m_{1} \dfrac{\Delta r_{1}}{d t}+m_{2} \dfrac{\Delta r_{2}}{d t}=0$
$\Rightarrow m_{1} \Delta r_{1}+m_{2} \Delta r_{2}=0$
Let 2nd particle has been displaced by distance $x$.
$\Rightarrow m_{1}(d)+m_{2}(x)=0$
$\Rightarrow x=-\dfrac{m_{1} d}{m_{2}}$

question: Two bodies of masses $1\,kg\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,3\,kg$ have position vectors $\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $-3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$, respectively. The centre of mass of this system has a position vector.

$\dfrac{1}{4}(-8 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$

question: $(n-1)$ equal point masses each of mass $m$ are placed at the vertices of a regular ${\rm{n}}$-polygon. The vacant vertex has a position vector $a$ with respect to the centre of the polygon. Find the positive vector of centre of mass.
explanation:
The given system can be asumed as a combination of $nm$ at the centre of the hexagon and $-m$ at the place where valancy is present.
$r_{C M}=\dfrac{(n m) 0+(-m) a}{n m-m}=\dfrac{-a}{(n-1)}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Center of Mass of a Group of Particles

question: In the $HCL$ molecule, the separation between the nuclei of the two atoms is about $1.27\mathop {{\rm{ }}A}\limits^o {\mkern 1mu} \,\,{\mkern 1mu} 1\mathop A\limits^o  = {10^{ - 10}}\,m$. Find the approximate location of the centre of mass of the molecule, given that a chlorine atom is about 35.5 times as massive as a hydrogen atom and nearly all the mass of an atom is concentrated in its nucles.
explanation:
Given, separation between the nuclei of
$H$ and $Cl$ is $1.27\mathop A\limits^o = 1.27 \times {10^{ - 10}}m$
Let mass of hydrogen atom $=m$
$\therefore$ Mass of chlorine atom $ = 35.5m$https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955828/preptracker/questions/q_34922_explanation_1764955828281.png Let mass of hydrogen atom be at origin, i.e., position vector of it, $r_{1}=0$
$\therefore$ Position vector of chlorine atom
${r_2} = 1.27 \times {10^{ - 10}} m$
${r_{CM}} = \frac{{{m_1}{r_1} + {m_2}{r_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}$
$ = \frac{{m \times 0 + 35.5\,m \times 1.27 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{m + 35.5\,m}}$
$ = \frac{{35.5 \times 1.27 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{{36.5}} = 1.24\mathop A\limits^o $
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Center of Mass of a Group of Particles

question: Three bricks each of length $L$ and mass $M$ are arranged as shown from the wall. The distance of the centre of mass of the system from the wall is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951391/preptracker/questions/q_34918_explanation_1764951391221.png From figure,
$\begin{gathered}x_{1}=\dfrac{L}{2}, x_{2}=\dfrac{L}{2}+\dfrac{L}{2}=L \\x_{3}=\dfrac{L}{2}+\dfrac{L}{4}+\dfrac{L}{2}=\dfrac{5 L}{4} \\\therefore x_{C M}=\dfrac{M_{1} x_{1}+M_{2} x_{2}+M_{3} x_{3}}{M_{1}+M_{2}+M_{3}} \\=\dfrac{M \times \dfrac{L}{2}+M \times L+M \times \dfrac{5 L}{4}}{M+M+M} \\=\dfrac{\dfrac{11}{4} M L}{3 M}=\dfrac{11 L}{12}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Center of Mass of Composite Bodies

question: A thin rod of length ' $L$ ' is lying along the $x$ - axis with its ends at $x=0$ and $x=L$. Its linear density (mass/ length) varies with $x$ as $k\left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)^{n}$, where \[n\] can be zero or any positive number. If the position $x_{C M}$ of the centre of mass of the rod is plotted against ' $n$ ', which of the following graphs best approximates the dependence of $x_{C M}$ on \[n\] ?
explanation:
The $x$-position of $CM$ of the rod is
${x_{CM}} = \frac{{\int\limits_0^L {xdm} }}{{\int\limits_0^L {dm} }} = \frac{{\int\limits_0^L {x\lambda dx} }}{{\int\limits_0^L {\lambda dx} }} = \frac{{\int\limits_0^L k {{\left( {\frac{x}{L}} \right)}^n}xdx}}{{\int\limits_0^L k {{\left( {\frac{x}{L}} \right)}^n}dx}}$
$ = \frac{{\left[ {\frac{{K{x^{n + 2}}}}{{(n + 2){L^n}}}} \right]_0^L}}{{\left[ {\frac{{k{x^{n + 1}}}}{{(n + 1){L^n}}}} \right]_0^L}} = \frac{{L(n + 1)}}{{n + 2}}$
For $n=0 x_{C M}=\dfrac{L}{2}$
$n=1$,
$x_{C M}=\dfrac{2 L}{3}$
$n=2, x_{C M}=\dfrac{3 L}{4}$
$x_{C M}=\dfrac{L\left(1+\dfrac{1}{n}\right)}{\left(1+\dfrac{2}{n}\right)}$
As $n \rightarrow \infty$
$x_{C M}=L$
The correct graph is (1)
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: COM of Continuous Mass Distribution

question: The inclined surfaces of two movable wedges of same mass $M$ are smoothly conjugated with the horizontal plane as shown in the figure. A washer of mass $m$ slides down the left wedge from a height $h$. To what maximum height will the washer rise along the right wedge? Neglect friction.
explanation:
For wedge (1)
From conservation of angular momentum.
$0+0=m V_{1}-M V_{2}$
Where ${V_1}{\rm{ }}\,{\rm{\& }}\,\,{V_2}$ are velocities of block and the wedge
${V_2} = \frac{{m{V_1}}}{M}$
$mgh = \frac{1}{2}MV_2^2 + \frac{1}{2}mV_1^2{\mkern 1mu} $
$V_1^2 = \frac{{2Mgh}}{{M + m}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For wedge (2)
$\begin{aligned}& m V_{1}=(m+M) V_{0} \\& \dfrac{1}{2} m V_{1}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}(m+M) V_{0}^{2}+m g x \\& \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}}{2}\left[\dfrac{M}{m+M}\right]=g x \\& \dfrac{g h M^{2}}{(m+M)^{2}}=g x \quad \text { (From equation 1) } \\& x=\dfrac{h M^{2}}{(m+M)^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Displacement of Centre of Mass

question: The velocity of the $CM$ of a system changes from $\overrightarrow {{v_1}}  = 4\hat i\,\,m/s$ to $\overrightarrow {{v_2}}  = 3\hat j\,\,m/s$ during time $\Delta t=2 s$. If the mass of the system is $m = 10\,kg$, the constant force acting on the system is:
explanation:
$\vec{F}=m \dfrac{\Delta \vec{v}_{c m}}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{10(3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{i})}{2}=5(3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{i})$
$|\vec{F}|=5 \sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}=25 N$
subject: Physics

explanation:

${v_{2cm}} = {v_2} - {V_{cm}} = 0 - 10 = 10 m/s$

question:
A man of mass $m$ stands at one end of a plank of length $\mathrm{L}$ which lies at rest on a frictionless surface. The man walks to the other end of the plank. If the mass of the plank is $m / 3$, the distance that the man moves relative to the
ground is
explanation:
External force acting on the system is zero. The displacements of man and boat are related as
${m_1}{{\vec s}_1} + {m_2}{{\vec s}_2} = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${m_1} = m,{m_2} = m/3$
${\vec S_1}\,\,\& \,\,{\vec S_2}$ are displacements w.r.to ground.
Given that $\overrightarrow {{s_{12}}} = \overrightarrow {{s_1}} - \overrightarrow {{s_2}} = L\hat i\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq's (1) & (2) $$\overrightarrow {{s_1}} = \frac{L}{4}\hat i$

question: Two particles of equal masses have velocity $\overrightarrow {{v_1}}  = 2\hat i\,m/s$ and $\overrightarrow {{v_1}}  = 2\hat i\,m/s$. The first particle has an acceleration $\overrightarrow {{a_1}}  = (3\hat i + 3\hat j)m/{s^2}$ while the acceleration of the other particle is zero. The centre of mass of the two particles moves in a:
explanation:
${\vec v_{cm}} = \frac{{m2\hat i + m2\hat j}}{{m + m}} = \hat i + \hat j$
${\vec a_{cm}} = \frac{{m(3\hat i + 3\hat j) + m \times 0}}{{m + m}} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j$
As both vectors make $45^{\circ}$ with $x$-axis so both move along a straight line.

explanation:

question: From the circular disc of radius $4R$ two small disc of radius $R$ are cut off. The centre of mass of the new structure will be:
explanation:
If mass of removed disc is $m$ then mass of big disc will be $16\,m$.
Here, $m_{1}=16 m, m_{2}=m_{3}=-m$
$x_{c m}=\dfrac{16 m(0)-m(0)-m(3 R)}{16 m-m-m}=-\dfrac{3}{14} R$
Because of symmetry we can say $y_{c m}$ will also have same magnitude
$\begin{aligned}& y_{c m}=-\dfrac{3}{14} R \\& \vec{r}_{c m}=-\dfrac{3}{14} R(\hat{i}+\hat{j})\end{aligned}$
So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: In the figure one fourth part of a uniform disc of radius $R$ is shown. The distance of the centre of mass of the object from centre $O'$ is
explanation:
The $CM$ has both $x$ and $y$ coordinates. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951280/preptracker/questions/q_34680_explanation_1764951279699.png By using papus theorem, rotate the material about $x$-axis.
Volume covered $=$ distance moved by $CM\,\, \times $ Area of the material
$\frac{2}{3}\pi {R^3} = 2\pi {y_{CM}} \times \frac{{\pi {R^2}}}{4}{y_{CM}} = \frac{{4R}}{{3\pi }}$
Similarly $x_{C M}=\dfrac{4 R}{3 \pi}$
$r_{C M}=\sqrt{x_{C M}^{2}+y_{C M}^{2}}=\dfrac{4 \sqrt{2} R}{3 \pi}$
subject: Physics

question: Particles of masses $m, 2 m, 3 m, \ldots . n m$ grams are placed on the same line at distance $l,\,2l,\,3l, \ldots  \ldots .nl\,cm$ from a fixed point. The distance of centre of mass of the particles from the fixed point in centimetres is
explanation:
$X_{C M}=\dfrac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}+\ldots \ldots}{m_{1}+m_{2}+\ldots \ldots}$
$=\dfrac{m l+2 m \cdot 2 l+3 m \cdot 3 l+\ldots}{m+2 m+3 m+\ldots}$
$ = \frac{{ml(1 + 4 + 9 + \ldots \ldots )}}{{m(1 + 2 + 3 + \ldots .)}} = $
$\frac{{\frac{{\ln (n + 1)(2n + 1)}}{6}}}{{\frac{{n(n + 1)}}{2}}} = \frac{{l(2n + 1)}}{3}.$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Center of Mass of a Group of Particles

question: A wedge $\mathrm{Q}$ of mass $M$ is placed on a horizontal frictionless surface $A B$ and a block $P$ of mass $m$ is released on its frictionless slope. As $P$ slides by a length $L$ on this slope of inclination $\theta, \mathrm{Q}$ would slide by a distance of
explanation:

$L \cos \theta-x$
Now apply
$\begin{aligned}& m_{1} x_{1}-m_{2} x_{2}=0 \Rightarrow M x=m(L \cos \theta-x) \\& \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{m L \cos \theta}{M+m}\end{aligned}$

question: A stationary pulley carries a rope one end of which supports a ladder with a man and the other a counter weight of mass $M$. The man of mass $\mathrm{m}$ climbs up a distance $l$ w.r.to, the ladder and then stops. The displacement of the centre of mass of this system is:
explanation:
Mass of $\operatorname{man}=m$
Mass of ladder $=\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{m}$
Let $x$ is distance travelled by the ladder and the weight $M$. The displacement of the man w.r.to the ground is $l-x$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955745/preptracker/questions/q_34537_explanation_1764955745395.png $\begin{aligned}& \Delta x_{C M}=\dfrac{M x-(M-m) x+m(l-x)}{2 M} \\& =\dfrac{m x+m l-m x}{2 M}=\dfrac{m l}{2 M}\end{aligned}$

question:
The figure shows a man of mass $m$ standing at the end $A$ of a trolley of mass $M$ placed at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. The man starts moving towards the end $B$ with a velocity $u_{r e l}$ with respect to the trolley. The length of the trolley is $L.$
When the man starts moving, then the velocity of the trolley with respect to ground will be
explanation:
From conservation of linear momentum
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{P}_{i}=\vec{P}_{f} \\& \vec{o}=m_{1} \vec{v}_{1}+m_{2} \vec{v}_{2} \\& m_{1}=m, m_{2}=M \\& \vec{v}_{12}=\vec{v}_{1}-\vec{v}_{2}=u_{r e l} \hat{i}\end{aligned}$
From the above equations we get
$\vec{v}_{2}=\dfrac{-m u_{r e l} \hat{i}}{(m+M)}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Velocity of Centre of Mass

question: Centre of mass of a particle of mass $6\,kg$ is at the point $(1 m, 2 m, 3 m)$ and center of mass of another mass $5\,kg$ is at point $( - 1\,m,\,\,3\,m,\, - 2\,m)$. Where a $5\,kg$ particle should be placed, so that center of mass of the system of all three particles shifts to centre of mass of the first particle?
explanation:
Let the coordinates of $5 kg\,\,({3^{rd}}particle)$ particle is at $(x, y, z)$. As the system C.M lies at $(1,2,3)$
$\begin{aligned}& 1=\dfrac{6(1)+5(-1)+5(x)}{6+5+5} \\\Rightarrow & x=3 \\& 2=\dfrac{6(2)+5(3)+5(y)}{6+5+5} \\\Rightarrow & y=1 \\3 & =\dfrac{6(3)+5(-2)+5(z)}{6+5+5} \\\Rightarrow & =8\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The centre of mass of a system of three particles of masses $1\,g$, $2\,g$, and $3\,g$ is taken as the origin of a coordinate system. The position vector of a fourth particle of mass $4\,g$ such that the centre of mass of the four particle system lies at the point $(1,2,3)$ is $\alpha(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$, where $\alpha$ is a constant. The value of $\alpha$ is
explanation:
The $(x, y, z)$ coordinate of masses $1 g$, $2 g, 3 g$ and $4 g$ are
$\begin{gathered}\left(x_{1}=0, y_{1}=0, z_{1}=0\right),\left(x_{2}=0, y_{2}=0, z_{2}=0\right) \\\left(x_{3}=0, y_{3}=0, z_{3}=0\right),\left(x_{4}=\alpha, y_{4}=2 \alpha, z_{4}=3 \alpha\right) \\\therefore X_{C M}=\dfrac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}+m_{3} x_{3}+m_{4} x_{4}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}+m_{4}} \\X_{C M}=\dfrac{1 \times 0+2 \times 0+3 \times 0+4 \times \alpha}{1+2+3+4} \\1=\dfrac{4 \alpha}{10} \text { or } \alpha=\dfrac{5}{2}\end{gathered}$
The value of $\alpha$ can also be calculated by $Y_{C M}$ and $Z_{C M}$ also
subject: Physics

question: Two particles of mass $1\,kg$ and $3\,kg$ have position vectors $2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$ and $-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$ respectively. The centre of mass has a position vector
explanation:

The position vector of the centre of mass is
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{r}_{1}=\dfrac{m_{1} \vec{r}_{1}+m_{2} \vec{r}_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}} \\& =\dfrac{(1)(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})+(3)(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})}{1+3} \\& =\dfrac{-4 \hat{i}+12 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}}{4}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}\end{aligned}$

question: Two masses ${m_1} = 1\,kg$ and ${m_2} = 2\,kg$ are connected by a light inextensible string and suspended by means of a weightless pulley as shown in the figure. Assuming that both the masses start from rest, the distance travelled by the centre of mass in two seconds is (Take $\left. {g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}} \right)$
explanation:
Here, $m_{1}=1 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_{2}=2 \mathrm{~kg}$
$\begin{aligned}& a_{C M}=\left(\dfrac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{m_{2}+m_{2}}\right)^{2} g=\dfrac{g}{9} \\& s_{C M}=\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{10}{9} \times 4=\dfrac{20}{9}\end{aligned}$

question: Two persons $60\,kg$ and $80\,kg$ are sitting at the ends of a boat of mass $60\,kg$ and length $4\,m$. The boat is stationary. If the men now exchange their positions, then:
explanation:
Let $m_{1}, m_{2}$ are masses of two persons. $m_{3}$ is the mass of the boat.
$m_{1} \vec{s}_{1}+m_{2} \vec{s}_{2}+m_{3} \vec{s}_{3}=0$
$\vec{s}_{3}$ is the displacement of boat
Where ${\vec s_1}{\text{ & }}{\vec S_2}$ are displacements of persons
Given that
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{s}_{13}=4 \hat{i}=\vec{s}_{1}-\vec{s}_{3} \\& \vec{s}_{23}=-4 \hat{i}=\vec{s}_{2}-\vec{s}_{3}\end{aligned}$
From the above equations we get
$\begin{aligned}& 60\left(4 \hat{i}+\vec{s}_{3}\right)+80\left(\vec{s}_{3}-4 \hat{i}\right)+60 \vec{s}_{3}=0 \\& 200 \vec{s}_{3}=80 \hat{i} \\& \vec{s}_{3}=0.4 \hat{i}\end{aligned}$

options.text: The centre of mass of the two men shifts by $2\,m$
The centre of mass of the two men shifts by $\frac{4}{7}m$

question: Three particles of masses $1\,kg$, $\frac{3}{2}\,kg$, and $2\,kg$ are located at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $a$. Here $1\,kg$ is placed at origin and $\dfrac{3}{2} k g$ on the $x$-axis. The $x, y$ coordinates of the centre of mass are
explanation:
Let the masses $1kg,\frac{3}{2}kg\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,2kg$ are located at the vertices $A,B{\text{ and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} C$ as shown in figure. The coordinates of points $A,B{\rm{ and}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} C$ are $(0,0),(a,0),\left( {\frac{a}{2},\frac{{\sqrt 3 a}}{2}} \right)$ respectively.
The coordinates of centre of mass are
$\begin{aligned}& X_{C M} \dfrac{m_{1} x_{1}+m_{2} x_{2}+m_{3} x_{3}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}} \\= & \dfrac{\left[1 \times 0+\dfrac{3}{2} \times a+2 \times \dfrac{a}{2}\right]}{\left[1+\dfrac{3}{2}+2\right]}=\dfrac{5 a}{9} \\& Y_{C M} \dfrac{m_{1} y_{1}+m_{2} y_{2}+m_{3} y_{3}}{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}} \\= & \dfrac{\left[1 \times 0+\dfrac{3}{2} \times 0+2 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3} a}{2}\right]}{\left[1+\dfrac{3}{2}+2\right]}=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{3} a}{9}=\dfrac{2 a}{3 \sqrt{3}}\end{aligned}$

question: A ball of radius $R=20 {~cm}$ has mass $m=0.75 {~kg}$ and moment of inertia (about its diameter) $I=0.0125 {~kg} {~m}^{2}$. The ball rolls without sliding over a rough horizontal floor with velocity ${v_0} = 10\,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ towards a smooth vertical wall.If coefficient of restitution between the wall and the ball is $e=0.7$, calculate velocity $v$ of the ball long after the collision (in ${m} / {s}$ ). (Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
As the ball is rolling on ground, the lowest point of the ball should be at rest for this, $v_{0}=\omega_{0} R$ hence the angular velocity $\left(\omega_{0}\right)$ of the ball just before collision with wall is,
${\omega _0} = \frac{{{v_0}}}{R} = \frac{{10}}{{0.2}} = 50\,\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$(in counterclockwise sense)
Now the ball collides with wall.
The velocity of centre of ball, just after collision should be
$u=e v_{0}=0.7 \times 10=7 {~ms}^{-1} \text { (Rightwards) }$
Since the wall is smooth, therefore no tangential force is applied by the wall. It means, angular velocity $\omega_{0}$ of ball remains unchanged during collision. Now surface of ball slides on floor to the right as shown in the figure. Hence the friction should act towards left https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954277/preptracker/questions/q_37092_explanation_1764954276518.png From free-body diagram of ball while sliding: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954277/preptracker/questions/q_37092_explanation_1764954277295.png For vertical forces,$N=m g$
Let coefficient of friction between ball and the floor be $\mu$.
For horizontal forces, $\mu N=m a \Rightarrow a=\mu g$
Applying torque equation about centre of ball (about $O$ ) of force acting on the ball,
$\mu NR = I\alpha $
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{\mu (mg)R}}{I}$
$ = \frac{{\mu \times (0.75 \times 10) \times 0.20}}{{0.0125}}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=120 \mu$ (in clockwise sense)
Long after the collision, there will be no sliding or it will be pure rolling. Let sliding stop after a time $t$ after collision, then final translational velocity,
$v=(7-a t)$ or $v=7-10 \mu t$
and final angular velocity, $\omega=\left(-\omega_{0}\right)+\alpha t$
$\omega = (120\mu t - 50)rad\,\,{s^{ - 1}}$ (clockwise)
But at that instant, $v_{c m}=\omega R \Rightarrow(7-10 \mu t)=0.2(120 \mu t-50)$
$\mu t=0.5 \Rightarrow v=2 {~ms}^{-1}$

explanation:

we get $m v R=\left(m R^{2}+m R^{2}\right) \omega ; \omega=\dfrac{v}{2 R}$

question: A mass $m$ is moving at speed $v$ perpendicular to a rod of length $d$ and mass $M = 6\,m$ which pivots around a frictionless axle running through its centre. It strikes to the end of the rod. The moment of inertia of the rod about its centre is $\dfrac{M d^{2}}{12}$. Then the angular speed of the system just after the collision is
explanation:
Let $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the system after collision.
By conservation of angular momentum about pivot
$\begin{aligned}& L=I \omega \\& \dfrac{m v d}{2}=\left[\dfrac{M d^{2}}{12}+m\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^{2}\right] \omega \\& =\left(\dfrac{m d^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{m d^{2}}{4}\right) \omega \\& (M=6 m) \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{2}{3} \dfrac{v}{d}=\omega\end{aligned}$

question: A block of mass $m$ is attached to a pulley disc of equal mass $m$ and radius $r$ by means of a slack string as shown in figure. The pulley is hinged about its centre on a horizontal table and the block is projected with a initial velocity of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The velocity of the block when the string becomes taut will be
explanation:
Apply conservation of angular momentum about the hinge we get
$\begin{aligned}& m v R=\left(\dfrac{m}{2} R^{2}\right) \omega+m(R \omega) R \\& R \omega=\dfrac{2 v}{3}=\dfrac{2 \times 5}{3}=\dfrac{10}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} .\end{aligned}$

question: An object of mass $3\,m$ splits into three equal fragments. Two fragments have velocities $\hat{v j}$ and $\hat{v i}$. The velocity of the third fragment is
explanation:
Initial momentum of $3\,m$ mass $=0$ $\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Due to explosion this mass splits into three fragments of equal masses .
Final momentum of system
$ = m\vec V + mv\hat i + mv\hat j\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From law of conservation of linear momentum
$m V+m v \hat{i}+m v \hat{j}=0$
$\Rightarrow V=-v(\hat{i}+\hat{j})$

question: A bomb at rest explodes into 3 parts of same mass. The momentum of two parts is $-3 p \hat{i}$ and $2 p \hat{j}$ respectively. The magnitude of momentum of the third part is
explanation:
If the momentum of the third particle is $\overrightarrow{p_{3}}$, then using conservation of linera momentum,
$0=-3 p \hat{i}+2 p \hat{j}+\vec{p}_{3} \Rightarrow \vec{p}_{3}=3 p \hat{i}-2 p \hat{j}$
Hence magnitude of momentum of the third particle,
$\left|\overrightarrow{p_{3}}\right|=\sqrt{(3 p)^{2}+(2 p)^{2}}=\sqrt{13} p$

question: A shell of mass $m$ which is at rest initially. It explodes into three fragments having masses in the ratio $2: 2: 1$. If the fragments having equal masses fly off along mutually perpendicualr directions with speed $v$, the speed of the third (lighter) fragment is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951384/preptracker/questions/q_35104_explanation_1764951383998.png https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951385/preptracker/questions/q_35104_explanation_1764951384704.png Apply $x-y$ axis convention using unit vectors $\hat{i}, \hat{j}$
By conservation of linear momentum
$\begin{aligned}& m(0)=\dfrac{2 m}{5}(-v \hat{i})+\dfrac{2 m}{5}(-v \hat{j})+\dfrac{m}{5} \vec{v}^{\prime} \\& \Rightarrow \vec{v}^{\prime}=2 v \hat{i}+2 v \hat{j} \\& \Rightarrow v^{\prime}=\sqrt{(2 v)^{2}+(2 v)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{2} v\end{aligned}$

question: A stationary body of mass $m$ gets exploded in 3 parts having mass in the ratio of $1: 3: 3$. Its two fractions having equal mass moving at right angle to each other with velocity of $15\,m/\sec $. Then the velocity of the third body is :-
explanation:
Let ${m_1},\,\,{m_2}\,\,\& \,\,{m_3}$ are the masses
Given that $m_{1}: m_{2}: m_{3}=1: 3: 3$
${m_1} = k:{m_2} = 3k{\mkern 1mu} \,\,\& \,\,{m_3} = 3k$ (where $k$ is constant)
$\begin{aligned}& m=m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}=7 k \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{m}{7} \\& m_{1}=\dfrac{m}{7}, m_{2}=\dfrac{3 m}{7} m_{3}=\dfrac{3 m}{7}\end{aligned}$
The resultant momentum of ${m_2}\,\,\& \,\,{\mkern 1mu} {m_3}$ is
$\left(\dfrac{3 m}{7}\right)(15) \sqrt{2}$
From the conservation of linear momentum
$\begin{gathered}\left(\dfrac{m}{7}\right) v=\left(\dfrac{3 m}{7}\right)(15) \sqrt{2} \\v=45 \sqrt{2} m / s\end{gathered}$

question: ${ }_{84}^{210} X$ originally at rest emits $\alpha$-particles of kinetic energy K. Find the $K E$ of recoiling nucleus.

$K E$ of recoiling nucleus $=\dfrac{1}{2}(206)\left(v^{\prime}\right)^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2}(206)\left(\dfrac{4 v}{206}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow K^{\prime}=\dfrac{4}{206} K$

question: A stationary body explodes into four identical fragments such that three of them fly off mutually perpendicular to each other, each with same $K.E$. The minimum energy of explosion will be:
explanation:
Let $E_{0}=K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}$
Where $v_{0}$ is the velocity of one of three fragments
From conservation of $LM$
$\begin{aligned}& 0=m \vec{v}+m\left[v_{0} \hat{i}+v_{0} \hat{j}+v_{0} \hat{k}\right] \\& v=\sqrt{3} v_{0}\end{aligned}$
The energy of explosion is
$\begin{aligned}K E & =\dfrac{1}{2} m\left(\sqrt{3} v_{0}\right)^{2}+3\left(\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}\right) \\E & =6 \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=6 E_{0}\end{aligned}$

question: A stationary particle explodes into two particles of a masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ which move in opposite directions with velocities $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$. The ratio of their kinetic energies $E_{1} / E_{2}$ is
explanation: $\begin{gathered}E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m} \quad(\text { Linear momentum is conserved }) \\\Rightarrow E \propto \dfrac{1}{m} \Rightarrow \dfrac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} .\end{gathered}$

question: A body of mass $M$ splits into two parts $\alpha M$ and $(1-\alpha) M$ by an internal explosion, which generates kinetic energy $T$. After explosion if the two parts move in the same direction as before, their relative speed will be
explanation:
Let the speed of the body before explosion be $u$. After explosion, if the two parts move with velocities $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ in the same direction, then according to conservation of momentum,
$\alpha M v_{1}+(1-\alpha) M v_{2}=M u$
The kinetic energy $T$ liberated during explosion is given by
$\begin{aligned}& T=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha M v_{1}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}(1-\alpha) M v_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2} M u^{2} \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha M v_{1}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}(1-\alpha) M v_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{1}{2 M}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{gathered}{\left[\alpha M v_{1}+(1-\alpha) M v_{2}\right]^{2}} \\=\dfrac{1}{2} M \alpha(1-\alpha)\left[v_{1}^{2}+v_{2}^{2}-2 u_{1} u_{2}\right] \\\left(v_{1}-v_{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{2 T}{\alpha(1-\alpha) M} \\\Rightarrow \quad\left(v_{1}-v_{2}\right)=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 T}{\alpha(1-\alpha) M}}\end{gathered}$

question: A shell is fired from a cannon with velocity $v \mathrm{~m} /$ sec at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal direction. At the highest point in its path it explodes into two pieces of equal mass. One of the pieces retrace its path to the cannon and the speed in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{sec}$ of the other piece immediately after the explosion is
explanation:
Shell is fired with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
Velocity at the highest point $=v \cos \theta$
Momentum of shell before explosion $=m v \cos \theta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955747/preptracker/questions/q_34546_explanation_1764955746959.png When it breakes into two equal pieces one piece retraces its path to the canon, then other part moves with velocity $V$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955755/preptracker/questions/q_34546_explanation_1764955747636.png So momentum of two pieces after explosion
$=\dfrac{m}{2}(-v \cos \theta)+\dfrac{m}{2} V$
By the law of conservation of momentum
$m v \cos \theta=\dfrac{-m}{2} v \cos \theta+\dfrac{m}{2} V \Rightarrow V=3 v \cos \theta .$

question: A body just dropped from a tower explodes into two pieces of equal mass in mid-air. Which of the following is not possible?
explanation: If the velocities acquired due to explosions are in vertical direction, then options (4) and (1) are possible. If one of them follows parabolic path (or acquires velocity in horizontal direction), then other also has to follow parabolic path (or acquire velocity in horizontal direction to keep the momentum zero in horizontal direction). Hence, option (3) can never be possible.

question: A bomb of mass $9\,kg$ explodes into 2 pieces of mass $3\,kg$ and $6\,kg$. The velocity of mass $3\,kg$ is $1.6\,\,m/s,$ the $K.E$ of mass $6\,kg$ is
explanation:
${v_1}{\text{ & }}{v_2}$ are velocities of two parts after explosion
As the bomb initially was at rest therefore
Initial momentum of bomb $=0$
Final momentum of system $=m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}$
As there is no external force
$\therefore m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}=0 \Rightarrow 3 \times 1.6+6 \times v_{2}=0$
velocity of $6\,kg\,mass{\mkern 1mu} \,\,{v_2} = 0.8{\mkern 1mu} m/s$
Its kinetic energy
$ = \frac{1}{2}{m_2}v_2^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times {(0.8)^2} = 1.92J$

question: A disc of radius ${r=0.1 {~m}}$ is rolled from a point ${A}$ on a track as shown in the figure. The part ${A B}$ of the track is a semi-circle of radius ${R}$ in a vertical plane. The disc rolls without sliding and leaves contact with the track at its highest point ${B}$. Flying through the air it strikes the ground at point ${C}$. The velocity of the centre of mass of the disc makes an angle of ${30^{\circ}}$ below the horizontal at the time of striking the ground. At the same instant, velocity of the topmost point ${P}$ of the disc is found to be ${6 {~m} / {s}}$. The value of ${R}$ (in $m$) (Take ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$) is
explanation:
Since the disc was rolling, the velocity of its top point at the instant of leaving the track was zero. It means ${v_{H}=\omega r}$. When the disc is in air ${v_{H}}$ and ${\omega}$ both do not change. Hence the horizontal component of the velocity of the top point ${P}$ of the disc at every instant is zero and the vertical component of the velocity of the point ${P}$ is equal to the vertical component of velocity of the ${C M}$ of disc.
${\Rightarrow \sqrt{2 g h}=\sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot 2(R-0.1)}=6}$
${\Rightarrow \quad 4 \times 10(R-0.1)=36}$
$R=1 {~m}$

question: A body of mass $10\,kg$ moves with a velocity of  $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$  along a circular path of radius  $8\,m.$  The power produced by the body will be
explanation:
Power is defined as the rate of change of energy in a system or the time rate of doing work.
$P=\dfrac{d E}{d t}=\dfrac{d W}{d t}$
Also, work $=$ force $\times$ displacement$=F \times d$
In a circular motion, displacement is zero, therefore
$P=\dfrac{d}{d t}(F \times d)=\dfrac{d}{d t}(0)=0$

question: Ratio of radius of gyration of a hollow sphere to that of a solid cylinder of equal mass, for moment of inertia about their diameter axis $A B$ as shown in figure is $\sqrt{\dfrac{8}{x}}$. The value of $x$ is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of hollow sphere,
$I_{S}=\dfrac{2}{3} M R^{2}$
Radius of gyration of hollow sphere,
$\dfrac{1}{2} m K_{S}^{2}=\dfrac{2}{3} M R^{2}$
$K_{s}=\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{S}}{M}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} R$
For solid cylinder:
Applying theorem of parallel axes,
$I_{C}=I_{c m}+M d^{2}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954482/preptracker/questions/q_37160_explanation_1764954481943.png
or, $\quad I_{C}=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}+\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}+M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}$
or, $I_{C}=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}+\dfrac{M L^{2}}{3}$
or, $I_{C}=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{4}+\dfrac{M}{3}(4 R)^{2}$
or, $I_{C}=\dfrac{67}{12} M R^{2}$
$\therefore \quad$ Radius of gyration,
$\dfrac{1}{2} m K_{C}^{2}=\dfrac{67}{12} M R^{2}$
$K_{C}=\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{C}}{M}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{67}{12}} R$
Now, $\dfrac{K_{s}}{K_{C}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{12}{67}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{8}{67}} \quad \therefore \quad x=67$
So, option (4) is correct.

question: Three identical spherical shells, each of mass $2\,kg$ and radius $10\,cm$ are placed as shown in figure.Consider an axis $XX'$ which is touching to two shells and passing through diameter of third shell. Moment of inertia of the system consisting of these three spherical shells about $XX'$ axis is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951701/preptracker/questions/q_36711_explanation_1764951700722.png Using parallel axes theorem,
${I_1} = \frac{2}{3}m{r^2} + m{r^2}$ and ${I_1} = {I_2}$
${I_3} = \frac{2}{3}m{r^2}$
$\therefore M.I$ of system about $XX'$ is
$I = {I_1} + {I_2} + {I_3}$
$ = 2\left( {\frac{2}{3}m{r^2} + m{r^2}} \right) + \frac{2}{3}m{r^2}$
$ = 4m{r^2} = 4 \times 2 \times {\left( {10 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)^2}$
$ = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$\therefore I = 0.08\,kg\,{m^2}$

question:
A thin rod of mass ${m}$ and length ${l}$ is bent into a
${V}$-shaped frame at its mid point as shown. The moment of inertia of the system about an axis passing through $O$ perpendicular to the plane of the frame is equal to
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the rod about an end perpendicular to the plane of the paper is$I=\dfrac{1}{3} m l^{2}$
Now, the rod has two parts each of mass ${\dfrac{m}{2}}$ and length ${\dfrac{l}{2}}$.
Moment of inertia of the rod about an axis passing though ${O}$ perpendicular to the plane of paper will be
$I=\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{m}{2}\right)\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{24} m l^{2}$
Moment of inertia at ${O}$ for both the rods will be$2 I=2 \times\left(\dfrac{1}{24} m l^{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{12} m l^{2}$.
So correct option is (1)

question: Five particles of each mass $2\,kg$ are attached to the rim of a circular disc of radius $0.1\,\,m$ and negligible mass. Moment of inertia of the system about the axis passing through the centre of the disc and perpendicular to its place is

question: If the moment of inertia of a disc about an axis tangential and parallel to its surface be $I$, then what will be the moment of inertia about the axis tangential but perpendicular to the surface?

or $\quad M R^{2}=(4 / 5) I$

$\begin{aligned}I^{\prime} & =\dfrac{3}{2} M R^{2} \\\Rightarrow \quad I^{\prime} & =\dfrac{3}{2}\left(\dfrac{4}{5} I\right)=\dfrac{6}{5} I\end{aligned}$

question: The moment of inertia of two equal masses each of mass $m$ at separation $L$ connected by a rod of mass $M$, about an axis passing through centre and perpendicular to length of rod is
explanation: Moment of inertia of a rod about $X Y$, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951691/preptracker/questions/q_36544_explanation_1764951691135.png $I_{1}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}$    and   $\quad I_{2}=m\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}+m\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{m L^{2}}{2}$$\therefore \quad I=I_{1}+I_{2}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{m L^{2}}{2}$$=\dfrac{(M+6 m) L^{2}}{12}$

question: $A B C$ is right angled triangular plane of uniform thickness. The sides are such that $A B>B C$ as shown in figure. $I_{1}, I_{2}, I_{3}$ are moments of inertia about $A B, B C$ and $A C$, respectively. Then, which of the following relations is correct?
explanation:
The moment of inertia of a body about an axis depends not only on the mass of the body but also on the distribution of mass about the axis. For a given body, mass is same, so it will depend only on the distribution of mass about the axis. The mass is farthest from axis $B C$ so $I_{2}$ is maximum, mass is nearest to axis $A C$ so $I_{3}$ is minimum.
Hence, the correct sequence will be $I_{2}>I_{1}>I_{3}$.

question: Find ratio of radius of gyration of a disc and ring of same radii at their tangential axis in plane.
explanation:
For disc moment of inertia about the diameter, $I_{d}=m r^{2} / 4$ and moment of intertia about the tangential axis,
${I_{disc{\text{ }}}} = {I_d} + m{r^2}$
${I_{disc{\text{ }}}} = \frac{{m{r^2}}}{4} + m{r^2} = \frac{{5\,m{r^2}}}{4}$
Let the radius of gyration of disc is $k_{\text {disc }}$.$I_{\text {disc }}=m k_{\text {disc }}^{2}$
$\dfrac{5 m r^{2}}{4}=m k_{\text {disc }}^{2}$
$k_{\text {disc }}^{2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{4}} r$
For ring, moment of inertia about the diameter,
$I_{a}=\dfrac{m r^{2}}{2}$
and moment of inertia about the tangential axis
$I_{\text {ring }}=I_{a}+m r^{2}=\dfrac{m r^{2}}{2}+m r^{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} m r^{2}$
Let the radius of gyration of ring is $k_{\text {ring }}$.
$\begin{aligned}& I_{\text {ring }}=m k_{\text {ring }}^{2} \\& \dfrac{3}{2} m r^{2}=m k_{\text {ring }}^{2} \\& k_{\text {ring }}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}} r \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{k_{\text {disc }}}{k_{\text {ring }}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{4} r}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2} r}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{6}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Two discs having mass ratio $1: 2$ and diameter ratio $2: 1$, then find the ratio of moment of inertia.
explanation:
Given, mass ratio of two discs,
${m_1}:{m_2} = 1:2,{\text{ }}i.e.{\text{ }}\frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{1}{2}$
and diameter ratio $\dfrac{d_{1}}{d_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{1} \Rightarrow \dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{1}$
$\therefore$ Ratio of their moment of inertia,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{m_{1} r_{1}^{2}}{2}}{\dfrac{m_{2} r_{2}^{2}}{2}}=\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}} \cdot\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{2}{1}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{2}{1} \\& \therefore \quad I_{1}: I_{2}=2: 1\end{aligned}$

question:
Assertion: Moment of inertia of circular ring about a given axis is more than moment of inertia of the circular disc of same mass and same size. about the same axis.

Reason: The circular ring is hollow so its moment of inertia is more than circular dise which is solid.
explanation:
The moment of inertia depends on the distribution of mass around the axis of rotation. In the case of a ring, the mass is distributed longer distance from the axis compared to a solid disc of the same size and mass, resulting in a higher moment of inertia.
So correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

question:
Assertion: Inertia and moment of inertia are same quantities.

Reason: Inertia represents the capacity of a body to oppose change in state of its configuration (distribution of mass).
explanation: Inertia refers to an object's resistance to changes in its state of motion or rest, while moment of inertia relates to distribution of mass within body with respect to an axis of rotation. So correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Moment of inertia of a body is same, whatever be the axis of rotation.

Reason: Moment of inertia depends on mass of the body also.
explanation: The moment of inertia of a body depends on the axis of rotation. It is the product of the mass of body $(M)$ and square of radius of gyration $(\mathrm{K}) . I=M K^{2}$. The value of $K$ depends on axis of rotation. So correct option is (4).

question: A hoop of mass $500\,gm$ & radius $10\,cm$ is placed on a nail. Then the moment of inertia of the hoop, when it is rotated about the nail will be ____ $kg{m^2}$.
explanation:
From parallel axes theorem
$I = m{r^2} + m{r^2} = 2m{r^2}$
$ = 2 \times 0.5 \times {(0.1)^2} = 0.01\,kg{m^2}$

question: The moment of inertia of a square lamina about the perpendicular axis through its centre of mass is $20\,kg{\rm{ - }}{m^2}$. Then, its moment of inertia about an axis touching its side and in the plane of the lamina will be:
explanation:
$I=20 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}{ }^{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951633/preptracker/questions/q_36083_explanation_1764951632549.png , $I=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{6}=20 \Rightarrow M L^{2}=120$ $I^{\prime}=I_{C}+M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{M L^{2}}{4}$
$ \Rightarrow {I^\prime } = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{2} = \frac{{120}}{3} = 40\,kg\,{m^2}$

question: Three identical thin rods each of length $l$ and mass $M$ are joined together to form a letter $H$. The moment of inertia of the system about one side of $H$.
explanation:
We are taking side $A$.
Moment of inertia of system $I=I_{1}+I_{2}+I_{3}$
$I=0+\dfrac{M L^{2}}{3}+M L^{2}=\dfrac{4}{3} M L^{2}$
( $I_{c}=M L^{2}$, according to parallel axis theorem)

question: Two particles each mass $m$, and other particle of mass $2 m$ are situated at the verticles of an equilateral triangle $A B C$ of side $L$. What is the moment of inertia of the system about the line $A X$ perpendicular to $A B$ in the plane of $A B C$ ?
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the system
$I=I_{A}+I_{B}+I_{C}=0+m L^{2}+2 m \times\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}$
$=m L^{2}+\dfrac{2 m L^{2}}{4} \Rightarrow m L^{2}+\dfrac{m L^{2}}{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} m L^{2}$

question: What is the moment of inertia of earth about its diameter, taking it to be a sphere of radius $6400\,km$ and mass $6 \times {10^{24}}\,kg$?
explanation:
Moment of inertia of sphere $=\dfrac{2}{5} M R^{2}$
$I=\dfrac{2}{5} \times 6 \times 10^{24}\left(64+10^{5}\right)^{2}$
$=4096 \times 10^{10} \times 6 \times 10^{24} \times \dfrac{2}{5}$
$I = 9830.4 \times {10^{34}} = 9.83 \times {10^{37}}\,kg\,{m^2}$

Total amount of inertia of system https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951630/preptracker/questions/q_36067_explanation_1764951629881.png $I=I_{A}+I_{B}=0+\dfrac{m L^{2}}{12}$
$I=\dfrac{m L^{2}}{12}$

question: Five solid spheres each of mass ' $m$ ' and radius ' $r$ ' are rotating about an axis $A A$ ' as shown in figure. Hence the moment of inertia of the system about axis of rotation $A A^{\prime}$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951587/preptracker/questions/q_35986_explanation_1764951586957.png Moment of inertia (MI) of solid sphere about its axis $=\dfrac{2}{5} m r^{2}$
where ' $m$ ' is mass of solid sphere and ' $r$ ' is radius of solid sphere.
So, M.I of system about $A A^{\prime}$ is
$I=3\left(\dfrac{2}{5} m r^{2}\right)+2\left(\dfrac{2}{5} m r^{2}+m r^{2}\right)$
(By parallel axes theorem)
$\Rightarrow I=\dfrac{6}{5} m r^{2}+\dfrac{14}{5} m r^{2}=\dfrac{20}{5} m r^{2}$
$\Rightarrow I=4 m r^{2}$
So, option (2) is correct.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: The moment of inertia of a circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about an axis passing through the centre of mass is $I_{0}$. The moment of inertia of another circular disc of same mass and thickness but half the density about the same axis is
explanation:
For same mass $M_{1}=M_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \rho \pi R_{1}^{2} t=\dfrac{\rho}{2} \pi R_{2}^{2} t$ (where $\rho$ is for density)
$\Rightarrow R_{1}^{2}=\dfrac{R_{2}^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow R_{2}=\sqrt{2} R_{1}$
Moment of inertia of first disc
$I_{1}=I_{0}=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$
Moment of inertia of second disc
$I_{2}=\dfrac{M R_{2}^{2}}{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow I_{2}=\dfrac{M(\sqrt{2} R)^{2}}{2} \\& \Rightarrow I_{2}=2\left(\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}\right) \\& \Rightarrow I_{2}=2 I_{0}\end{aligned}$
So, option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question:
Assertion: Two circular discs of equal mass and thickness made of different materials, will have same moment of inertia about their central axes of rotation.

Reason: Moment of inertia depends upon the distribution of mass in the body
explanation:
The moment of inertia is given by $I=\sum m r^{2}$.
The moment of inertia depends on the mass and distribution of mass w.r.t the axis of rotation in the body, so that moment of inertia of two circular discs having equal mass and thickness made of different material will be same.

question: For the given uniform square lamina $A B C D$, whose centre is $O$.
explanation:
Let the each side of square lamina is $d$.
So, $I_{E F}=I_{G H}$ (due to symmetry)
and $I_{A C}=I_{B D}$ (due to symmetry) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956908/preptracker/questions/q_35946_explanation_1764956907838.png Now, according to the perpendicular axes theorem,
$I_{A C}+I_{B D}=I_{0}$
or $2{I_{AC}} = {I_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
and $I_{E F}+I_{G H}=I_{0}$
or $2{I_{EF}} = {I_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From Eqs. (1) & (2), we get $I_{A C}=I_{E F}$
From parallel axis theorem,
$\therefore I_{A D}=I_{E F}+\dfrac{m d^{2}}{4}$
$=\dfrac{m d^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{m d^{2}}{4}\left(a s I_{E F}=\dfrac{m d^{2}}{12}\right)$
So, $I_{A D}=\dfrac{m d^{2}}{3}=4 I_{E F}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question: Seven identical discs are arranged in a planar pattern, so as to touch each other as shown in the figure. Each disc has mass ' $m$ ' and radius $R$. What is the moment of inertia of system of six discs about an axis passing through the centre of central disc and normal to plane of all discs?
explanation:
Distance between the centre of two discs $=2 R$.
Six discs surround the central disc.
${I_c} = \frac{{m{R^2}}}{2} + 6\left[ {\frac{{m{R^2}}}{2} + m{{(2R)}^2}} \right]$
$ = \frac{7}{2}m{R^2} + 24m{R^2}$
$ = 55\left( {\frac{{m{R^2}}}{2}} \right)$
Correct option is (1).

question: The moment of inertia of a rigid body about an axis
explanation:
Moment of inertia of a body depends on position and orientation of axis of rotation. It also depends on shape, size of the body and also on the distribution of mass.
Correct option is (1).

question: Four small balls are placed at the corner of a square of length $\ell$. Evaluate MOI of system about a line passing through $A$ and parallel to BD.
explanation:
$\mathrm{I}=2 \mathrm{~m}\left(\dfrac{\ell}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\mathrm{m}(\sqrt{2} \ell)^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{2 \mathrm{~m} \ell^{2}}{2}+2 \mathrm{~m} \ell^{2} \\& =3 \mathrm{~m} \ell^{2}\end{aligned}$

question: The radius of gyration of a uniform rod of length $\ell$, about an axis passing through a point $\dfrac{\ell}{4}$ away from the centre of the rod, and perpendicular to it, is
explanation:
$I=\dfrac{M \ell^{2}}{12}+M\left(\dfrac{\ell}{4}\right)^{2}$
$I=\dfrac{7}{48} M \ell^{2}$
$M k^{2}=\dfrac{7}{48} M \ell^{2}$
$k=\ell \sqrt{\dfrac{7}{48}}$

question: Moment of inertia of an equilateral triangular lamina $A B C$, about the axis passing through its centre $\mathrm{O}$ and perpendicular to its plane is $I_{0}$ as shown in the figure. A cavity DEF is cut out from the lamina, where D, E, F are the mid points of the sides. Moment of inertia of the remaining part of lamina about the same axis is
explanation:
From the concept of dimensions
$I_{0}=K m l^{2}(B C=l)$
where $\mathrm{K}$ is a constant
$\begin{aligned}& I_{D E F}=K \dfrac{m}{4}\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{K}{16} m l^{2} \\& I_{D E F}=\dfrac{I_{0}}{16} \\& I_{\text {remain }}=I_{0}-\dfrac{I_{0}}{16}=\dfrac{15 I_{0}}{16}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Bodies having Cavities

question: Consider a thin uniform square sheet made of a rigid material. If its side is ' $a$ ' mass $\mathrm{m}$ and moment of inertia $I$ about one of its diagonals, then:
explanation:
Using perpendicular axis theorem.
$\begin{aligned}& I_{0}=I_{x}+I_{y} \\& I_{0}=2 I\left(I_{x}=I_{y}=I\right)\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955918/preptracker/questions/q_35661_explanation_1764955918148.png $\Rightarrow I=\dfrac{I_{0}}{2}=\dfrac{m a^{2}}{12}$
subject: Physics

question: A square plate has a moment of inertia $I_{o}$ about an axis lying in its plane, passing through its centre and making an angle $\theta$ with one of the sides. Which graph represents the variation of I with $\theta$ ?
explanation:
The moment of inertia about the axis shown is independent of the angle $\theta$. The three axes are present in the plane of the square. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956890/preptracker/questions/q_35624_explanation_1764956889738.png $I_{x}=I_{y}=I_{o}=\dfrac{m l^{2}}{12}$
$I_{o}=$ constant

question: Moment of inertia I of a solid sphere about an axis parallel to a diameter and at a distance $\mathrm{x}$ from it varies as:
explanation:
$I=I_{c}+m x^{2}$
$(\mathrm{m}=$ mass of the sphere)
$I=I_{c}$ at $x=0$
Therefore, I versus $\mathrm{x}$ graph is a parabola with minimum value of $I=I_{c}$ at $x=0$.
Therefore, the correct graph is (1).

question: Consider a rod of length $l$ and mass $\mathrm{m}$. The radius of the rod is negligible. The moment of inertia of the rod about the axis as a function of $\theta$ is given by the graph.
explanation: $I=\dfrac{m l^{2}}{12} \sin ^{2} \theta$

question: Moment of inertia of a sphere of mass $M$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is $\mathrm{I}$. Keeping M constant, if a graph is plotted between I and R, then its form would be: (1)
explanation:
$I=\dfrac{2}{5} M R^{2} \quad \therefore I \propto R^{2}$
This relation shows that graph between I and R will be a parabola.

question: Seven identical circular planar disks, each of mass $\mathrm{M}$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ are welded symmetrically as shown. The moment of inertia of the arrangement about the axis normal to the plane and passing through the point $\mathrm{P}$ is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955903/preptracker/questions/q_35494_explanation_1764955902720.png $I_{o}=I_{c m}+m d^{2}$
$=\dfrac{7 M R^{2}}{2}+6\left(M \times(2 R)^{2}\right)=\dfrac{55 M R^{2}}{2}$
$I_{P}=I_{o}+m d^{2}$
$=\dfrac{55 M R^{2}}{2}+7 M\left(3 R^{2}\right)=\dfrac{181}{2} M R^{2}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Let $I$ be the moment of inertia of a uniform square plate about an axis $\mathrm{AB}$ that passes through its centre and is parallel to two of its sides. CD is a line in the plane of the plate and it passes through the centre of the plate making an angle $\theta$ with $\mathrm{AB}$. The moment of inertia of the plate about the axis $\mathrm{CD}$ is equal to:
explanation:
As shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951433/preptracker/questions/q_35491_explanation_1764951433051.png $I_{C D}=I_{C^{\prime} D^{\prime}}$
So, $2 I_{C D}=I \Rightarrow I_{C D}=\dfrac{I}{2}$
Also, $2 I_{A B}=I=I_{A B}=\dfrac{I}{2}$
So $\quad I_{C D}=I_{A B}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: From a solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ a cube of maximum possible volume is cut. Moment of inertia of cube about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to one of its faces is :
explanation:
For maximum possible volume of cube $2 R=\sqrt{3} a, a$ is side of the cube. Moment of inertia about the required axis $I=\dfrac{m a^{2}}{6}=\rho a^{3} \dfrac{a^{2}}{6}$,
where $\rho=\dfrac{M}{\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}}$
$I = \frac{{3M}}{{4\pi {R^3}}}\frac{1}{6}{\left( {\frac{{2R}}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)^5}$
$ = \frac{{3M}}{{4\pi {R^3}}}\frac{1}{6}\frac{{32{R^5}}}{{9\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{{4M{R^2}}}{{9\sqrt 3 \pi }}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Bodies having Cavities

question: Moment of inertia of uniform triangular plate about axis passing through sides $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{AC}$, and $\mathrm{BC}$ are $I_{P}, I_{B}$ and $I_{H}$ respectively and about an axis perpendicular to the plane and passing through point $\mathrm{C}$ is $I_{C}$. Then:
explanation:
Moment of inertia is more when mass is farther from the axis. In case of axis BC, mass distribution is closest to it and in case of axis $\mathrm{AB}$ mass distribution is farthest. For the axis passing through $\mathrm{C}$ and perpendicular to the plane the moment of inertia is maximum.
$\therefore I_{C}>I_{P}>I_{B}>I_{H}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Four holes of radius $\mathrm{R}$ are cut from a thin square plate of side $4 \mathrm{R}$ and mass $\mathrm{M}$. The moment of inertia of the remaining portion about $z$ - axis is:-
explanation:
Mass of each disc
$m=\dfrac{M}{16 R^{2}} \times \pi R^{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{16} \mathrm{M}$
Moment of inertia of the remaining part is $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{\text {square }}-4 I_{\text {disc }}$
$ = \frac{{M{{(4R)}^2}}}{6} - 4\left[ {\frac{{m{R^2}}}{2} + m{{(\sqrt 2 R)}^2}} \right]$
$ = \frac{8}{3}M{R^2} - 10m{R^2}\left[ {{\rm{ here }}m = \frac{{M\pi }}{{16}}} \right]$
$ = \left( {\frac{8}{3} - \frac{{5\pi }}{8}} \right){\rm{M}}{{\rm{R}}^2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Bodies having Cavities

question: The moment of inertia of a uniform cylinder of length $\ell$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ about its perpendicular bisector is $\mathrm{I}$. What is the ratio $\ell / \mathrm{R}$ such that the moment of inertia is minimum?
explanation:
Momentum of inertia about perpendicular bisector is
${\rm{I}} = \frac{{m{\ell ^2}}}{{12}} + \frac{{{\rm{m}}{{\rm{R}}^2}}}{4}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{I}} = \frac{m}{4}\left( {\frac{{{\ell ^2}}}{3} + {{\rm{R}}^2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also $m=\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \ell \rho$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{2}=\dfrac{m}{\pi \ell \rho}$ Put in equation (1)
$\mathrm{I}=\dfrac{m}{4}\left(\dfrac{\ell^{2}}{3}+\dfrac{m}{\pi \ell \rho}\right)$
For maxima & minima
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{d} \ell}=\dfrac{m}{4}\left(\dfrac{2 \ell}{3}-\dfrac{m}{\pi \ell^{2} \rho}\right)=0 \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 \ell}{3}=\dfrac{m}{\pi \ell^{2} \rho} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 \ell}{3}=\dfrac{\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \ell \rho}{\pi \ell^{2} \rho} \\& \text { or } \dfrac{2 \ell}{3}=\dfrac{\mathrm{R}^{2}}{\ell} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\ell^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}=\dfrac{3}{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{\ell}{\mathrm{R}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform rod of mass $M$ and lenght $L$ about an axis passing through its mid-point and perpendicular to its length is $I_{0}$. Its moment of inertia about an axis passing through one of its ends and perpendicular to its length is
explanation:
According to parallel axes theorem
$I = {I_{CM}} + M{d^2}$
${\rm{So, }}I = {I_0} + M{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)^2}$
$ \Rightarrow I = {I_0} + \frac{{M{L^2}}}{4}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: The ratio of the radii of gyration of a circular disc to that of a circular ring, each of same mass and radius, around their respective axes is
explanation:
As we know that radius of gyration
$k=\sqrt{\dfrac{I}{m}}$
So, for two different cases
$\frac{{{k_{{\rm{ring }}}}}}{{{k_{{\rm{disc }}}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{I_{{\rm{ring }}}}}}{{{I_{{\rm{disc }}}}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{M{R^2}}}{{\frac{1}{2}M{R^2}}}} $
$\frac{{{k_{{\rm{disc }}}}}}{{{k_{{\rm{ring }}}}}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question: If radius of the solid sphere is doubled by keeping its mass constant, the ratio of their moment of inertia about any of its diameter is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of a solid sphere about its diameter is$I = \frac{2}{5}M{R^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As the radius of a solid sphere is doubled then the new moment of inertia of the sphere will be
${I^\prime } = \frac{2}{5}M{(2R)^2} = \frac{8}{5}M{R^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq. (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{I}{I^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{2}{5} M R^{2}}{\dfrac{8}{5} M R^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question: A square frame $A B C D$ is formed by four identical rods each of mass $m$ and length $l$. This frame is in $x y$ - plane such that side $AB$ coincides with $X$ - axis and side $AD$ along $Y$ - axis. The moment of inertia of the frame about $X$ - axis is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955861/preptracker/questions/q_35232_explanation_1764955860995.png The moment of inertia of the frame about $X$ axis,
$I = {I_{AB}} + {I_{BC}} + {I_{CD}} + {I_{DA}}$
$ = 0 + \frac{{m{l^2}}}{3} + m{l^2} + \frac{{m{l^2}}}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow I = \frac{{5m{l^2}}}{3}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Let $M$ be the mass and $L$ be the length of a thin uniform rod. In first case, axis of rotation is passing through centre and perpendicular to the length of the rod. In second case, axis of rotation is passing through one end and perpendicular to the length of the rod. The ratio of radius of gyration in first case to second case is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of rod whose axis is passing through centre and perpendicular to the rod is
${I_1} = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{{12}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\therefore$ Radius of gyration,
${K_1} = \sqrt {\frac{{{I_1}}}{M}} = \frac{L}{{2\sqrt 3 }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
In second case, moment of inertia when axis is passing through one end of the rod is
${I_2} = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
$\therefore$ Radius of gyration,
${K_2} = \sqrt {\frac{{{I_2}}}{M}} = \frac{L}{{\sqrt 3 }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
From (2) and (4), $\dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{L \times \sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{3} \times L}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: A thin uniform rod has mass $M$ and length $L$. The moment of inertia about an axis perpendicular to it and passing through the point at a distance $\dfrac{L}{3}$ from one of its end, will be
explanation:
A point '$P$' at a distance $\dfrac{L}{3}$ from one end of the rod is at a distance of $d = \frac{L}{2} - \frac{L}{3} = \frac{L}{6}$ from the centre '$C$' of rod
The moment of inertia of the rod, about an axis passing through centre and perpendicular to it, is
$I_{c}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}$
Using parallel axes theorem, moment of inertia of the rod, about an axis perpendicular to the rod and passing through $P$, is
$I_{p}=I_{c}+M d^{2}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}+M\left(\dfrac{L}{6}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{9}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: The ratio of radii of gyration of a ring to a disc ( both circular) of same radii and mass, about a tangential axis perpendicular to the plane is
explanation:
The radius of gyration is given by
$K=\sqrt{\dfrac{I}{M}} \Rightarrow K \propto \sqrt{I}$
Moment of inertia of the ring about a tangential axis perpendicular to the plane of the ring is (using parallel axes theorem),
$I_{\text {ring }}=M R^{2}+M R^{2}=2 M R^{2}$
Similarly, for the disc,
$I_{\text {disc }}=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}+M R^{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} M R^{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{K_{\text {ring }}}{K_{\text {disc }}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{\text {ring }}}{I_{\text {disc }}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 M R^{2}}{\dfrac{3}{2} M R^{2}}}=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Two circular loops $A$ and $B$ of radii $R$ and $N R$ respectively are made from a uniform wire. Moment of inertia of $B$ about its axis is 3 times that of $A$ about its axis. The value of $N$ is
explanation:
Let $\rho=$ mass per unit length of the wire.
Mass of loop A, $m_{A}=2 \pi R \rho$
Mass of loop B, $m_{B}=2 \pi N R \rho=N m_{A}$
Given, $\quad I_{B}=3 I_{A}$
$\Rightarrow m_{B} r_{B}^{2}=3 \times m_{A} r_{A}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(N m_{A}\right)(N R)^{2}=3 m_{A}(R)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow N^{3}=3 \Rightarrow N=(3)^{1 / 3}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question:
A thin uniform rectangular plate of mass $2\,kg$ is placed in $x-y$ plane as shown in figure.
The moment of inertia about $X$-axis is ${I_x} = 0.2\,kg\,{m^2}$ and moment of ineria about $y$-axis is ${I_x} = 0.3\,kg{\mkern 1mu} {m^2}$. The radius of gyration of the plate about the axis passing through O and per perpendicular to the plane of the plate is
explanation:
Given mass, $M = 2\,kg,{I_X} = 0.2\,kg\,{m^2}$ and ${I_Y} = 0.3\,kg\,{m^2}$.
From perpendicular axes theorem
${I_Z} = {I_X} + {I_Y} = (0.2 + 0.3)kg{m^2} = 0.5\,kg\,{m^2}$ Also, ${I_Z} = M{k^2}$
$\therefore 0.5 = 2{k^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{0.5}}{2} = \frac{1}{4}\,{m^2}$
$\therefore$ Radius of gyration, $k = \frac{1}{2}\,m = 50\,cm$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Moment of inertia of a body about two perpendicular axes $X$ and $Y$ in the plane of lamina are $20\,kg\,{m^2}$ and $25\,kg\,{m^2}$ respectively. Its moment of inertia about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the lamina and passing through the point of intersection of $X$ and $Y$ axes is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the body about $X$ axis, ${I_X} = 20\,kg\,{m^2}$ and about $Y\,\,axis,\,\,{I_Y} = 25\,kg\,{m^2}$ $\because$ The body is a planar lamina, using the theorem of perpendicular axes, the moment of inertia of the body about the given axis (i.e., $Z$ axis) is
${I_Z} = {I_X} + {I_Y} = (20 + 25)kg{m^2} = 45\,kg\,{m^2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: The moment of inertia of a circular ring of radius $r$ and mass $M$ about diameter is
explanation: Moment of inertia of a circular ring of radius $r$ and mass $M$ about its diameter is $\frac{{M{r^2}}}{2}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question: The moment of inertia of a circular disc of radius $2\,m$ and mass $1\,kg$ about an axis passing through the centre of mass but perpendicular to the plane of the disc is $2\,kg\,{m^2}$. Its moment of inertia about an axis parallel to this axis but passing through the edge of the disc is____  (See the given figure).
explanation:
Here,
Mass of the disc, $M = 1\,kg$
Radius of the disc, $R = 2\,m$
Moment of inertia of thecircular disc about $XY$ is
${I_{XY}} = 2\,kg\,{m^2}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {Given} \right)$
According to theorem of parallel axes, the moment of inertia of the circular disc about ${X_1}{Y_1}$ is
(1) ${I_{{X_1}{Y_1}}} = {I_{XY}} + M{R^2}$
$ = 2kg{m^2} + (1\,kg) \times {(2\,m)^2} = 6\,kg\,{m^2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: A thin rod of length $L$ and mass $M$ is bent at its mid-point into two halves so that the angle between them is $90^{\circ}$. The moment of inertia of the bent rod about an axis passing through the bending point and perpendicular to the plane defined by the two halves of the rod
explanation:
As the rod is bent into two equal halves the mass and length of each half is
$\dfrac{M}{2} \text { and } \dfrac{L}{2} \text { respectively. }$
The moment of inertia about an axis passing through bending point and perpendicular to the rod.
$I = 2\left[ {\frac{{{\rm{ Mass }} \times {{({\rm{ length }})}^2}}}{3}} \right]$
$\therefore I = 2 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{M}{2}{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{{12}}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Four identical thin rods each of mass $M$ and length $l$, form a square frame. Moment of inertia of this frame about an axis through the centre of the square and perpendicular to its plane is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the system
$=\dfrac{4\left[I_{C M}+M d^{2}\right]}{3}=4\left[\dfrac{M l^{2}}{12}+M\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^{2}\right]=\dfrac{4}{3} M l^{2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: A solid cylinder has mass $M$, radius $R$ and length $l$. Its moment of inertia about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its own axis is
explanation:
Let $X X^{\prime}$ be the axis of symmetry and $Y Y^{\prime}$ be the axis perpendicular to $X{X^\prime }$ at the centre of the cylinder. Let us consider a circular disc of width $d x$ at a distance $x$ from $Y Y^{\prime}$ axis. Mass per unit length of the cylinder is $\dfrac{M}{l}$. Thus, the mass of disc is $\dfrac{M}{l} d x$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951424/preptracker/questions/q_35123_explanation_1764951424049.png Moment of inertia of disc about $ZZ'$ axis, passing through a diameter of the disc parallel to $Y{Y^\prime }$,$d I^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{M}{l} d x\right) \dfrac{R^{2}}{4}$Moment of inertia of this disc about $Y{Y^\prime }$ axis using parallel axes theorem,
$d I=\left(\dfrac{M}{l} d x\right) \dfrac{R^{2}}{4}+\left(\dfrac{M}{l} d x\right) x^{2}$
Therefore, moment of inertia of cylinder about $Y{Y^\prime }$ axis,
$I=\int_{-l / 2}^{l / 2}\left(\dfrac{M}{l} d x\right) \dfrac{R^{2}}{4}+\int_{-l / 2}^{l / 2}\left(\dfrac{M}{l} d x\right) x^{2}$
$ = \frac{M}{l}\left[ {\left( {\int_{ - l/2}^{l/2} {\frac{{{R^2}}}{4}} + {x^2}} \right)dx} \right]$
$ = \frac{M}{l}\left[ {\frac{{{R^2}x}}{4} + \frac{{{x^3}}}{3}} \right]_{ - l/2}^{l/2}$
$ \Rightarrow I = M\left[ {\frac{{{R^2}}}{4} + \frac{{{l^2}}}{{12}}} \right]$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: Three points masses, each of mass $m$ are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle of side $l$. The moment of inertia of the system about an axis passing through one of the vertices and parallel to the side joining other two vertices, will be
explanation:
The given situation is as shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955845/preptracker/questions/q_35119_explanation_1764955845419.png The distance of line $X X^{\prime}$ from base of triangle,
$d=\sqrt{l^{2}-\dfrac{l^{2}}{4}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} l$
Total moment of inertia about $X X^{\prime}$ due to three point masses,
$I=m \times 0+m\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} l\right)^{2}+m\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} l\right)^{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} m l^{2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Group of Particles

question: Moment of inertia of a thin uniform rod rotating about the perpendicular axis passing through its centre is $I$. If the same rod is bent into a ring and its moment of inertia about its diameter is $I^{\prime}$, then the ratio $\dfrac{I}{I^{\prime}}$ is
explanation:
Let $M$ be the mass of the rod and $L$ be its length. Then moment of inertia of the rod about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to the rod is:
$I = \frac{1}{{12}}M{L^2}$
When the same rod is bent into the ring of radius $\mathrm{R}$, then
$2\pi R = L\quad or\,\,\,\,R = \frac{L}{{2\pi }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (1)$
Its moment of inertia about its diameter is
${I^\prime } = \frac{1}{2}M{R^2} = \frac{1}{2}M{\left( {\frac{L}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( {u\sin g{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 1 \right)} \right)$
$ = \frac{1}{{8{\pi ^2}}}M{L^2}$
$\therefore \frac{I}{{{I^\prime }}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{12}}M{L^2}}}{{\frac{1}{{8{\pi ^2}}}M{L^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{8{\pi ^2}}}{{12}} = \frac{2}{3}{\pi ^2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Parallel and Perpendicular Axis Theorems and Their Application

question: The ratio of the radius of gyration of a thin unifrom disc about an axis passing through its centre and normal to its plane to the radius of gyration of the disc about its diameter is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955844/preptracker/questions/q_35100_explanation_1764955843920.png $k = \sqrt {\frac{I}{m}} $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{m{R^2}/2}}{{m{R^2}/4}}} = \sqrt 2 :1\,\,$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Different Rigid Bodies Like Rod, Sphere, Disc etc

question: From a circular ring of mass ' $M$ ' and radius ' $R$ ' an arc corresponding to a $90^{\circ}$ sector is removed. The moment of inertia of the remaining part of the ring about an axis passing through the centre of the ring and perpendicular to the plane of the ring is $'K'$ times $'M{R^2}$. Then the value of $'K'$ is
explanation:
After removing the sector the mass of the remaining portion is $\dfrac{3 M}{4}$
$\begin{aligned}& I^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{3}{4} M\right) R^{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} M R^{2}=k M R^{2} \\& \Rightarrow K=\dfrac{3}{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Bodies having Cavities

question: From a disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$, a circular hole of diameter $R$, whose rim passes through the centre is cut. What is the moment of inertia of the remaining part of the disc about a perpendicular axis, passing through centre?
explanation:
Mass per unit area of disc $=\dfrac{M}{\pi R^{2}}$ Mass of removed portion of disc, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951378/preptracker/questions/q_35034_explanation_1764951378116.png $M^{\prime}=\dfrac{M}{\pi R^{2}} \times \pi\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{M}{4}$
Moment of inertia of removed portion about an axis passing through centre of disc $O$ and perpendicular to the plane of disc.
$\begin{gathered}I_{O}^{\prime}=I_{o^{\prime}}+M^{\prime} d^{2} \\=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{M}{4} \times\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2}+\dfrac{M}{4} \times\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2} \\=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{32}+\dfrac{M R^{2}}{16}=\dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{32}\end{gathered}$
The moment of inertia of complete disc about centre $O$ is
$I_{O}=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}$
So, moment of inertia of the disc with removed portion is
$I=I_{O}=I_{O}^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}-\dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{32}=\dfrac{13 M R^{2}}{32}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Bodies having Cavities

question:
Assertion: Radius of gyration of body is a constant quantity.


Reason: The radius of gyration of a body about an axis of rotation may be defined as the root mean square distance of the particle from the axis of rotation.
explanation:
Radius of gyration value changes with the change in location of the axis of rotation. Radius of gyration of a body about a given axis is given as
$K=\sqrt{\dfrac{r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}+\ldots .+r_{n}^{2}}{n}}$

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

explanation:

explanation:

question: Moment of inertia of a solid cylinder of mass $m_{1}$ length $L$ and diameter $D$ about an axis passing through its centre of mass and perpendicular to its geometric axis is
explanation: The moment of inertia,  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951335/preptracker/questions/q_34748_explanation_1764951335392.png $\begin{aligned}& d I=\dfrac{d m r^{2}}{4}+x^{2} d m \\& I=\dfrac{r^{2}}{4} \int d m+\int_{-L / 2}^{L / 2} \rho\left(\pi r^{2}\right) x^{2} d x \\& I=m\left(\dfrac{L^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{r^{2}}{4}\right)=m\left(\dfrac{L^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{D^{2}}{16}\right)\end{aligned}$

explanation: $I=2\left[\dfrac{2}{5} M\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2}\right]+M(2 R)^{2}=\dfrac{21}{5} M R^{2}$

question: Three identical thin rods, each of mass $m$ and length $\ell$, are joined to form an equilateral triangular frame. The moment of inertia of the frame about the axis parallel to its one side and passing through the opposite vertex is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of each of $\operatorname{rod} A C$ and $BC$ about the given axis $\mathrm{OO}$ ' is
$I_{A C}=I_{B C}=\dfrac{m \ell^{2}}{3} \sin ^{2} 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{m \ell^{2}}{4}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951334/preptracker/questions/q_34740_explanation_1764951333322.png and $M.I$ of rod $AB$ about the given axis $OO$ is
$I_{A B}=m\left(\dfrac{\ell \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} m \ell^{2}$
Hence,
$I=I_{A C}+I_{B C}+I_{A B}=\dfrac{m \ell^{2}}{4}+\dfrac{m \ell^{2}}{4}+\dfrac{3}{4} m \ell^{2}=\dfrac{5}{4} m \ell^{2}$
subject: Physics

question: The moment of inertia of a door of mass $m$, length $2 l$ and width $l$ about its longer side is
explanation:

Using parallel axis theorem:(about $AD$)
$I_{A D}=\dfrac{m l^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{m l^{2}}{4}=\dfrac{m l^{2}}{3}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955784/preptracker/questions/q_34736_explanation_1764955783860.png

question: From a uniform circular disc of radius $R$ and mass $9M$, a small disc of radius $\dfrac{R}{3}$ is removed as shown in the figure. The moment of inertia of the remaining disc about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the disc and passing through centre of disc is :
explanation:
MOI of removed part about axis passing through COM & $\perp$ to plane of disc
$=I_{c m}+M d^{2}$
$\dfrac{(M)(R / 3)^{2}}{2}+M\left[\dfrac{4 R^{2}}{9}\right]=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$
So $MOI$ of remaining portion
$=[\mathrm{MOI}$ of whole disc-MOI of removed part $]$
$=(9 M) \dfrac{R^{2}}{2}-\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}=4 M R^{2}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Bodies having Cavities

question: From a disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$, a circular hole of diameter $R$, whose rim passes through the centre is cut. What is the moment of inertia of the remaining part of the disc about a perpendicular axis, passing through the centre?
explanation:
$I_{\text {Total disc }}=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/08/09/EI-19_(1).png ${M_{{\mathop{\rm Re}\nolimits} moved}} = \frac{M}{{\pi {R^2}}} \times \pi {\left( {\frac{R}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{M}{4}$
$I_{\text {Removed }}$ (about same perpendicular axis)
$=\dfrac{M}{4} \dfrac{(R / 2)^{2}}{2}+\dfrac{M}{4}\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{32}$
$I_{\text {Remaining disc }}=I_{\text {Total }}-I_{\text {Removed }}$
$=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}-\dfrac{3}{32} M R^{2}=\dfrac{13}{32} M R^{2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Moment of Inertia of Bodies having Cavities

question: The moment of inertia of a uniform rod of length $2 l$ and mass $m$ about an axis $x x^{\prime}$ passing through its centre and inclined at an angle $\alpha$ is
explanation:
https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/08/09/EI-19_(2).png $dI = dm{x^2}{\sin ^2}\alpha $
$I = \int {{x^2}} {\sin ^2}\alpha \mu {\mkern 1mu} dx$
$(dm = \mu \,dx)$
$I = {\sin ^2}\alpha \mu \int_0^x {{x^2}} dx$
$I = \frac{{{{\sin }^2}\alpha \mu {l^3}}}{3}$
$I = \frac{{{{\sin }^2}\alpha {l^3}(m)}}{{6l}}\left( {\mu = \frac{m}{{2l}}} \right)$
${I_{{\rm{Net }}}} = 2I = \frac{{m{l^2}{{\sin }^2}\alpha }}{3}$

explanation: $L=2 \pi r \Rightarrow r=\dfrac{L}{2 \pi}, m=\rho L$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956873/preptracker/questions/q_34574_explanation_1764956872760.png $\begin{aligned}L_{x y} & =\dfrac{3}{2} m r^{2} \\& =\dfrac{3}{2} \rho L\left(\dfrac{L}{2 \pi}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{3}{8} \dfrac{\rho L^{3}}{\pi^{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: A rod of length $L$ is composed of a uniform length $\dfrac{1}{2} L$ of wood whose mass is $m_{w}$ and a uniform length $\dfrac{1}{2} L$ of brass whose mass is $m_{b}$. The moment of inertia I of the rod about an axis perpendicular to the rod and through its centre is equal to
explanation:
Treat each half separately. For the wood, the moment of inertia about the given axis is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951246/preptracker/questions/q_34570_explanation_1764951246333.png $I_{w}=\dfrac{1}{12} m_{w}\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}+m_{w}\left(\dfrac{L}{4}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{12} m_{w} L^{2}$
Similarly, for the brass, the moment of inertia about the given axis is
$I_{b}=\dfrac{1}{12} m_{b}\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}+m_{b}\left(\dfrac{L}{4}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{12} m_{b} L^{2}$
$\therefore$ The moment of inertia of the given rod about the given axis is $I=I_{w}+I_{b}$
$=\dfrac{1}{12} m_{w} L^{2}+\dfrac{1}{12} m_{b} L^{2}=\dfrac{1}{12}\left(m_{w}+m_{b}\right) L^{2} .$

question:

C. Thin rod of length $L$ about an axis passing through end point
D. Circular disc of radius $R$ about an axis perpendicular to the disc and touching the edge.

Q. $M{L^2}/3$

explanation:
Circular ring of radius $R$ having axis perpendicular to the plane and passing through centre of mass $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{MR}^{2}$.
For the same ring if the axis is passing through diameter then
$I=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$
For the given rod
$I=I_{C M}+M\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{M L^{2}}{4}=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{3}$
For the disc
$I=I_{C M}+M\left(R^{2}\right)=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}+M R^{2}=\dfrac{3 M R^{2}}{2}$
Option (3) is correct

question: A disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is rolling horizontally without slipping with speed $v$. It then moves up an inclined smooth surface as shown in figure. The maximum height that the disc can go up the incline is
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& E_{A}=E_{B} \\& \dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=M g h+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} \\& \dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}=M g h\end{aligned}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954450/preptracker/questions/q_37196_explanation_1764954449894.png
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{3}{2} M v^{2}=M g h$
So, maximum height, $h=\dfrac{1 v^{2}}{2 g}$

question: A loop and a disc roll without slipping with same linear velocity $v$. The mass of the loop and the disc is same. If the total kinetic energy of the loop is 8 $J$ , find the kinetic energy of the disc
explanation:
Total kinetic energy of loop
$=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega+\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}$$=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(M R^{2} \omega^{2}\right)+\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}=M v^{2}=8 {~J}(\text { given })$
Total kinetic energy of disc
$ = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2} + \frac{1}{2}M{v^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{1}{2}M{R^2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{{v^2}}}{{{R^2}}}} \right) + \frac{1}{2}M{v^2}$
$ = \frac{3}{4}M{v^2} = \frac{3}{4}(8) = 6\,J$
