question: A boy is pushing a ring of mass 2 $kg$ and radius 0.5 $m$ with a stick as shown in the figure. The stick applies a force of 2 $N$ on the ring and rolls it without slipping with an acceleration of $0.3\,m/{s^2}.$ The coefficient of friction between the ground and the ring is large enough that rolling always occurs and the coefficient of friction between the stick and the ring is $(P / 10)$.  Find the value of $P$
explanation:
Note: If net force applied by the rod is considered to be 2 $N$ . https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954331/preptracker/questions/q_37117_explanation_1764954330410.png $\sqrt {{f^{\prime 2}} + {F^2}} = 2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$FR - {f^\prime }R = 2m{R^2}\frac{a}{R}$
$F - f' = 2\,ma = 1.2{\mkern 1mu} \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), $\left(1.2+f^{\prime}\right)^{2}+f^{\prime 2}=2^{2}$
$2{f^{\prime 2}} + 2.4{f^\prime } + 1.44 = 4$
${f^{\prime 2}} + 1.2{f^\prime } + 0.72 - 2 = 0$
${f^{\prime 2}} + 1.2{f^\prime } - 1.28 = 0$
${f^\prime } = \frac{{ - 1.2 \pm \sqrt {1.44 + 4 \times (1.28)} }}{2}$
$ = 0.6 \pm \sqrt {0.36 + 1.28} $
$ = - 0.6 \pm \sqrt {0.64} = 0.68$
From Eq. (2), $F=1.88$.
$\mu = \frac{{0.68}}{{1.88}} = \frac{P}{{10}} \Rightarrow P = 3.16 \approx 4$
But if only normal reaction applied by the rod is considered to be 2 $N$ .
IInd Law $\Rightarrow 2-f=2[0.3] \Rightarrow f=2-0.6$
$f = 1.4\,Nx\quad \quad \quad (1)$
$A = R\,\alpha $
$ \Rightarrow \quad 0.3 = \alpha [0.5]$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{3}{5}rad/s\quad \quad \quad (2)$
${\tau _c} = {I_c}\alpha \Rightarrow fR - 2\,\,\mu R = m{R^2}\alpha $
$f - 2\,\,\mu = mR\alpha $
$1.4 - 2\,\,\mu = \frac{2}{2}\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right)$
$1.4 - 0.6 = 2\mu $
$0.8 = 2\,\,\mu \Rightarrow \mu = 0.4 = \frac{P}{{10}}$
$\therefore \quad P = 4$
subject: Physics

question: A solid sphere of mass $m$ and radius $r$ is released from rest from the given position. The sphere rolls without sliding on the circular track of radius $R=8 r$. If $r=1 {~m}$ then find its speed  when it reaches its lowest position. (Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
As the sphere is rolling, the static friction does not perform any work. Hence mechanical energy is conserved
According to conservation of energy, $\Delta K+\Delta U=0$
or $ \Rightarrow \left( {{K_f} - {K_i}} \right) + \Delta U = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When the sphere reaches its lowest position it loses gravitational potential energy by $\Delta U$ and gains a kinetic energy by $\Delta K$, where$\Delta U = - mg(R - r)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955992/preptracker/questions/q_37068_explanation_1764955991780.png The sphere is released from rest, its initial kinetic energy $K_{i}=0$
And kinetic energy when the sphere reaches at its lowest position
${K_f} = (1 + c)\frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
For sphere,
$c=\dfrac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{2}{5} R^{2}}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{2}{5}$
$K_{f}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{2}{5}\right)=\dfrac{7}{10} m v^{2}$
Hence from (1), $\left(\dfrac{7}{10} m v^{2}-0\right)+(-m g(R-r))=0$
We get,
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{10}}{7}g(R - r)} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{10}}{7} \times 10 \times 7 \times 1} = 10\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform cylinder (mass $M$ ) of radius $R$ is kept on an accelerating platform (mass $M$ ) as shown in figure. If the cylinder rolls without slipping on the platform, determine the magnitude of acceleration of the centre of mass of cylinder. Assuming the coefficient of friction $\mu=0.4$. determine  the maximum acceleration of the platform   may have without slip between the cylinder and the platform. (Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
As there is no sliding between the cylinder and plank, the friction will be static in nature. For finding the direction of friction assume, there is no friction anywhere, it suggest sliding tendency of the cylinder w.r.t plank will be in backward direction. It means friction force on the cylinder will act in forward direction. The friction acting on the cylinder will be responsible for angular acceleration of the cylinder. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954209/preptracker/questions/q_37062_explanation_1764954209196.png Let angular acceleration of the cylinder is $\alpha$ and the acceleration be $a_{1}$.
From F.B.D of cylinder
Force equation of cylinder; $f = M{a_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
As axes of rotation is moving so we can apply torque equation about centripetal axis
$\begin{align*}& \tau_{0}=I_{0} \alpha \Rightarrow f \cdot R=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} \alpha \\& \Rightarrow \quad R \alpha=\dfrac{2 f}{M} \tag{2}\end{align*}$
As the cylinder is rolling, it means point of contact of cylinder with plank will not slide.
It means acceleration of the plank $=$ acceleration of point $P$
$\begin{equation*}a=a_{0}+\alpha R \tag{3}\end{equation*}$
From (1),(2) and (3) we get
$f = \frac{{Ma}}{3}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
As there is no sliding, it means
$\frac{{Ma}}{3} \le \mu Mg \Rightarrow a = 3\mu g$
$ = 3 \times 0.4 \times 10 = 12\,m/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

subtopic: Pure Rolling on an Inclined Plane
parent_topic: Plane Motion of a Rigid Body (Translation and Rotation)

question: A bobbin has inner radius $r$ and outer radius $R$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface. A light is string wrapped over inner core, connects a block with bobbin as shown in the figure. Now system is released from rest and bobbin moves on the horizontal surface without sliding, and the string does not slide from bobbin, If $\dfrac{r}{R}=0.25$, find the ratio of the acceleration of the block and bobbin $\left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right)$.
explanation:
As the motion of the bobbin is pure rolling, point $P$ should be instantaneous centre of rotation $(ICR)$ of the bobbin. Hence we can consider pure rotation of the bobbin about $P$. The acceleration of point $C ; a_{C}=\alpha R$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951736/preptracker/questions/q_36996_explanation_1764951735607.png But the bobbin is moving with acceleration $A$,
Hence $A = \alpha R\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The acceleration of point $Q ; a_{Q}=\alpha(R-r)$.
But point $O$ is directly connected with block, it means the magnitude of acceleration of the point $Q$ should be equal to the acceleration of the block
${a_Q} = a = \alpha (R - r)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{a}{A}=\dfrac{R-r}{R}=\left(1-\dfrac{r}{R}\right)=1-0.25=0.75$

question: A solid sphere of mass $10\,kg$ is placed on a rough surface having coefficient of friction $\mu  = 0.1.$ A constant force $F=7\,N$ is applied along a line passing through the centre of the sphere as shown in the figure. What is the value of frictional force  on the sphere?
explanation:
Let ${f}$ be frictional force towards left for pure rolling.
$a=\alpha R$
where, ${a=\dfrac{F-f}{m}=\dfrac{7-f}{10}}$
${\alpha=\dfrac{\tau}{I}=\dfrac{f R}{I}=\dfrac{f R}{\dfrac{2}{5} m R^{2}}=\dfrac{5 f}{2 m R}}$
Hence, ${\dfrac{7-f}{10}=\dfrac{5 f}{20} \Rightarrow f=2 {~N}}$

question: A small solid ball (mass ${=0.1 {~kg}}$) rolls without slipping along the track shown in the figure. The radius of the circular track is ${R}$. If the ball starts from rest at a height ${8 R}$ above the bottom, the horizontal force acting on it at point ${P}$ is ${5 x}$ newton.  Find  the value of ${x}$   (Given, ${g=10 {~ms}^{-2}}$)
explanation:
At point ${P}$,
Decrease in GPH ${=}$ Increase in ${(K)_{T}+}$ Increase in ${(K)_{R}}$
$mg \cdot 7R = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{2}{5}m{r^2}} \right) \cdot \frac{{{v^2}}}{{{r^2}}}$
${=\dfrac{7}{10} m v^{2}(r}$ is radius of ball ${)}$
${\therefore \quad v^{2}=10 g R}$
Centripetal force is
$\frac{{m{v^2}}}{R} = \frac{{m(10gR)}}{R} = 10mg$
$ = 10(0.1)(10) = 10\,N \Rightarrow x = 2$

explanation:

$V_{\text {plank }}=v_{\text {disc }}+\omega R$
$ = 2 + \left( {2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \right) = 3\,m/s$

question:
A heavy uniform bar is carried by two men on their shoulder. The weight of the bar is $42\,N.$ If one man lets it fall from the end carried by him, the weight experienced by the other at the instant the rod is falling is
(Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955981/preptracker/questions/q_36678_explanation_1764955981231.png The bar turns about $A$ with an angular acceleration $\alpha$ after withdrawing the support of one man.
$\therefore \quad I\alpha = W\left( {\frac{l}{2}} \right)$
$\therefore \quad \alpha = \frac{{Wl}}{{2I}}$
For a bar, $I = \frac{{m{l^2}}}{3}$
$\therefore \quad \alpha = \frac{{mgl \times 3}}{{2 \times m{l^2}}} = \frac{{3\,g}}{{2l}}$
The linear acceleration of $C.M.$
$a = \frac{l}{2} \times \alpha = \frac{l}{2} \times \frac{{3\,g}}{{2l}} = \frac{{3\,g}}{4}$
From figure, $W - R = ma$
$\therefore mg - R = m \times \left( {\frac{{3\,g}}{4}} \right)$
$\therefore \quad R = \frac{{mg}}{4} = \frac{W}{4} = \frac{{42}}{4} = 10.5\,N$
subject: Physics

question: A thin hollow sphere of mass ${m}$ is completely filled with an ideal liquid of mass ${m}$. When the sphere rolls with a velocity ${v}$, kinetic energy of the system is equal to :
explanation:
There are two components of $K.E$. in this problem. One from translational motion for which both sphere and liquid will contribute giving total linear $K.E$. as
${\text{K}}.{{\text{E}}_l}{\text{ = }}\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = m{v^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The second component of K.E. will be rotational for which only sphere will contribute (liquid will not rotate) and will be
$K.E{._r} = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{2}{3}m{r^2}} \right){\omega ^2} = \frac{1}{3}m{v^2}$
$\left[ {\because {I_{{\text{hollow sphere }}}} = \frac{2}{3}m{R^2}{\text{ and }}v = r\omega } \right]\,\,(2)$
Total K.E. of the system will be
$K.E. = K.E{._l} + K.E.\,r$
${\mkern 1mu} \quad \,{\mkern 1mu} = m{v^2} + \frac{1}{3}m{v^2} = \frac{4}{3}m{v^2}.$
So correct option is (3)

question: One solid sphere and disc of same radius are falling along an inclined plane without slipping. One reaches earlier than the other due to
explanation:
(i) For solid sphere, the moment of inertia about the diameter is ${I_s} = \frac{2}{5}M{R^2}$
Now $I = M{K^2}$ for any body, where $K$ is radius of gyration of that body.
so $M{K^2} = \frac{2}{5}M{R^2} \Rightarrow K = R\sqrt {2/5} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
(ii) The moment of inertia of disc about an axis passing through its centre & perpendicular to plane is
${I_d} = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2} = M{K^2} \Rightarrow K = R\sqrt {1/2} {\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Now acceleration of any body which is rolling on an inclined plane is
$a = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{1 + {K^2}/{R^2}}}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
For same $R$, the acceleration of the body depends only on radius of gyration $K$ [see eq(3)] so solid sphere will reach earlier to bottom of an inclined plane than disc.

question: The ratio of the accelerations for a solid sphere (mass '$m$' and radius '$R$') rolling down an incline of angle '$\theta$' without slipping and slipping down the incline without rolling is :
explanation:
For solid sphere rolling without slipping on inclined plane, acceleration
${a_1} = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{1 + \frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}}}}$
For solid sphere slipping on inclined plane without rolling, acceleration
$a_{2}=g \sin \theta$
Therefore required ratio $ = \frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}}$
$ = \frac{1}{{1 + \frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}}}} = \frac{1}{{1 + \frac{2}{5}}} = \frac{5}{7}$

question: A body is rolling down an inclined plane. If $K E$ of rotation is $40 \%$  $K.E.$  of in translatory state, then the body is a
explanation:
$K E$ of rotation is $40 \%$ of in translatory
$\begin{aligned}(K E)_{\text {rot }} & =40 \% \times(K E)_{\text {trans }} \\\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} & =\dfrac{40}{100} \times \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\\Rightarrow \quad I \omega^{2} & =\dfrac{2}{5} m(\omega r)^{2} \quad[\because v=\omega r] \\\Rightarrow \quad I & =\dfrac{2}{5} m r^{2}\end{aligned}$
where, $I$ is moment of inertia.Thus, the body is a solid sphere (solid ball).

question: A coin is of mass $4.8\,kg$ and radius $1\,m$ rolling on a horizontal surface without sliding with angular velocity 600 rot ${\min ^{ - 1}}.$ What is total kinetic energy of the coin?
explanation:
Angular velocity is given by
$\omega = 600\,\,rot\,\,{\min ^{ - 1}}$
$ = \frac{{600 \times 2\pi }}{{60}}\,\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
$ = 20\,\pi \,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
Kinetic energy of coin which is due to rotation and translation is
$\begin{aligned}& K=(K E) \text { rotation }+(K E) \text { translation } \\& K=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} \omega^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m(r \omega)^{2}\end{aligned}$
Given, $m = 4.8\,kg$ and $r=1 \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned}=\dfrac{1}{4} \times 4.8 & \times(1)^{2}(20 \pi)^{2} \\& \quad+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4.8 \times(20 \pi \times 1)^{2}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}& =480 \pi^{2}+960 \pi^{2} \\& =1440 \pi^{2} J\end{aligned}$

question: A cylinder rolls up an inclined plane, reaches some height and then rolls down (without slipping throughout these motions). The directions of the frictional force acting on the cylinder are
explanation: The direction of the frictional force will be up the inclined plane in both the motions. The friction acts opposite to the relative motion of the point of contact on ground. In both cases, the force acting on the point of cylinder in contact with the plane is $m g \sin \theta$. As there is pure rolling, the velocity and acceleration of the point of contact must be zero, so friction acts upwards along the inclined plane in both the motions (ascending or descending). https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955979/preptracker/questions/q_36588_explanation_1764955978651.png

options.text: up the incline while ascending and down the incline while descending
up the incline while ascending as well as descending
down the incline while ascending and up the incline while descending
down the incline while ascending as well as descending

question: A disc is rolling without slipping on a surface. The radius of the disc is $R$. At $t=0$, the top most point on the disc is $A$ as shown in figure. When the disc completes half of its rotation, the displacement of point $A$ from its initial position is
explanation:
Consider motion of the rolling disc as shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951666/preptracker/questions/q_36344_explanation_1764951665949.png For pure rolling motion, during half of the rotation the new locations of $O$ and $A$ will $O^{\prime}$ and $A^{\prime}$ as shown.
Thus, displacement of point $A$ is given by
$AA' = \sqrt {{{\left( {OA'} \right)}^2} + {{(OA)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {{{(\pi R)}^2} + {{(2R)}^2}} = R\sqrt {\left( {{\pi ^2} + 4} \right)} $

question:
Assertion: A sphere cannot roll on a smooth inclined surface.

Reason: For a smooth inclined surface force of friction is equal to zero.
explanation:
$F_{1}$ and $F_{2}$ are components of $F=m g$. Here $N$ and $F_{2}$ cancel. $F_{1}$ causes linear acceleration $a=g \sin \theta$ and so sphere slides down. Surface is smooth
(i.e, coefficient of friction $\mu=0$ ) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955952/preptracker/questions/q_36229_explanation_1764955951513.png Both $N$ and $m g(=F)$ are passing through the centre of ball $\Rightarrow$ They (i.e, $N$ and $m g$ ) can not apply any torque on sphere $\Rightarrow$ Angular acceleration $\alpha$ will be zero. $\Rightarrow$ Rolling is not possible (if $\mu=0$ ) on inclined surface. It just acts like a block on a friction-less ramp. So, correct option is (1).

question: A spherical shell rolls on a table without slipping. What is the percentage of its K.E which is rotational?
explanation:
$K . E$ of rotation $=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} I \times \dfrac{v^{2}}{r^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{m \times r^{2} \times v^{2}}{r^{2}}$
K.E of radiation $=\dfrac{1}{3} m v^{2}$
Total energy
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{K^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{2}{3}\right)=\dfrac{5}{6} m v^{2}$
$\therefore \frac{{{\rm{Rotational}}\,{\rm{K}}.{\rm{E}}}}{{{\rm{Total}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Energy}}}} \times 100 = \frac{{\frac{1}{3}m{v^2}}}{{\frac{5}{6}m{v^2}}} \times 100$
$=\dfrac{2}{5} \times 100=40 \%$

question: A ball rolls without slipping. The radius of gyration of the ball about an axis passing through its centre of mass is $K$. If radius of the ball be $R$, then the fraction of total enery associated with its rotational energy will be
explanation:

question: A sphere rolling with the centre of mass having a linear velocity $2.8\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ rolls up an inclined plane as shown. The maximum distance it travels on the plane is.
explanation:
Given, initial velocity of sphere, $u = 2.8\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ Acceleration on the inclined plane,
$a = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{1 + \frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where, $K$ and $R$ are the radius of gyration and radius of sphere, respectively.
Moment of inertia of sphere,
$I=m K^{2}=\dfrac{2}{5} m R^{2}$
$\frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}} = \frac{2}{5}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we have
$a=\dfrac{g \sin 30^{\circ}}{1+\dfrac{2}{5}}\left[\because \theta=30^{\circ}, \text { given }\right]$
$=\dfrac{5 g}{14}=\dfrac{25}{7} m s^{-2} \quad\left[\therefore g=10 m^{-2}\right]$
If $s$ be the maximum distance travelled by the sphere, then at maximum distance, $v=0$.
$\therefore$ From equation of motion,
$v^{2}=u^{2}-2 a s$
$0=u^{2}-2 a s$
$\Rightarrow s=\dfrac{u^{2}}{2 a}=\dfrac{(2.8)^{2} \times 7}{2 \times 25}$
$ = 1.09\,m \simeq 1.38\,m$

question: A thin hollow sphere of mass $m$ is completely filled with a liquid of mass $m$. When the sphere rolls with a velocity $v$, kinetic energy of the system is (neglect friction)
explanation:
Total kinetic energy $(K E)$ of the system
$=K E($ translational $)+K E($ rotational $)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2}(2 m) v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2}(2 m) v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{2}{3} m R^{2}\right) \dfrac{v^{2}}{R^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2}(2 m) v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{3} m v^{2}=\dfrac{4}{3} m v^{2}$

question:
Assertion: The total kinetic energy of a rolling solid sphere is the sum of translational and rotational kinetic energies.

Reason: For all solid bodies, total kinetic energy is always twice of translational kinetic energy.
explanation:
The kinetic energy of a rolling solid sphere
$=\left(E_{k}\right)_{\text {trans }}+\left(E_{k}\right)_{\text {rot }}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{5} m R^{2} \omega^{2} \quad\left[\because \omega=\dfrac{v}{R}\right]$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{5} m v^{2}=7 / 10 m v^{2}$
Hence, total kinetic energy is sum of translation and rotational kinetic energies.
For different solid bodies, total kinetic energy is not always twice of translation kinetic energy. Hence, Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and reason are incorrect.

question:
Assertion: The velocity of a body at the bottom of an inclined plane of given height is more when it slides down the plane, compared to when it rolling down the same plane.

Reason: In rolling down, a body acquires both, kinetic energy of translation and rotation.
explanation:
In sliding down, the entire potential energy of a body is converted only into translational energy. While in rolling motion, some part of potential energy is converted into kinetic energy of rotation and rest into kinetic energy of translation. Therefore, in sliding motion, the velocity acquired by the body is more.
Hence, the velocity of a body at the bottom of an inclined plane is more when it slides as compared to rolls.
So, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question: A solid sphere is rolling on a frictionless surface, shown in figure with a translational velocity $v\,{\rm{m}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - 1}}$. If it is to climb the inclined surface to a height ' $h$ ', then $v$ should be
explanation:
According to energy conservation,
Where,
$U=E_{k}+\left(E_{k}\right)_{r o t}$
$U=$ Potential energy
$E_{k}=$ Translational kinetic energy
$\left(E_{k}\right)_{r o t}=$ Rotational kinetic energy
$\Rightarrow m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{2}{5} m R^{2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{R^{2}}$
$\left[\because I=(2 / 5) m R^{2}\right.$ for a solid sphere $]$
$\Rightarrow m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{5} m v^{2}=\dfrac{7}{10} m v^{2}$
$\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{10}{7} g h}$.
Hence, to climb the inclined surface $v$ should be $\geq \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{7} g h}$.

question: A uniform disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is free to rotate only about its axis. A string is wrapped over its rim and a body of mass $m$ is tied to the end of the string as shown in the figure. The body is released from rest. Then the acceleration of the body
explanation:
Let $a$ is the acceleration of the block and $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration of the disc. Force on the block is
$m g-T=m a$
Torque on the disc is
$\begin{aligned}& R T=I \alpha \\& R T=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{a}{R} \\& T=\dfrac{M a}{2}\end{aligned}$
From the above equations.
$\begin{aligned}& m g-\dfrac{M a}{2}=m a \\& a=\dfrac{2 m g}{M+2 m}\end{aligned}$

question: A uniform solid cylindrical roller of mass ' $m$ 'is being pulled on a horizontal surface with force F parallel to the surface and applied at its centre. If the acceleration of the cylindrical roller is ' $a$ ' and it is rolling without slipping then the value of 'F' is
explanation:
Let $a$ and $\alpha$ are the linear and angular accelerations of the cylinder. $f$ is the static friction acting in backward direction.
$F-f=m a \text { and } f R=I \alpha$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951561/preptracker/questions/q_35657_explanation_1764951560423.png For pure rolling $a=\alpha R$
$F - f = ma\quad \,\,\,\,\,\,({\rm{1}})$
$fR = \frac{{m{R^2}}}{2} \cdot \frac{a}{R}f = \frac{{ma}}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow F = \frac{3}{2}ma$

question:
Consider a cylinder of mass $\mathrm{M}$ resting on a rough horizontal rug that is pulled out from under it with acceleration ' $a$ ' perpendicular to the axis of the cylinder.
What is $\mathrm{F}_{\text {friction }}$ at point $\mathrm{P}$ ? It is assumed that the cylinder does not slip.
explanation:
Let $f$ be the friction present between the plank and cylinder. Let $a_{1}$ and $\alpha$ are linear and angular accelerations of the cylinder. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956898/preptracker/questions/q_35640_explanation_1764956897622.png $f = M{a_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$fR = \frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}\alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
constraint relation
$a = {a_1} + R\alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From the above three eq's we get
$f=\dfrac{M a}{3}$

question: A cylinder of mass $M_{c}$ and sphere of mass $M_{s}$ are placed at points $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ of two inclines respectively. (See Figure). If they roll on the incline without slipping such that their accelerations are the same, then ratio of $\dfrac{\sin \theta_{\mathrm{C}}}{\sin \theta_{\mathrm{s}}}$ is :
explanation:
The acceleration of rolling body on an incline plane is
$a=\dfrac{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta}{\left[1+\dfrac{\mathrm{K}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\right]}$
Given that $a_{c}=a_{s}$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta_{\mathrm{c}}}{1+\dfrac{1}{2}}=\dfrac{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta_{\mathrm{s}}}{1+\dfrac{2}{5}} \\& \dfrac{\sin \theta_{\mathrm{c}}}{\sin \theta_{\mathrm{s}}}=\dfrac{3 / 2}{7 / 5}=\dfrac{15}{14}\end{aligned}$

question: A ring A is initially rolling without sliding with a velocity $v$ on the horizontal surface of the body B (of same mass as A). All surfaces are smooth. $B$ has no initial velocity. What will be the maximum height reached by A on $\mathrm{B}$ ?
explanation:
When the ring is at the maximum height, the wedge and the ring have the same horizontal component of velocity. As all the surfaces are smooth, angular velocity of the ring remains constant.
From conservation of mechanical energy, we get
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{\prime 2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{\prime 2}+m g h$
where $v^{\prime}$ is final common velocity
From conservation of momentum
$v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{2} \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{v^{2}}{4 g}$

question: A solid sphere of mass $m$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is lying on a rough horizontal plane. A constant force $4 \mathrm{mg}$ acts vertically at point $\mathrm{P}$ such that $\mathrm{OP}$ makes an angle $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. Find the minimum value of coefficient of friction so that sphere starts pure rolling.
explanation:
Net torque about point $\mathrm{O}$ is $\tau_{O}=I_{O} \alpha$
$4 m g\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)-f R=I_{C M} \alpha=I_{C M}\left(\dfrac{a}{R}\right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955906/preptracker/questions/q_35530_explanation_1764955905816.png $\Rightarrow \dfrac{2}{5} m R \alpha+f=2 m g$
$\& f=m a=m \alpha R$
$\Rightarrow f=\dfrac{10}{7} m g \Rightarrow \dfrac{10}{7} m g \leq \mu N$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{10}{7} m g \leq \mu(5 m g) \Rightarrow \mu \geq\left(\dfrac{2}{7}\right)$

question: A uniform disc of mass $m$ is fitted (pivoted smoothly) with a rod of mass $\dfrac{m}{2}$. If the bottom of the rod is pulled with a velocity $v$ it moves without changing its angle of orientation and the disc rolls without sliding. The kinetic energy of the system(rod+disc) is
explanation:
Rolling can be considered as pure rotation about point of contact
${K_{{\rm{rolling }}}} = \frac{1}{2}{I_P}{\omega ^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{m{R^2}}}{2} + m{R^2}} \right){\omega ^2}$
$ = \frac{3}{4}m{R^2}{\omega ^2}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$
The rod translates with the velocity $v$ hence velocity of centre of disc will also be $v$
$v = \omega R\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2); $K_{\text {rolling }}=\dfrac{3}{4} m v^{2}$
Kinetic energy of the rod
$\begin{aligned}& K_{\text {rod }}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{m}{2}\right) v^{2}=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{4} \\& K_{\text {total }}=k_{\text {rolling }}+k_{\text {rod }}=m v^{2}\end{aligned}$

question: A solid cylinder is wrapped with a string and placed on an inclined plane as shown in the figure. Then the frictional force acting between cylinder and plane is
explanation:
To maintain the cylinder in rotational equilibrium the friction should act upward. Let us assume cylinder is not moving, then for translation equilibrium
$T+f_{s}=m g \sin \theta$
For rotational equilibrium
$T . R-f_{s} R=0 \Rightarrow f_{s}=\dfrac{m g \sqrt{3}}{4}$
$\operatorname{But}\left(f_{s}\right)_{\max }=\mu N=\mu m g \cos \theta$
$=0.4 . m g \times \dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{m g}{5}$
Here, $f_{S}>f_{S_{\max }}$ which is not possible so the body can't be at rest. So, friction existing must be kinetic
$f_{k}=\mu m g \cos \theta=0.4 \times m g \times \dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{m g}{5}$

question: A thin uniform hemi spherical bowl of mass $m$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is lying on a smooth horizontal surface. A horizontal force $\mathrm{F}$ is now applied perpendicular to the rim of the bowl. The instantaneous angular acceleration of the bowl will be
explanation:
From the centre of the hemisphere the C.M lies at a distance $\dfrac{R}{2}$. Frictional force acting on the sphere is zero. The normal force passes through the C.M https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951508/preptracker/questions/q_35384_explanation_1764951508242.png The torque is measured w.r.t. C.M
$\tau_{N}=\tau_{m g}=0$
$\tau_{c}=F \dfrac{R}{2}=I_{c} \alpha$
From parallel axis theorem
$I_{O}=I_{c}+m\left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)^{2}$
$\dfrac{2}{3} m R^{2}=I_{c}+\dfrac{m R^{2}}{4}$
$I_{c}=\dfrac{5 m R^{2}}{12}$
$F \dfrac{R}{2}=\dfrac{5 m R^{2}}{12} \alpha$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{6 F}{5 m R}$

question: When a body slides down a smooth inclined plane having an angle $\theta$, it reaches the bottom with velocity $v$. If a sphere rolls down the same inclined plane, its linear velocity at the bottom of the plane is
explanation:
If total height of the inclined plane is $h$, then speed of a body sliding down the smooth plane is $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$ When a sphere rolls down the incline, its linear velocity is
${v_{CM}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2gh}}{{1 + \frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}}}}} = \frac{v}{{\sqrt {1 + \frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}}} }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For uniform solid sphere
$\frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}} = \frac{2}{5}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Substittuting (2) in (1),
$v_{C M}=\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{1+\dfrac{2}{5}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{7}} v$

question: Three bodies a ring $(R)$, a solid cylinder $(C)$ and a solid sphere$(S)$ having same mass and same radius roll down the inclined plane without slipping. They start from rest, if ${v_R},{v_C}$ and ${v_S}$ are velocities of respective bodies on reaching the bottom of the plane, then
explanation:
Using conservation of energy, velocity of the body at the bottom of incline when its rolls down the inclined plane,
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{2gh}}{{\left( {1 + I/m{R^2}} \right)}}} $
${I_R} = m{R^2},{I_C} = \frac{1}{2}m{R^2},{I_S} = \frac{2}{5}m{R^2}$
$\because {I_R} > {I_C} > {I_S}\therefore {v_R} < {v_C} < {v_S}$

question: A solid sphere of mass $m$ rolls down an inclined plane without slipping, starting from rest at the top of an incined plane. The linear speed of the sphere at the bottom of the inclined plane is $v$. The kinetic energy of the sphere at the bottom is
explanation:
The kinetic energy of the solid sphere at the bottom is
$K = {K_T} + {K_R} = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{2}{5}m{R^2}} \right){\omega ^2}$
[For solid sphere, $I = \frac{2}{5}m{R^2}$]
$ = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2} + \frac{1}{5}m{R^2}{\omega ^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2} + \frac{1}{5}\,m{v^2}$
$[\because \,\,For\,\,rolling\,\,without\,\,slipping\,\,v = R\omega ]$
$ = m{v^2} \times \left[ {\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{5}} \right] = \frac{7}{{10}}\,m{v^2}$

question: A uniform disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$, is resting on a table on its rim. The coeeficient of friction between disc and table is $\mu$. Now the disc is pulled with a force $F$ as shown in the figure. What is the maximum value of $F$ for which the disc rolls without slipping?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951459/preptracker/questions/q_35289_explanation_1764951459027.png Let $a$ be the acceleration of the centre of mass of disc. Then
$Ma = F - f\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
(1) If there is no slipping, angular acceleration of the disc $\alpha = \frac{a}{R}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
(2) Now torque of the disc $\tau=I \alpha=R f$
${\text{or }}\tau = \left( {\frac{1}{2}M{R^2}} \right)\alpha = Rf\quad \left[ {\because {\text{ For }}\operatorname{disc} I = \frac{1}{2}M{R^2}} \right]$
$\therefore \left( {\frac{1}{2}M{R^2}} \right)\left( {\frac{a}{R}} \right) = Rf \Rightarrow Ma = 2f$ (Using $\left( 2 \right)$)
Substituting this in eq. (1), we get
$2 f=F-f \text { or } f=\dfrac{F}{3}$
Since there is no slipping,
$\therefore f \leq \mu M g \Rightarrow F \leq 3 \mu M g ; \therefore F_{\text {max }}=3 \mu M g$

question: The ratio of the accelerations for a solid sphere (mass $m$ and radius $R$) rolling down an incline of angle $\theta$ without slipping and slipping down the incline without rolling is
explanation:
A solid sphere rolling without slipping down an inclined plane
In this case,
$\left[ {\therefore {\rm{ for solid sphere, }}{k^2} = \frac{2}{5}{R^2}} \right]$
${a_1} = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{\left[ {1 + \frac{{{K^2}}}{{{R^2}}}} \right]}} = \frac{{g\sin \theta }}{{7/5}}$
$ \Rightarrow {a_1} = \frac{5}{7}g\sin \theta $
For a sphere slipping down an inclined plane
$ \Rightarrow {a_2} = g\sin \theta $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \frac{{5/7g\sin \theta }}{{g\sin \theta }}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \frac{5}{7}$

explanation:

So, ${v^2} = \frac{{2gh}}{{1 + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^2}}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$

question: A rope is wound around a solid cylinder of mass $1\,kg$ and radius $0.4\,m$. What is the angular acceleration of cylinder, if the rope is pulled with a force of $25\,N$ ? (cylinder is rotating about its own axis)
explanation:
The torque about centre of cylinder,
$\tau = F \times r = 25 \times 0.4 = 10Nm$
If $\alpha$ and I are the angular acceleration and moment of inertia of the cyclinder respectively, then
$\tau = I\alpha = \frac{{M{r^2}}}{2}\alpha \left( {\because I = \frac{{M{r^2}}}{2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 10 = \frac{{1 \times {{(0.4)}^2}}}{2}\alpha \Rightarrow \alpha = 20/0.16$
$ = 125{\rm{rad}}/{{\rm{s}}^2}$

question: A hoop of radius $2\,m$ weights $100\,kg$. It rolls along a horizontal floor so that its centre of mass has a speed of $20\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$. How much work has to be done to stop it?
explanation:
Here, $R = 2\,m,M = 100\,kg$,
$v = 20cm{s^{ - 1}} = 20 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Total kinetic energy of the hoop $=K_{T}+K_{R}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2} \omega^{2}$
$\left[\because\right.$ For a hoop, $\left.I=M R^{2}\right]$
$ = \frac{1}{2}M{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}M{v^2}\quad [\therefore v = R\omega ]$
work $ = M{v^2}$
Work required to stop the hoop $=$ Total kinetic energy of the hoop
$ = M{v^2} = (100\,kg){\left( {20 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\,m{s^{ - 1}}} \right)^2} = 4\,J$

question: A solid sphere rolls down from top of inclined plane $7\,m$ high without slipping. Its linear speed at the foot of plane is $\left( {g = 10\,m/{s^2}} \right)$
explanation:
Given, height of inclined plane, $h=7 \mathrm{~m}$
Using conservation of energy,
potential energy lost by the sphere in rolling down the inclined plane $=$ kinetic energy gained by the sphere
$\therefore mgh = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}$
$ \Rightarrow mgh = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\frac{2}{5}m{R^2}} \right]{\left( {\frac{v}{R}} \right)^2}$
( $\because$ for rolling without slipping, $v=R \omega$)
$ \Rightarrow mgh = \frac{7}{{10}}m{v^2}$
$ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{10}}{7}gh} = \sqrt {\frac{{10}}{7} \times 10 \times 7} $
$ = \sqrt {100} \,m/s$

question: A solid cylinder of radius $R$ and mass $M$ rolls down an inclined plane of height $h$. When it reaches the bottom of the plane, its rotational kinetic energy is ( $g{\rm{ }} = $ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
For pure rolling, linear velocity $v=R \omega$,
where $\omega$ is the angular velocity.
Rotational kinetic energy,
$K_{R}=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}\right) \omega^{2}=\dfrac{1}{4} M R^{2} \omega^{2} .$
Translational kinetic energy,$K_{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} M(R \omega)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2} \omega^{2}=2 K_{R} .$
Using conservation of energy,
${K_T} + {K_R} = Mgh \Rightarrow \left( {2{K_R}} \right) + {K_R} = Mgh$
$ \Rightarrow 3{K_R} = Mgh \Rightarrow {K_R} = \frac{{Mgh}}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: A solid is in the form of cylinder of mass $50\,kg$ and radius $0.5\,m$ is free to rotate about the horizontal axis. A massless string is wound round the cylinder with one end attached to it and other hanging freely. Tension in the string required to produce an angular acceleration of $2rev/{s^2}$ is
explanation:
Given, $m = 50\,kg,r = 0.5\,m,\alpha = 2rev/{s^2}$
$\Rightarrow$ Torque produced by the tension in the string
$\tau=T \times r=T \times 0.5$
$=\dfrac{T}{2} N-m$
We know $\tau=I \alpha$
$\frac{T}{2} = I\alpha = \left( {\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2}} \right) \times (2 \times 2\pi )rad/{s^2}$
$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{{50 \times {{(0.5)}^2}}}{2} \times 4\pi $
$T = 50 \times \frac{1}{4} \times 4\pi = 50\pi = 157\,N$

question: A disc of radius $2\,m$ and mass $100\,kg$ rolls on a horizontal floor. Its centre of mass has speed of $20\,cm/s$. How much work is needed to stop it?
explanation:
Work required = change in kinetic energy
$k_{f}=0$
$k_{i}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} m v^{2}$
$ = \frac{3}{4} \times 100 \times {\left( {20 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)^2} = 3\,J$
$|\Delta K E|=3 J=W$

explanation:

$v_{\text {ring }}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{\left(1+\dfrac{K^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1+1}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{2}}$

question: Starting from rest at the same time, A coin and a ring roll down an incline without slipping. Which reaches the bottom of the incline first?
explanation:
$t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 s}{a}}$
$a=\dfrac{g \sin \theta}{\left[1+\dfrac{K^{2}}{R^{2}}\right]}$
$t = \sqrt {\frac{{2\,s}}{{\,g\sin \theta }}\left( {1 + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{R^2}}}} \right)} $
${\left( {1 + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{R^2}}}} \right)_{ring{\rm{ }}}} > {\left( {1 + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{R^2}}}} \right)_{disc{\rm{ }}}}$
$t_{\text {Coin }} < t_{\text {ring }}$
So option (2) is correct.

question: Consider two objects with $m_{1}>m_{2}$ connected by a light string that passes over a pulley having a moment of inertia of  $I$ about its axis of rotation as shown in the figure. The string does not slip on the pulley or stretch. The pulley turns without friction. The two objects are released from rest separated by a vertical distance $2\,h$. The translational speeds of the objects as they pass each other is
explanation:
As there is no slipping then mechanical energy is conserved and we can state that the increase in kinetic energy of the system equals the decrease in potential energy. Since
$K_{i}=0$ (the system is initially at rest), we have $\Delta K=K_{f}-K_{i}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} m_{2} v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
Where $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ have a common speed. But
$v=R \omega$ so that $\Delta K=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}+\dfrac{I}{R^{2}}\right) v^{2}$
We see that the system loses potential energy because of the motion of $m_{1}$. Applying the law of conservation of energy.
$\Delta K+\Delta U=0$, gives
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}+\dfrac{I}{R^{2}}\right) v^{2}+m_{2} g h-m_{1} g h=0 \\& v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2\left(m_{1}-m_{2}\right) g h}{m_{1}+m_{2}+\dfrac{I}{R^{2}}}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform rod of length $l$ and mass $M$ is suspended from two inextensible string as shown in figure. Tension in left string at the instant, when right string breaks, is
explanation:
After breaking the tension let $a$ is the acceleration of $C.M$. $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration. From force equation $m g-\mathrm{T}=m a$ and https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956874/preptracker/questions/q_34638_explanation_1764956874533.png Torque equation about $C.M$
$\dfrac{m g l}{2}=I \alpha=\dfrac{m l^{2}}{3} \times \dfrac{a}{\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)}$
If the string is tight $\left(a=\dfrac{l}{2} \alpha\right)$ $\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{3 g}{4}$
So, $T=\dfrac{m g}{4}$

question: A solid sphere of mass $1\,\,kg$, radius $10\,\,cm$ rolls down an inclined plane of height $7\,\,m$. The velocity of its centre as it reaches the ground level is
explanation:
According to the law of conservation of energy, we get
$\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)=m g h$
where $h$ is the height of the inclined plane
$\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}}}$
For a solid sphere $\dfrac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{2}{5}$
Substituting the given values, we get
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 10 \times 7}}{{\left( {1 + \frac{2}{5}} \right)}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 10 \times 7 \times 5}}{7}} $
$ = 10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

question: A coin of mass $10\,g$ rolls along a horizontal table with a velocity of $6\,\,cm/s$. Its total kinetic energy is
explanation:
Here,
Mass of the coin, $M=10 \mathrm{~g}=10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}$
Velocity, $v = 6\,cm/s = 6 \times {10^{ - 2}}m/s$
The total kinetic energy of the coin is
$=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}\right) \omega^{2} \quad\left[\because I=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}\right]$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{4} M v^{2} \quad[\because v=R \omega]$
$=\dfrac{3}{4} M v^{2}=27 \mu J$.

question: Three bodies, a ring, a solid cylinder and a solid sphere roll down the same inclined plane without slipping. They start from rest. The radii of the bodies are identical. Which of the bodies reaches the ground with maximum velocity?
explanation:
According to conservation of mechanical energy
$m g h=K \cdot E_{t r a}+K \cdot E_{r o t}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)$
$v^{2}=\left(\dfrac{2 g h}{1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}}\right)$
It is independent of the mass of the rolling body.
For a ring, $k^{2}=R^{2}$
$v_{\text {ring }}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1+1}}=\sqrt{g h}$
For a solid cylinder, $k^{2}=\dfrac{R^{2}}{2}$
$v_{c y l i n d e r}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1+\dfrac{1}{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{4 g h}{3}}$
For a solid sphere, $k^{2}=\dfrac{2}{5} R^{2}$
$v_{\text {sphere }}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1+\dfrac{2}{5}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{10 g h}{7}}$
Among the given three bodies the solid sphere has the greatest velocity and the ring has the least velocity at the bottom of the inclined plane.

options.text: All reach the ground with same velocity

question: A disc is pulled by a force $F$ acting at a point above the centre of mass of the disc. The direction of frictional force $(f)$ acting on disc pushed on a rough surface will be represented by
explanation:
Let us assume friction is in backward direction and $a$ is the acceleration of the disc.
Then $F-f=m a$
The torque about centre is
$(F+f) R=\dfrac{m R^{2}}{2} \alpha$
As the body is in pure rolling
$a=R \alpha$
on solving the above eq's we get
$f=\dfrac{-m a}{4}$
As $f$ is (-)ve so our assumed direction for friction is opposite to the original direction.
That means ' $f$ ' should act in forward direction.

From eq’s (1) & (2)
$\Rightarrow F=\dfrac{7}{10} m a$

question: A ladder is leaned against a smooth wall and it is allowed to slip on a frictionless floor. The path followed by a general point $\mathrm{P}$ is
explanation:
Let 1 be the length of the rod and $\theta$ be the angle made by the rod with respect to the horizontal. The coordinates of the point $\mathrm{P}$ are. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951542/preptracker/questions/q_35514_explanation_1764951542391.png $x = l\cos \theta - b\cos \theta = (l - b)\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$y = b\sin \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From equations $1 \& 2$
$\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=\dfrac{x^{2}}{(l-b)^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$
The value of $b$ depends on point $P$. The path followed by the point $\mathrm{P}$ is ellipse.
subject: Physics

subtopic: Rigid Body Constraints

question: A cylinder is rolling without sliding over two horizontal planks (surfaces) 1 and 2. If the velocities of the surfaces 1 and 2 are $-v \hat{i}$ and $2 v \hat{i}$ respectively, find the instantaneous centre of the cyclinder?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951540/preptracker/questions/q_35510_explanation_1764951540429.png Since, there is no relative sliding between the cylinder and the planks 1 and 2, the points A and $\mathrm{B}$ of the disc will move with velocities equal to the velocities of the plank's.
$\vec{v}_{A}=-v \hat{i} \text { and } \vec{v}_{B}=+2 v \hat{i}$
Joining $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ and $A^{\prime}$ and $B^{\prime}$ we find the point $\mathrm{P}$ as IC. Then we have the similar triangles
$P A A^{\prime}$ and $P B B^{\prime}$. Using the properties of similar triangles we have
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{2 v}{B P}=\dfrac{v}{A P} \Rightarrow A P=\dfrac{B P}{2} \\& A P+B P=2 R \Rightarrow B P=\dfrac{4 R}{3}\end{aligned}$
Hence, IC is located at a distance of $4 \mathrm{R} / 3$ below the top of the disc.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Rigid Body Constraints

question: A bobbin is pushed along on a rough stationary horizontal surface as shown in the figure. The board is kept horizontal and there is no slipping at any contact points. The distance moved by the axis of the bobbin is $l$. The distance travelled by the board $(\mathrm{B})$ is
explanation:
For no slipping, ${v_{cm}} = \omega R\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The velocity of point $B$ is
${v_B} = \omega r + {v_{cm}} = {v_{cm}}\left( {1 + \frac{r}{R}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
$\frac{{{\ell _B}}}{\ell } = \frac{{{v_B}}}{{{v_{cm}}}} = \left( {1 + \frac{r}{R}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {\ell _B} = \ell \left( {1 + \frac{r}{R}} \right)$
Where ${l_B}\,\,\& \,\,l$ are the distance travelled by B and C.M.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Rigid Body Constraints

question: Two identical uniform discs of mass $m$ and radius $\mathrm{r}$ are arranged as shown in the figure. If $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration of the lower disc and $a_{c m}$ is acceleration of centre of mass of the lower disc, then relation among $a_{c m}, \alpha$ and $\mathrm{r}$ is:
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{T}$ be the tension in the string. Torque on the upper disc is
${\mathop{\rm Tr}\nolimits} = \frac{{m{r^2}}}{2}{\alpha _1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Torque on the lower disc is
$Tr = \frac{{m{r^2}}}{2}\alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
${\alpha _1} = \alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From (1) and (2) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956888/preptracker/questions/q_35406_explanation_1764956887761.png Acceleration of point $B=$ acceleration of point A
$r{\alpha _1} = {a_{cm}} - r\alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
$ \Rightarrow {a_{cm}} = 2r\alpha $
subject: Physics

question: A system of identical cylinders and plates is as shown in the figure. All the cylinders are identical and there is no slipping at any contact. The velocity of lower and upper plates are $v$ and $2 v$, respectively, as shown in the figure. Then the ratio of angular speeds of the upper cylinders to lower cylinders is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951467/preptracker/questions/q_35403_explanation_1764951467505.png Angular velocity of the upper cylinder is
$\begin{aligned}& \omega_{u p}=\dfrac{2 v-(-v)}{2 R}=\dfrac{3 v}{2 R} ; \text { for the lower cylinder } \\& \omega_{\text {lower }}=\dfrac{v-0}{2 R}=\dfrac{v}{2 R}\end{aligned}$
Hence, ratio $\dfrac{\omega_{u p}}{\omega_{\text {lower }}}=3$

question:
A sphere is rolling without slipping on a fixed horizonal plane surface. In the figure, $\mathrm{A}$ is the point of contanct, $\mathrm{B}$ is the center of the sphere and $\mathrm{C}$ is its topmost point. Then:
(i) ${\overrightarrow V _C} - {\overrightarrow V _A} = 2\left( {{{\overrightarrow V }_B} - {{\overrightarrow V }_C}} \right)$
(ii) ${\overrightarrow V _C} - {\overrightarrow V _B} = {\overrightarrow V _B} - {\overrightarrow V _A}$
(iii) $\left| {{{\overrightarrow V }_C} - {{\overrightarrow V }_A}} \right| = 2\left| {{{\overrightarrow V }_B} - {{\overrightarrow V }_C}} \right|$
(iv) $\left| {{{\overrightarrow V }_C} - {{\overrightarrow V }_A}} \right| = 4\left| {{{\overrightarrow V }_B}} \right|$
explanation:
We know
$\vec{V}_{C}=2 v \hat{i} ; \vec{V}_{B}=v \hat{i}, \vec{V}_{A}=0$
(ii) $\vec{V}_{C}-\vec{V}_{B}=2 v \hat{i}-v \hat{i} ; \vec{V}_{B}-\vec{V}_{A}=v \hat{i}-0=v \hat{i}$, correct
(iii) $\left|\vec{V}_{C}-\vec{V}_{A}\right|=2 v, 2\left|\vec{V}_{B}-\vec{V}_{C}\right|=2 v$, correct option is (1).

explanation: In rolling all points of rigid body have the same angular speed but different linear speed. Rotational motion may cause friction which may effect linear motion.

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question: A large spool of rope stands on the ground with the end of the rope lying on the top edge of the spool. A person grabs the end of the rope and walks a distance $l$, holding onto it. The spool rolls behind the person without slipping. What is the length of rope that unwinds from the spool? How far does the spool's $CM$ move?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951361/preptracker/questions/q_34824_explanation_1764951361115.png If the spool rolls without slipping then
$v_{P}=2 v_{C M} \Rightarrow \Delta x_{\mathrm{P}}=2 \Delta x_{\mathrm{CM}}$
Given that $\Delta x_{P}=l \Rightarrow \Delta x_{C M}=\dfrac{l}{2}$.
If the point $P$ (person) moves a distance $l$ then $CM$ travels by $\dfrac{l}{2}$. So the additional length $\dfrac{l}{2}$ has to come out from the spool.

question: The velocities are in ground frame and the cylinder is performing pure rolling on the plank, velocity of point ' $A$ ' would be
explanation:
For pure rolling velocity of the point of contact has to be equal to the velocity of the surface.
Let us say cylinder rolls with angular velocity $\omega$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955802/preptracker/questions/q_34820_explanation_1764955801513.png At point $B$,
$v_{C}-\omega r=v_{P} \Rightarrow \omega r=v_{C}-v_{P}$
At point $A$,
$v_{A}=v_{C}+\omega r=2 v_{C}-v_{P}$

question: End of a rod $A B$ is being pulled on the floor with a constant velocity $v_{0}$ as shown in the figure. Taking the length of the rod as $l$, at an instant when the rod makes an angle $37^{\circ}$ with the horizontal, calculate the velocity of end $B$
explanation:
From the given figure
Let $OA$=x, $OB$=y
${x^2} + {y^2} = {l^2}\,\,and\,\,x = l\cos \theta $
Differentiate ${x^2} + {y^2} = {l^2}$ with respect to time.
$2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0$
${v_B} = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = - \frac{x}{y}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}$
$ = - {v_A}\cot \theta = \frac{4}{3}{v_0} \downarrow $

question: A hoop rolls on a horizontal ground without slipping with linear speed $v$. Speed of a particle $P$ on the circumference of the hoop at angle $\theta$ is
explanation:
$v_{p}=\sqrt{v_{t}^{2}+v_{C M}^{2}+2 v_{t} v_{C M} \cos (80-\theta)}$
As the body is in pure rolling $v_{t}=v_{C M}=v$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951349/preptracker/questions/q_34808_explanation_1764951349425.png $v_{p}=\sqrt{v^{2}+v^{2}+2 v v \cos (180-\theta)}$
$\Rightarrow v_{p}=\sqrt{2 v^{2}[1-\cos \theta]}=2 v \sin (\theta / 2)$

question: A ladder is leaned against a smooth wall and it is allowed to slip on a frictionless floor. The path followed by the $CM$ is
explanation:

${v_C} = \omega R = \frac{{{v_P}R}}{{(R + r)}} = \frac{{6 \times 20}}{{(20 + 10)}} = 4\,m/s$

question:
A rigid body is rolling without sliping on the horizontal surface, then match the Column I with Column II and select the correct answer from the codes given below.
A. Velocity at point A
B. Velocity at point B
C. Velocity at point C
D. Velocity at point D
P. $\sqrt 2 v$
Q. $0$
R. $v\sqrt {2 - \sqrt 2 } $
S. $2v$
explanation:
If $v$ is the velocity $CM$ of the body of radius $r$, then
Velocity at point $A,$ ${v_A} = 0$
Velocity at point $B,{v_B} = v\sqrt 2 $
Velocity at point $C,{v_c} = v + r\omega = 2v$
Velocity of point $D$, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951196/preptracker/questions/q_34035_explanation_1764951196486.png $\begin{aligned}& v_{0}=\sqrt{v^{2}+v^{2}+2 v^{2} \cos 135^{\circ}} \\& =\sqrt{2 v^{2}-\sqrt{2} v^{2}}=v \sqrt{2-\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$
Hence, $A \rightarrow Q, B \rightarrow P, C \rightarrow S$ and $D \rightarrow R$

question: A pulley of radius $R$ is unwinding with the downward linear acceleration $a$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$. If the string is unstretchable and there is no slipping between the string and the pulley, then the relation between $a$ and $\alpha$ is
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& a_{s}=a_{P_{1}} \\& \left(a_{t}-a_{C M}\right)=a_{P_{1}}=a_{P_{2}}=0 \\& a_{t}=a_{C M} \Rightarrow R \alpha=a\end{aligned}$

question: A uniform rod $A B$ of mass 2 $kg$ is hinged at one end $A$. The rod is kept in the horizontal position by a massless string tied to point $B$. Find the reaction of the hinge  on end$A$ of the rod at the instant when string is cut. $\left(g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right)$
explanation:
When the string is cut, the weight of the rod constitutes torque about the hinge, so
${\tau _A} = mg\frac{l}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
According to Newton's second law,
${\tau _A} = I\alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
where $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration of the rod about the end $A$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$I \alpha=m g \dfrac{l}{2}$
or $\alpha=\dfrac{m g \dfrac{l}{2}}{I}$
Here $I=m l^{2} / 3$, therefore
$\therefore \quad \alpha=\dfrac{m g l / 2}{m l^{2} / 3}=\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{g}{l}$
Acceleration of the CM of the rod is$a_{{CM}}=\alpha r=\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{g}{l} \times \dfrac{l}{2}=\dfrac{3 g}{4}$
Again by Newton's second law,
$\begin{array}{ll} & m g-R_{A}=m a_{{CM}} \\\text { or } & m g-R_{A}=m \times \dfrac{3 g}{4} \\\therefore & R_{A}=\dfrac{M g}{4}=\dfrac{2 \times 10}{4}=5 {~N}\end{array}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Hinge Force on a Rotating Body about a Fixed Axis

question: A wheel has moment of inertia ${5 \times 10^{-3} {~kg} {~m}^{2}}$ and is making ${20 {rev} / {s}}$. The torque needed to stop it in 10 $s$ is ____ $Nm$ .
explanation:
Here, ${I=5 \times 10^{-3} {~kg} {~m}^{2}, v=20 {rev} / {s}}$
$t = 10\,s$
Torque ,$\tau = I\alpha $ ($\alpha = $ angular acceleration)
$\tau = \frac{{I\omega }}{t}\,\,\,$ $\left( {\because {\omega _f} = 0\,\,\,\therefore \alpha = \frac{\omega }{t}} \right)$
$\alpha = \frac{{I(2\pi v)}}{t} = \frac{{2\pi \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 20}}{{10}}Nm$
$\tau = 2\pi \times {10^{ - 2}}\,N m$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion of Rigid Body about a Fixed Axis

question: A turbine fan in a jet engine has moment of inertia ${2.5 {~kg} {~m}^{2}}$ about its axis of rotation. Its angular velocity is $40\,{t^2}$. The net torque is
explanation:
Given, moment of inertia ${(I)=2.5 {~kg} {~m}^{2}}$
angular velocity ${(\omega)=40 t^{2}}$
Torque is given by,
$\tau = \frac{{dL}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d(I\omega )}}{{dt}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\tau = \frac{{Id\omega }}{{dt}} = 2.5\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {40{t^2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\tau = 2.5(80t) = 200t$.
So correct option is (3)

question: If object starts from rest and covers angle of  $60\,rad$  in  $10\,s$  in circular motion, then magnitude of angular acceleration will be
explanation:
Given, initial angular velocity of object,
$\omega_{0}=0$
Angular displacement, $\theta = 60\,rad$
and $\quad \Delta t=10 \mathrm{~s}$
From equation of rotational motion,
$\theta = {\omega _0}t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha {t^2}$
$60 = 0 \times t + \frac{1}{2} \times \alpha \times {10^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{{60}}{{50}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 1.2\,rad{s^{ - 2}}$

question: A thin rod of mass $m$ and length $2 l$ is made to rotate about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to it. If its angular velocity changes from 0 to $\omega$ in time $t$, the torque acting on it is
explanation:
Since, $\tau=I a$
So, $\tau = \left( {\frac{{m{{(2\,l)}^2}}}{{12}}} \right)\left( {\frac{\omega }{t}} \right)\left( {\because I = \frac{{m{l^{\,2}}}}{{12}}} \right)$
or $\tau=\dfrac{m \times 4 l^{2} \times \omega}{12 \times t}$
or $\tau=\dfrac{4 m l^{2} \omega}{12 t}=\dfrac{m l^{2} \omega}{3 t}$

question: A wheel having moment of inertia $2 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^{2}$ about its vertical axis, rotates at the rate of $60\,\,rpm$ about this axis. The torque which can stop the wheel's rotation in one minute would be
explanation:
Given, $I = 2\,kg - {m^2},$
$\omega_{0}=\dfrac{60}{60} \times 2 \pi r a d s^{-1}, \omega=0$ and $t = 60\,s$
The torque required to stop the wheel's rotation is
$ = I\alpha = I\left( {\frac{{{\omega _0} - \omega }}{t}} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\tau = \frac{{2 \times 2\pi \times 60}}{{60 \times 60}} = \frac{\pi }{{15}}N{m^{ - 1}}$

question: A disc of mass ${M}$ and radius ${R}$ can rotate freely about a horizontal shaft ${S}$ at distant ${r}$ from the centre of mass ${(O)}$ of the disc. The angular acceleration after release will be
explanation:
M.I. of disc about the shaft, ${I=I_{C}+M d^{2}}$
${=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}+M r^{2}}$
Torque acting on the disc is ${\tau={Mgr}}$
also ${\tau=I \alpha}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951686/preptracker/questions/q_36474_explanation_1764951686113.png ${\therefore \alpha=\dfrac{M g r}{I}}$
${=\dfrac{M g r}{M\left(\dfrac{R^{2}}{2}+r^{2}\right)}=\dfrac{g r}{\dfrac{R^{2}}{2}+r^{2}}}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Dynamics of Rotational Motion of Rigid Body about a Fixed Axis

question:
A rigid body is rotating such that angular displacement at any instant ${t}$ is given by ${\theta=t^{2}+2 t+3}$
A. Angular velocity at $t = 1\,units$
B. Angular acceleration at $t = 2\,units$
C. Angular velocity between $t = 0$ and $t = 2\,units$
D. Angular acceleration between $t = 0$ and $t = 4\,units$
P. $3\,units$
Q. $0\,units$
R. $2\,units$
S. $4\,units$
explanation:
Given that,
${\theta=t^{2}+2 t+3}$
${\omega=\dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=2 t+2}$
${\alpha=\dfrac{d \omega}{d t}=2}$
${\omega_{(t=1)}=2(1)+2=4 \quad(A-S)}$
${\alpha=2}$ (constant) ${(B-R)}$
${\omega_{\text {avg }}=\dfrac{\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}}{\Delta t}=\dfrac{\left((2)^{2}+2(2)+3\right)-(3)}{2}}$
${=4 {rad} / {s} \quad(C-S)}$
${\alpha_{\text {avg }}=\alpha=2 \quad(D-R)}$
Correct Option is (1).

options.text: A - S, B - R, C - S, D - R
A - Q, B - R, C - S, D - P
A - R, B - P, C - R, D - Q
A - S, B - Q, C - P, D - P

subject: Physics

subtopic: Kinematics of Rotational Motion of Rigid Body about a Fixed Axis

question: The diameter of a disc is $0.5\,m$ and its mass is $16\,kg$. What torque will increase its angular velocity from zero to 120 rotations/minute in 8 seconds?
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the disc
${I=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 16 \times\left[\dfrac{0.5}{2}\right]^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} {~kg}-{m}^{2}}$
Angular velocity ${\omega=\dfrac{120 \times 2 \pi}{60}=4 \pi {rad} / {s}}$ and change in angular momentum
${\Delta L=I \omega-0=I \omega=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \pi=2 \pi}$
${\therefore}$ Angular impulse is ${\tau \times t=\Delta L \Rightarrow \tau=\dfrac{\Delta L}{t}}$
${=\dfrac{1}{8} \times 2 \pi=\dfrac{\pi}{4} N-m}$
subject: Physics

question:

Reason: Angular velocity is inversely proportional to the total time.
explanation:

$\Rightarrow$ Assertion is true
But $\omega$ is not inversely proportional to the total time. It is a constant value regardless of the total time. So correct option is (3).

question:
A metre stick is held vertically with one end on the floor and is then allowed to fall. If the end touching the floor is not allowed to slip, the other end will hit the ground with a velocity
$\left( {g = 9.8\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}} \right)$
explanation:
The decrease in potential energy is equal to increase in rotational kinetic energy of the rod
$i.e.,{\rm{ }}\Delta U = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}$
$m g\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{m L^{2}}{3} \omega^{2}$$\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 g}{L}}$
Linear velocity of the end point is
$v = r\omega = L\omega = L\left( {\sqrt {\frac{{3g}}{L}} } \right)g$
$ = \sqrt {3gL} = \sqrt {30} = 5.4\,m{\rm{/}}s.$

question: A particle moves along a circle of radius $\dfrac{40}{\pi} m$ with constant tangential acceleration. If the velocity of the particle is $100\,m{\rm{/}}s$ at the end of the 4th revolution after motion has begin, the tangential acceleration is
explanation:
As we know,
$\omega=\dfrac{v}{r}=\dfrac{100}{40} \times \pi=\dfrac{5 \pi}{2}$
$\omega=2 \pi n=2 \pi \times 4=8 \pi \quad \because \alpha=\dfrac{\omega^{2}}{2 \theta}$
$\alpha=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{5}{2} \pi\right)}{2 \times 8 \pi} \Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{25}{64} \pi$
$a_{r}=\alpha r$
$=\dfrac{25}{64} \pi \times \dfrac{40}{\pi} \Rightarrow a_{r}=\dfrac{125}{8}$

question: A fly wheel is accelerated uniformly from rest and rotates through $5 \mathrm{rad}$ in the first second. The angle rotated by the fly wheel in the next second, will be:
explanation:
$\theta^{\prime}=\theta_{0}+\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}$
Where $\alpha$ is angular acceleration
$5=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha(1)^{2}$
$ = 10\,rad/{s^2}$
$\theta=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha(2)^{2}$ [since $\alpha$ is constant throughout the motion]
$ \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{1}{2} \times (10)(4) = 20\,rad$
$(\Delta \theta)=$ angle rotated (from $t = 1\,\,to\,\,t = 3\sec $)
$ = (20 - 5) = 15rad.$

question: A thin uniform rod, pivoted at $\mathrm{O}$, is rotating in the horizontal plane with constant angular speed $\omega$, as shown in the figure. At time, $t=0$, a small insect starts from $\mathrm{O}$ and moves with constant speed $\mathrm{v}$ with respect to the rod towards the other end. It reached the end of the rod at $t=T$ and stops. The angular speed of the system remains $\omega$ throughout. The magnitude of the torque $(|\vec{\tau}|)$ on the system about O, as a function of the time is best represented by which plot
explanation:

question: A rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ is hinged at one of its end $A$ as shown in figure. A force $F$ is applied at a distance $\mathrm{x}$ from $\mathrm{A}$. The acceleration of centre of mass varies with $\mathrm{x}$ as:
explanation:
The rod will rotate about point A with angular acceleration :
$\begin{aligned}& \alpha=\dfrac{\tau}{I}=\dfrac{F \cdot x}{\dfrac{m l^{2}}{3}}=\dfrac{3 F x}{m l^{2}} \\& \therefore a=\dfrac{l}{2} \alpha=\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{F x}{m l}\end{aligned}$
or $a \propto x$
i.e., $a-x$ graph is a straight line passing through origin.

question: A uniform rod of mass $m$ length $l$ is pivoted at point $\mathrm{A}$, which is at a distance $\dfrac{l}{4}$ from one end as shown in the figure. The rod is free to rotate in a vertical plane. The rod is released from the horizontal position. What is the reaction at the hinge, when kinetic energy of the rod is maximum?
explanation:
$\mathrm{KE}$ of the rod is maximum when it is in the vertical position.
From conservation of energy, we get https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951522/preptracker/questions/q_35526_explanation_1764951521513.png $\dfrac{m g l}{4}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{m l^{2}}{12}+\dfrac{m l^{2}}{16}\right] \omega^{2}$
$\omega^{2}=\dfrac{24 g}{7 l}$
and $R_{y}-m g=m(l / 4) \omega^{2}$
$R_{y}=m g+m\left(\dfrac{6 g}{7}\right)=\dfrac{13}{7} m g$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ is hinged at its upper end. It is released from a horizontal position. When it becomes vertical, what force does it exert on the hinge?
explanation:
The forces acting on the rod are
$N-m g=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$
From conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=m g \dfrac{l}{2} \\& \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{m l^{2}}{3} \omega^{2}=m g \dfrac{l}{2} \\& \dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=\dfrac{3 m g}{2}\end{aligned}$
$N=m g+\dfrac{3}{2} m g=\dfrac{5}{2} m g$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955904/preptracker/questions/q_35522_explanation_1764955904095.png

question: A slender rod of mass $m$ and length $L$ is pivoted about $\mathrm{P}$ at one end and released from rest at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ above the horizontal. The force exerted by the pivot on the rod at the instant when the rod passes through a horizontal position is
explanation:
The angular velocity of the rod about the pivot when it passes through the horizontal position is given by
$mg \times \frac{L}{2}\sin {\rm{ }}30^\circ = \frac{{m{L^2}}}{3} \times \frac{{{\omega ^2}}}{2}$
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{3g}}{{2L}}} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955886/preptracker/questions/q_35419_explanation_1764955885221.png Radial acceleration of the centre of mass (as centre of mass is moving in a circle of radius L/ 2) is given by
$a_{r}=\omega^{2} \dfrac{L}{2}=\dfrac{3 g}{4}$
Torque about $(\mathrm{P})$, in the horizontal position, is
$\tau = mg\frac{L}{2} = I\alpha $
$\alpha = \frac{{mgL/2}}{{m{L^2}/3}} = \frac{{3g}}{{2L}}$
Tangential acceleration of the centre of mass,
$a_{t}=\dfrac{L}{2} \alpha=\dfrac{3 g}{4}$.
Let ${R_1}\,\,\& \,\,{R_2}$ are the forces exerted by $(\mathrm{P})$.
Use Newton's second law of equation. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955887/preptracker/questions/q_35419_explanation_1764955887066.png $\begin{aligned}& R_{1}=m a_{r}=\dfrac{3 m g}{4} \\& m g-R_{2}=m \times a_{t}=\dfrac{3 m g}{4} \\& R_{2}=\dfrac{m g}{4}\end{aligned}$
So, reaction force by the pivot on the rod, $R=\sqrt{R_{1}^{2}+R_{2}^{2}}=\sqrt{10} \mathrm{mg} / 4$ at angle of $\tan ^{-1}\left(R_{2} / R_{1}\right)\left[=\tan ^{-1}(1 / 3)\right]$ with the horizontal.

options.text: $\sqrt{\dfrac{10}{4}} m g$ along a line making an angle of $\tan ^{-1}(3)$ with the horizontal

$\dfrac{\sqrt{10}}{4} m g$ along a line making an angle of
$\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right) \text { with the horizontal }$

question: A light rigid rod of length $4 \mathrm{~m}$ is connected rigidly with two identical particles each of mass $\mathrm{m}=$ $2 \mathrm{~kg}$. The free end of the rod is smoothly pivoted at $\mathrm{O}$. The rod is released from rest from its horizontal position at $t=0$. Find the reaction offered by the pivot at $t=0$ (in $N$ )
explanation:
Torque equation: The internal forces are the reaction forces at $\mathrm{O}$. Then the net torque about $\mathrm{O}$ is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956893/preptracker/questions/q_35415_explanation_1764956893130.png ${\tau _0} = mgl + mg\left( {2l} \right) = 3mgl$
Which produces an angular acceleration $\alpha$.
Using Newton's 2nd law of rotation $\tau=I \alpha$
We have $3 m g l=I_{0} \alpha$ where
$I_{0}=m l^{2}+m(2 l)^{2}=5 m l^{2}$
This gives $\alpha=\dfrac{3 g}{5 l}=\dfrac{3 \times 10}{5 \times 2}=3 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$
Force equation: Referring FBD, we have
$\left( {mg + mg} \right) - R = m{a_1} + m{a_2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956894/preptracker/questions/q_35415_explanation_1764956893935.png Substituting $a_{1}=l \alpha$ and $a_{2}=2 l \alpha$, we have
$\begin{aligned}& R=m(2 g-3 l \alpha) \\& =2(2 \times 10-3 \times 2 \times 3)=2(20-18)=4 N .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Hinge Force on a Rotating Body about a Fixed Axis

question: A disc has mass ' $M$ ' and radius ' $R$ '. How much tangential force should be applied to the rim of the disc, so as to rotate with angular velocity ' $\omega$ ' in time ' $\mathrm{t}$ '?
explanation:
If $\alpha$ and I are the angular acceleration and moment of inertia of the disk respectively, then torque applied to the disk,
$\tau = I\alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
But $\tau=F \times R, I=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2}$ and $\alpha=\dfrac{\omega}{t}
$Therefore, $ F \times R=\dfrac{M R^{2}}{2} \times \dfrac{\omega}{t}$
$\Rightarrow F=\dfrac{M R \omega}{2 t}$

question: The instantaneous angular position of a point on a rotating wheel is given by the equation $\theta(t)=2 t^{3}-6 t^{2}$. The torque on the wheel becomes zero at
explanation:
We know that
$\alpha=\dfrac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}}$
Given, $\theta(t)=2 t^{3}-6 t^{2}$
So, $\dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=6 t^{2}-12 t$
$\alpha=\dfrac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}}=12 t-12$
When torque is zero
$\alpha=0$$\Rightarrow 12 t-12=0 \Rightarrow t=1 s$

question: A rotating wheel changes angular speed from $1800\,rpm\,\,to\,\,3000\,rpm$ in $20\,s$. What is the angular acceleration assuming to be uniform?
explanation:
Here,
Initial angular speed of the wheel,
${\omega _0} = 1800 \times \frac{{2\pi }}{{60}}rad\,{s^{ - 1}} = 60\pi \,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
Final angular speed of the wheel,
$\omega=3000 \times \dfrac{2 \pi}{60} \operatorname{rad~s}^{-1}=100 \pi \operatorname{rad~s}^{-1}$
Time during which this change of speed takes place, $t = 20\,s$
Let $\alpha$ be angular acceleration of the wheel.
As
$\omega = {\omega _0} + \alpha t$
$\therefore \alpha = \frac{{\omega - {\omega _0}}}{t} = \frac{{100\pi - 60\pi }}{{20}}rad{s^{ - 2}}$
$ = 2\pi {\mkern 1mu} \,rad{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A ceiling fan rotates about its own axis with some angular velocity. When the fan is switched off, the angular velocity becomes $\left( {\frac{1}{4}} \right)th$ of the original in time ' $t$ ' and ' $n$ ' revolutions are made in that time. The number of revolutions made by the fan during the time interval between switch off and rest are (Angular reterdation is uniform)
explanation:
Let initial angular velocity of the fan be $\omega_{0}$
Given $\omega=\dfrac{\omega_{0}}{4} ; \theta=2 \pi n$
Using the relation ${\omega ^2} = \omega _0^2 - 2\alpha \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
we get retardation $\alpha$ as
$\alpha = \frac{{\omega _0^2 - {\omega ^2}}}{{2\theta }} = \frac{{\omega _0^2 - {{\left( {\frac{{{\omega _0}}}{4}} \right)}^2}}}{{(2 \times 2\pi {\rm{n}})}} = \frac{{15\omega _0^2}}{{64\pi {\rm{n}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
After switching off, if the fan comes to rest after $n^{\prime}$ revolutions,
$\omega = 0,{\theta ^\prime } = 2\pi {n^\prime }$
${\rm{Using (1), }}0 = \omega _0^2 - 2\alpha \left( {2\pi {{\rm{n}}^\prime }} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {n^\prime } = \frac{{\omega _0^2}}{{4\pi \alpha }} = \frac{{\omega _0^2}}{{\left( {4\pi \times \frac{{15\omega _0^2}}{{64\pi n}}} \right)}} = \frac{{16}}{{15}}n$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Kinematics of Rotational Motion of Rigid Body about a Fixed Axis

question: A rod $P Q$ of mass $M$ and length $L$ is hinged at end $P$. The rod is kept horizontal by a massless string tied to a point $Q$ as shown in figure. When string is cut, the initial angular acceleration of the rod is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955848/preptracker/questions/q_35147_explanation_1764955848503.png ${\rm{Torque}}\,\,{\rm{on}}\,\,{\rm{therod}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \tau = Mg\frac{L}{2}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \tau = I\alpha $
$I = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{3}$
$Mg\frac{L}{2} = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{3}\alpha \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{3g}}{{2L}}$

question: A solid cylinder of mass $2\,kg$ and radius $4\,cm$ is rotating about its axis at the rate of $3\,rpm$. The torque required to stop after $2 \pi$ revolutions is
explanation:
From work energy theorem.
$W=\dfrac{1}{2} I\left(\omega_{f}^{2}-\omega_{i}^{2}\right)$
$\theta = 2\pi \times 2\pi = 4{\pi ^2}rad$
${\omega _i} = 3 \times \frac{{2\pi }}{{60}}rad/s$
$\Rightarrow \quad-\tau \theta=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}\left(0^{2}-\omega_{i}^{2}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \tau = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {{\left( {4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)}^2}{{\left( {3 \times \frac{{2\pi }}{{60}}} \right)}^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\tau = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}Nm$

question: A body is rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}=(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$. The linear velocity of a point having position vector $\vec{r}=(5 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})$ is
explanation:
Here, $\vec{\omega}=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$
$\vec{r}=5 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}$
As $\vec{v}=\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -4 & 1 \\ 5 & -6 & 6\end{array}\right|$
$=\hat{i}(-24-(-6))-\hat{j}(18-5)+\hat{k}(-18-(-20))$
$=-18 \hat{i}-13 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$

question:
Two points $A$ and $B$ on a disc have velocities $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ respectively, at some moment. Their directions make angles $60^{\circ}$ and $30^{\circ}$, respectively, with the line of separation as shown in the figure. The angular velocity of disc is $(A$and $B$ are seperated by $'d'$)
$30 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$
explanation:
For rigid body, separation between two points remain same
$v_{1} \cos 60^{\circ}=v_{2} \cos 30^{\circ}$
$\dfrac{v_{1}}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} v_{2}}{2} \Rightarrow v_{1}=\sqrt{3} v_{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956881/preptracker/questions/q_34800_explanation_1764956880995.png ${\omega _{disc}}\left| {\frac{{{v_2}\sin \,30^\circ - {v_1} - \sin \,60^\circ }}{d}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{\frac{{{v_2}}}{2} - \frac{{\sqrt 3 {v_1}}}{2}}}{d}} \right|$
${\omega _{disc}}\left| {\frac{{{v_2} - \sqrt 3 \times \sqrt 3 {v_2}}}{{2d}}} \right| = \frac{{2{v_1}}}{{2d}} = \frac{{{v_2}}}{d}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is mounted on an axle supported in frictionless bearings. A light cord is wrapped around the rim of the disc and a steady downward pull $T$ is exerted on the cord. The angular acceleration of the disc is
explanation:
Torque exerted on the disc $\tau=T R$
Now $\tau=I \alpha$
$\alpha=\dfrac{\tau}{I}=\dfrac{T R}{\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}}=\dfrac{2 T R}{M R^{2}}=\dfrac{2 T}{M R}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951347/preptracker/questions/q_34796_explanation_1764951347444.png

question: In the figure (i) half of the meter scale is made of wood while the other half, of steel. The wooden part is pivoted at $O$. A force $F$ is applied at the end of steel part. In figure (ii) the steel part is pivoted at $O$ and the same force is applied at the wooden end
explanation:
As $\tau=I \alpha$
$\alpha \propto \dfrac{1}{I} \quad(\tau$ is constant $)$
Moment of inertia of figure (ii) is smaller hence acceleration is greater.
So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Same acceleration will be produced in both conditions

More acceleration will be produced in (i)

question: Two particles $A$ and $B$ are situated at a distance $d = 2\,m$ apart. Particle $A$ has a velocity of $10\,m/s$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ and particle $B$ has velocity $v$ at an angle $30^{\circ}$ as shown in the figure. The distance $\mathrm{d}$ between $A$ and $B$ is constant. The angular velocity of $B$ with respect to $A$ is:
explanation:
If distance between them remains constant, then
$v\cos 30^\circ = u\cos 60^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow v\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = 10\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow v = \frac{{10}}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
$\omega=\left(v \sin 30^{\circ}-u \sin 60^{\circ}\right) / d$
$=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \times \dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{10 \times \sqrt{3}}{2}}{2}=-\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} .$
subject: Physics

question: A slender uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $\ell$ is pivoted at one end so that it can rotate in a vertical plane (see figure). There is negligible friction at the pivot. The free end is held vertically above the pivot and then released. The angular acceleration of the rod when it makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical is:
explanation:
The gravitational force on the rod is shown in the figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951284/preptracker/questions/q_34615_explanation_1764951284269.png Taking torque about pivot $\tau=\mathrm{I} \alpha$
${\mathop{\rm mg}\nolimits} \sin \theta \frac{l}{2} = \frac{{{\rm{m}}{l^2}}}{3}\alpha $
$\alpha=\dfrac{3 \mathrm{~g}}{2 \ell} \sin \theta$
subject: Physics

question: A bullet of mass $10\,g$ and speed $500\,m/s$ is fired into a door and gets embedded exactly at the centre of the door. The door is $1.0\,m$ wide and weight $12\,kg$. It is hinged at one end and rotates about a vertical axis practically without friction. The angular speed of the door just after the bullet embeds into it is
explanation:
Given, mass of bullet$({\rm{m}}) = 10 g = 0.01 kg$ Speed of bullet $(v) = 500\,m/s$ Width of the door $(l) = 1.0 m$
Mass of the door $(M) = 12 Kg$
As bullet gets embedded exactly at the centre of the door, therefore its distance from the hinged end of the door is $=\dfrac{1}{2} m$
Angular momentum transferred by the bullet to the door,
$(L)=m v \times r=0.01 \times 500 \times \dfrac{1}{2}=2.5 \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s}$
Moment of inertia of the door about the vertical axis at one of its end,
$(I)=\dfrac{M l^{2}}{3}=\dfrac{12 \times(1)^{2}}{3}=4 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^{2}$
Angular momentum, $(L)=I \omega$
$\therefore 2.5=4 \times \omega$
$\omega = 0.625\,\,rad/s$
subject: Physics

question: A wheel of moment of inertia $2.0 \times {10^3}kg{m^2}$ is rotating at uniform angular speed of $4\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$. What is the torque required to stop it in one second.
explanation:
$\alpha = \frac{\omega }{t} = \frac{4}{1} = 4\,\,rad/{{\rm{s}}^2}$
$\tau = I\alpha $
$2 \times {10^3} \times 4 = 8 \times {10^3}Nm$

question: If angular velocity of riding body is represented by $\omega=\alpha-\beta t$ at any time $t$ where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are constants. The angle through which it rotates before coming to rest is :
explanation:
Angular velocity becomes zero when
$\omega=0=\alpha-\beta t \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$
Also $\omega=\dfrac{d \theta}{d t} \Rightarrow d \theta=\omega d t$
$ \Rightarrow \int_0^\theta d \theta = \int_0^{t = \alpha /\beta } {(\alpha - \beta t)} dt$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = \left| {\alpha t - \frac{1}{2}\beta {t^2}} \right|_0^{\frac{\alpha }{\beta }} = \frac{{{\alpha ^2}}}{\beta } - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\alpha ^2}}}{\beta } = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\alpha ^2}}}{\beta }$
subject: Physics

question: A flywheel of moment of inertia $3 \times {10^2}\,kg - {m^2}$ is rotating with uniform angular speed of $4.6\,rad/s$. If a torque of $6.9 \times 10^{2} N-m$ retards the wheel, then the time in which the wheel comes to rest is
explanation:
Here, moment of inertia, $I = 3 \times {10^2}kg{m^2}$
Torque, $\tau = 6.9 \times {10^2}Nm$
Initial angular speed, ${\omega _0} = 4.6\,\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
Final angular speed, $\omega = 0\,\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
As $\omega=\omega_{0}+\alpha t$
$\therefore \alpha = \frac{{\omega - {\omega _0}}}{t} = \frac{{0 - 4.6}}{t} = - \frac{{4.6}}{t}rad{s^{ - 2}}$
Now, negative sign is for deceleration torque, $\tau=l \alpha$
$\begin{aligned}& 6.9 \times 10^{2}=3 \times 10^{2} \times \dfrac{4.6}{t} \\& t=\dfrac{3 \times 10^{2} \times 4.6}{6.9 \times 10^{2}}=2 s\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

explanation:

question: A massless rod is pivoted at one end so that it can swing freely as a pendulum. Two masses, 2 $m$ and $m$, are attached to it at distances $\mathrm{b}$ and $3 b$, respectively, from the pivot. The rod is held horizontal and then released. Find its angular acceleration at the instant it is released.
explanation:
The torque experienced by rod about the pivot given https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951241/preptracker/questions/q_34293_explanation_1764951241122.png $\tau=g(2 m b+m(3 b))=5 m g b[\therefore \tau=$ force $\times$ distance $]$
and $I=\left(2 m b^{2}+m\left(3 b^{2}\right)\right)=11 m b^{2}$
Since, $\tau=I \alpha$, we have $\alpha = 5 g/11 b$
