question: A particle moves along a circle of radius $\dfrac{20}{\pi} m$ with constant tangential acceleration. If the velocity of the particle is $80\,\,m/s$ at the end of two revolutions after motion has begun, the tangential acceleration is
explanation:
$\omega=\dfrac{v}{r}=\dfrac{80}{20 / \pi}=4 \pi, \omega_{0}=0, \theta=2 \pi(n)=4 \pi$
$(A s n=2)$
$\omega^{2}=\omega_{0}^{2}+2 \alpha \theta \Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{\omega^{2}-\omega_{0}^{2}}{2 \theta}=\dfrac{16 \pi^{2}}{2 \times 4 \pi}=2 \pi$
Tangential acceleration is
${a_t} = r\alpha = \frac{{20}}{\pi } \times 2\pi = 40\,m/{s^2}.$
subject: Physics

question: A wheel has a speed of 1200 revolutions per minute and is made to slow down at a rate of $4\,\,radians\,\,/\,\,{s^2}.$ The number of revolutions it makes before coming to rest is
explanation:
$\omega^{2}=\omega_{0}^{2}+2 \alpha \theta \Rightarrow 0=4 \pi^{2} n^{2}-2 \alpha \theta$
$\theta=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2}\left(\dfrac{1200}{60}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 4}=200 \pi^{2} \mathrm{rad}$
$\therefore 2 \pi n=200 \pi^{2} \Rightarrow n=100 \pi=314$

explanation:

Angular acceleration $\alpha=\dfrac{3 g}{2 l}$.

question: A heavy iron bar, of weight $W$ is having its one end on the ground and the other on the shoulder of a person. The bar makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The weight experienced by the person is ____ .
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954457/preptracker/questions/q_37156_explanation_1764954457354.png
Let $R$ be the reaction force on shoulder.
Let $L$ be the length of bar.
For the equilibrium, $\tau_{\text {net }}=0$ (or) $C W T=A C W T$
Take torque about $O$
$\therefore W\left(\dfrac{L}{2} \cos \theta\right)=R \times L \cos \theta$
$R=\dfrac{W}{2}$
Thus, weight experienced by the person is due to the reaction force.
subject: Physics

subtopic: Equilibrium of a Rigid Body

question: Figure shows a uniform block of mass ${m=\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{13}} {~kg}}$ having square base and equilateral triangular cross section placed at the edge of the floor of a truck which is under constant acceleration ${a=\dfrac{g \sqrt{3}}{4} {~m} / {s}^{2}}$ to the right. A horizontal force on the apex of the triangle ${F}$ is just enough to just tilt the block. Find the force  on the block at point ${A}$ where the block is in contact with the step edge when ${F}$ is applied. (Take ${g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}}$)
explanation:
Torque about ${A}$
$\begin{aligned}& F \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} d=m g \dfrac{d}{2}+m a \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} d \dfrac{1}{3} \\& \therefore \quad F=\dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{3}}+\dfrac{m a}{3} \\& \therefore \quad F_{H}=\dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{3}}-\dfrac{2 m a}{3} \\& \quad F_{v}=m g \\& \text { So, } \quad F_{A}=5 {~N}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Toppling or Tilting of a Body

question: Two rods each of mass ${m=4 {~kg}}$ and length ${l=1 {~m}}$ are in equilibrium being smoothly hinged at ${P}$. The tension in the connecting string as shown in figure is
explanation:
Drawing $FBD$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955986/preptracker/questions/q_36853_explanation_1764955986164.png ${N_{{x}} l \cos \phi-m g \dfrac{l}{2} \sin \phi-T \dfrac{l}{3} \cos \phi=0}$
${N_x} - \frac{T}{3} = \frac{{mg}}{2}\tan \phi \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${N_x} = T{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Then ${T=N_{x}=\dfrac{3 m g}{4}}$
On substituting ${m=4 {~kg}}$, we get ${T=30 {~N}}$

question: In the given figure, a uniform plank of length $6\,m$ and weight $200\,N$ rests horizontally on two supports, with $1\,m$ of the plank hanging over the right support. To what distance ${x}$ (in ${m}$) can a person who weighs $800\,N$ walk on the overhanging part of the plank before it just begins to tip?
explanation:
At every instant before the plank begins to tip, it is in equilibrium, and the net torque on it is zero: ${\Sigma \tau=0}$. When the person reaches the maximum distance ${x}$ along the overhanging part, the plank is just about to rotate about the right support. At that instant, the plank loses contact with the left support, the normal reaction at this point will become zero. This leaves only three vertical forces acting on the plank: the weight ${W_{\text {plank }}}$ of the plank, the normal reaction ${N_{{R}}}$ due to the right support, and the weight of the man ${W_{\operatorname{man}}}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951704/preptracker/questions/q_36845_explanation_1764951703937.png Let us taking torque about point ${B}$
${\Sigma \tau=W_{\text {plank }} l_{\text {plank }}-W_{\text {man }} l_{\text {man }}=0}$ or ${l_{\text {man }}=x=\dfrac{W_{\text {plank }} l_{\text {plank }}}{W_{\text {man }}}}$
Here, ${l_{\text {plank }}+1=\dfrac{6}{2}}$ or ${l_{\text {plank }}=2 {~m}}$
Hence from (1), ${x=\dfrac{(200 {~N})(2 {~m})}{800 {~N}}=0.50 {~m}}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Equilibrium of a Rigid Body

question: A particle moves in a straight line with constant acceleration. Its angular momentum about any fixed point is :
explanation:
Let a particle ${A}$ of mass ${m}$ is moving in a straight line with velocity ${v}$ as shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951697/preptracker/questions/q_36661_explanation_1764951697087.png Angular momentum of particle about any fixed point say ${O}$ will be
$\begin{aligned}& L=m\left(\overrightarrow{O A}^{\prime} \times \vec{v}\right) \\& L=m v O A^{\prime} \sin \theta=m v r \\& \Rightarrow L=m(a t) r \quad[a=\text { constant acceleration }] \\& L=(m a r) t \\& \therefore L \propto t\end{aligned}$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Measurement of Angular Momentum of a Particle

question: A particle of mass $(m)=5$ units is moving with a uniform speed $v=3 \sqrt{2}$ units in the xoy - plane along the line $y=x+4$. The magnitude of the angular momentum of the particle about the origin is
explanation:
Momentum of the particle
$\begin{aligned}& =\text { Mass } \times \text { Velocity } \\& =(5) \times(3 \sqrt{2})=15 \sqrt{2} \text { (given) }\end{aligned}$
The direction of momentum in the $X O Y$ plane is given by https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955975/preptracker/questions/q_36522_explanation_1764955974683.png $y=x+4$
Slope of the line $=1=\tan \theta$
$\theta=45^{\circ}$
Intercept of its straight line $=4$
Length of the perpendicular $Z$ from the origin of the straight line$=4 \sin 45^{\circ}=\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \sqrt{2}$
Angular momentum $=$ Momentum $\times$ Perpendicular length
$=15 \sqrt{2} \times 2 \sqrt{2}=60$ units
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Measurement of Angular Momentum of a Particle

question: A particle of mass $m$ is projected with a velocity $v$ making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The magnitude of angular momentum of the projectile about an axis of projection when the particle is of maximum height $h$ is
explanation:
Maximum height attained
$=\dfrac{v^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}=\dfrac{v^{2} \sin ^{2} 45^{\circ}}{2 g}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{4 g}$
$\left(\because \theta=45^{\circ}\right)$
At highest point, momentum
$=m v \cos 45^{\circ}=\dfrac{m v}{\sqrt{2}}$
Angular momentum
$=\dfrac{m v}{\sqrt{2}} \times \dfrac{v^{2}}{4 g}=\dfrac{m v^{3}}{4 \sqrt{2} g}$
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Measurement of Angular Momentum of a Particle

question: A force $F=2.0 \mathrm{~N}$ acts on a particle $P$ in the $x-z$ plane. The force $F$ is parallel to $X$-axis. question: Find the torque of force $F=-3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ acting at the point $r=8 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$.
explanation:
Given, ${\rm{F}} = - 3\hat i + 2j + \hat k$
and ${\rm{r}} = 8\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k$
Torque of the force, $r = r \times F$
${\rm{So, }}\tau = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k}\\ 8&2&3\\ { - 3}&2&1 \end{array}} \right|$
$ = \hat i(2 - 6) - \hat j(8 + 9) + \hat k(16 + 6)$
$ = - 4\hat i - 17\hat j + 22\hat k$

question: Four equal and parallel forces are acting on a rod (as shown in figure) at distances of $20\,cm$, ${40 {~cm}, 50 {~cm}}$ and $100\,cm$, respectively, from one end of the rod. Under the influence of these forces, the rod
explanation:

${\tau _o} = F \times 40 + F \times 100 - (F \times 20 + F \times 50)$

question: Three forces ${F, 2 F}$, and ${3 F}$ act on a rod ${A B}$, which is pivoted at ${O}$. The net torque produced by the forces about ${O}$ is
explanation:
The forces that act on the rod are shown in the figure. Hence we consider torque about point ${O}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955967/preptracker/questions/q_36450_explanation_1764955967215.png ${\tau_{F}=-F(2 a), \tau_{2 F}=2(F a)}$
${\tau_{3 F}=3 F(2 a) \quad(2 a}$-is a the lever arm ${)}$
The net torque by the three forces about ${O}$ is
${\tau_{o}=\tau_{2 F}+\tau_{F}+\tau_{3 F}=6 F a}$

question: Four equal and parallel forces are acting on a rod of length $100\,cm$, as shown in figure, at distances of ${20 {~cm}, 50 {~cm}, 60 {~cm}}$ and $80\,cm$ respectively from one end of the rod. Under the influence of these forces, the rod (neglecting its weight)
explanation:
Since, upward forces on the rod are equal to downward forces, hence, the resultant force on the rod is zero and there is no linear motion. Moment of forces about left end of rod
$ = - F \times 20 + F \times 50 - F \times 60 + F \times 80$
$ = F \times 50$
As it is not zero, hence the torque acts on the rod.
Correct Option is (2).

options.text: experiences torque and also a linear motion

question:
Assertion: A ladder is more likely to slip when a person is near the top than when he is near the bottom.

Reason: The friction between the ladder and floor decreases as he climbs up.
explanation: As he climbs and reaches top, frictional force at the base of the ladder decreases and it may not be sufficient and ladder may slip. The reason correctly explains this phenomenon. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: To unscrew a rusted nut, we need a wrench with longer arm.

Reason: Wrench with longer arm reduces the torque of the arm.
explanation:
We know $\tau=r \times F$
To unscrew a rusted nut, a wrench with a longer arm $(r)$ is needed to provide more torque. The reason is also true; a wrench with a longer arm increases the lever arm, which reduces the amount of force $(F)$ needed to produce the same torque. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The angular velocity of a planets orbiting around the sun increases when they are nearest to the sun.

Reason: The angular momentum of body is proportional to angular velocity when distance of the planet from sun remains constant.
explanation:
$L=m v r, v=r \omega$ is true that the angular velocity $\omega$ planets orbiting around the sun increases when they are nearest to the sun. ( $\mathrm{r}$ least). The change in angular velocity is due to conservation of angular momentum.
$\Rightarrow L \propto \omega \Rightarrow$ Reason is true
So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Torque due to force is maximum when angle is $90^{\circ}$.

Reason: The unit of torque is 'Newton-metre'.
explanation: Torque $\tau=r F \sin \theta$. Torque is maximum when the force is applied perpendicular to the lever arm (angle $\theta=90^{\circ}$ ). The unit of torque is indeed Newton-meter. Reason does not explain Assertion. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: It is harder to open and shut the door if we apply force near the hinge.

Reason: Torque is maximum at hinge of the door.
explanation:
For rotation $\tau \neq 0$ is the condition
At hinges $r=0 \Rightarrow \tau=0$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955942/preptracker/questions/q_36169_explanation_1764955942025.png $\Rightarrow$ Door cannot be rotated if $F$ is applied at hinges.
$\tau=r F \sin \theta$
For $\theta=90^{\circ}$ and $r$ is maximum at handle,
$\Rightarrow$ We have $\tau=\tau_{\text {max }}$ at handle
$\Rightarrow$ Reason is false
The torque required to open or close a door is highest when the force is applied at the handle which is the point farthest from the axis of rotation. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: A particle moving on a straight line with a uniform velocity, its angular momentum is always zero.

Reason: The angular momentum is a vector.
explanation:
$|\vec{L}|=m v r \sin \theta$
If straight line, $\theta=0$
$\Rightarrow L=0$. Angular momentum is zero only if the particle's velocity vector is parallel to the position vector so assertion is wrong. The angular momentum is a vector quantity. So correct option is (4).

question: A thin rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ is made to rotate about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to it. If its angular velocity changes from 0 to $\omega$ in time $t$, the torque acting on it is
explanation:
Since, $\tau=I \alpha$
So, $\tau=\left(\dfrac{m(2 \ell)^{2}}{12}\right)\left(\dfrac{\omega}{t}\right)$ or $\tau=\dfrac{m \times 4 \ell^{2} \times \omega}{12 \times t}$
Or $\tau = \frac{{4m{\ell ^2}\omega }}{{12t}} = \left( {\frac{{m{\ell ^2}\omega }}{{3t}}} \right)$

A set of $(m, x)$ values is recorded. The appropriate variables that give a straight line, when plotted, are:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956904/preptracker/questions/q_35677_explanation_1764956903889.png Balancing torque w.r.t. point of suspension
$\begin{aligned}& m g x=M g\left(\dfrac{l}{2}-x\right) \\& m=\left(M \dfrac{l}{2}\right) \dfrac{1}{x}-M\end{aligned}$
$Y=\alpha \dfrac{1}{x}-C$ equation of a straight line
The graph between $m$ and $\dfrac{1}{x}$ is a straight line

question: A particle of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is on a smooth horizontal table and moves in a circular path of radius 0.6 $\mathrm{m}$. The height of the table from the ground is 0.8 . If the angular speed of the particle is $12 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, the magnitude of its angular omentum about a point on the ground right under the centre of the circle is:
explanation:
The point $\mathrm{P}$ is shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955914/preptracker/questions/q_35652_explanation_1764955914069.png The angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{v}$ is $90^{\circ}$
Angular momentum, $L_{\mathrm{P}}=m v r \sin 90^{\circ}$
$=2 \times 0.6 \times 12 \times 1 \times 1$
[As $v=\mathrm{R} \omega, \sin 90^{\circ}=1$ ]
So, $L_{\mathrm{P}}=14.4 \mathrm{kgm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}$

question: A particle is moving in a circular path of radius $a$, with a constant velocity $v$ as shown in the figure. The centre of circle is marked by ' $\mathrm{C}$. The angular momentum from the origin $\mathrm{O}$ can be written as
explanation:
The angular momentum of the particle is $L_{O}=r P_{\perp}\left(P_{\perp}\right.$-component of momentum which is perpendicular to the radial distance) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951562/preptracker/questions/q_35648_explanation_1764951562159.png ${L_O} = rmv\cos \theta = (2a\cos \theta )mv\cos \theta $
${L_O} = 2mva{\cos ^2}\theta = mva(1 + \cos 2\theta )$
${L_O} = va(1 + \cos 2\theta )\quad (If\,\,m = 1)$

question: A ball of mass $160 \mathrm{~g}$ is thrown up at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal at a speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The angular momentum of the ball at the highest point of the trajectory with respect to the point from which the ball is thrown is nearly $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$
explanation:
The angular momentum of the particle at the heighest point is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955913/preptracker/questions/q_35644_explanation_1764955912489.png $L=\left(m u \cos 60^{\circ}\right) H$
$\begin{aligned}& =\left(\dfrac{m u}{2}\right) \dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} 60^{\circ}}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\& =\dfrac{m u^{3} \sin ^{2} 60^{\circ}}{4 \mathrm{~g}}=3.0 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

question: Two blocks each of the mass $m$ are attached to the ends of a massless rod which pivotes as shown in the figure. Initially the rod is held in the horizontal position and then released. Calculate the net torque on this system above pivot.
explanation:
Torque due to weight of left $\mathrm{m}$ :
$\vec{\tau}_{1}=m g l_{1} \hat{k}$
Torque due to weight of right $\mathrm{m}$ :
$\vec{\tau}_{2}=-m g l_{2} \hat{k}$
So net torque: $\vec{\tau}=\vec{\tau}_{1}+\vec{\tau}_{2}=\left(m g l_{1}-m g l_{2}\right) \hat{k}$

question: A particle of mass $m$ is moving along the side of a square of side ' $a$ ', with a uniform speed $v$ in the $x-y$ plane as shown in the figure. Which of the following statements is false for the angular momentum $\vec{L}$ about the origin?
explanation:
We know that $\vec{L}=\vec{r} \times m \vec{v}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955894/preptracker/questions/q_35455_explanation_1764955894449.png ${\vec L_O} = mv\frac{R}{{\sqrt 2 }}( - \hat k)[{\rm{ D}}\,\,{\rm{to}}\,\,A]$
${\vec L_O} = mv\left[ {\frac{R}{{\sqrt 2 }} + a} \right]\hat k[C\,\,to\,\,D]$
So the false option is (1)

options.text: $\vec{L}=m v\left[\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{2}}-a\right] \hat{k}$ when the particle is moving from $C\,\,to\,\,D.$
$\vec{L}=m v\left[\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{2}}+a\right] \hat{k}$ when the particle is moving from $\mathrm{B}$ to $\mathrm{C}$.
$\vec{L}=-\dfrac{m v}{\sqrt{2}} R \hat{k}$ when the particle is moving from $\mathrm{D}$ to $\mathrm{A}$.
$\vec{L}=-\dfrac{m v}{\sqrt{2}} R \hat{k}$ when the particle is moving from A to B.

question: Two uniform boards, tied together with the help of a string, are balanced on a surface as shown in the figure. The coefficient of static friction between boards and surface is 0.5 . The minimum value of $\theta$, for which this type of arrangement is possible is
explanation:
For the equilibrium of the entire structure, $N_{1}+N_{2}=2 M g$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951505/preptracker/questions/q_35372_explanation_1764951504814.png $N_{2}=M g, N_{1}=M g \quad\left(\text { since } N_{1}=N_{2}\right)$
For individual boards, $T_{1}=f_{1}, N_{1}=M g$
and $T_{1} \times l \sin \theta=M g \dfrac{l}{2} \cos \theta$
$f_{1}=T_{1}=\dfrac{M g}{2 \tan \theta}$
For safe equilibrium, $f_{1} < \mu_{s} M g$
$\dfrac{M g}{2 \tan \theta} < \mu_{s} M g \Rightarrow \tan \theta>\dfrac{1}{2 \mu_{s}} \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ}$

options.text: It is not possible to have this type of balanced arrangement

subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Rigid Body

question: A horizontal force F is applied such that the block remains stationary then which of the following statement is false
explanation:
As the block remains stationary therefore for translatory equilibrium https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951503/preptracker/questions/q_35368_explanation_1764951503168.png $\Sigma F_{x}=0 \therefore F=N$
and $\Sigma F_{y}=0 \therefore f=m g$
For rotational equilibrium $\Sigma \tau=0$
By taking the torque of different forces about point $\mathrm{O}$ $\overrightarrow{\tau_{F}}+\overrightarrow{\tau_{f}}+\overrightarrow{\tau_{N}}+\overrightarrow{\tau_{m g}}=0$
As $\mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{mg}$ passing through point $\mathrm{O}$
$\therefore \overrightarrow{\tau_{f}}+\overrightarrow{\tau_{N}}=0$
As $\overrightarrow{\tau_{f}} \neq 0 \therefore \overrightarrow{\tau_{N}} \neq 0$ and torque by friction and normal reaction will be in opposite direction.
subject: Physics

question: A particle of mass $m$ in the $XY$-plane with a velocity $v$ along the straight line $AB$. If the angular momentum of the particle with respect to orgin $O$ is $L_{A}$ when it is at $A$ and $L_{B}$ when it is at $B$, then
explanation:
From the definition of angular momentum,
$L=m v r \sin \phi=m v d$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956876/preptracker/questions/q_35269_explanation_1764956876033.png Where, $d=r \sin \phi$ is the distance of closed approach of the particle to the origin. As $d$ is same for both the particles, hence $L_{A}=L_{B}$.

options.text: The relationship between $L_{A}$ and $L_{B}$ depends upon the slope of the line $AB$

question: If there is a change of angular momentum from $1\,Js\,\,to\,\,4\,Js$ in $4\,s$, then the torque applied is
explanation:
The relation between torque $\tau$ and angular momentum
$L$ is $\tau=\dfrac{d L}{d t} \approx \dfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta t}$
Here, $\Delta L = (4 - 1) = 3Js$
and $\Delta L = (4 - 1) = 3Js$
$\therefore \tau=\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) J$

question: $A B C$ is an equilateral triangle with $O$ as its centre. $F_{1}, F_{2}$ and $F_{3}$ represent three forces acting along the sides $A B, B C$ and $A C$, respectively. If the total torque about $O$ is zero, then the magnitude of $F_{3}$ is
explanation:
If we take clockwise torque
${\tau _{net}} = {\tau _{{F_1}}} + {\tau _{{F_2}}} + {\tau _{{F_3}}}$
$0 = {F_1}r + {F_2}r - {F_3}r$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {F_3} = {F_1} + {F_2}$

question: A force $\vec{F}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$ acts on a particle whose position vector is $\vec{r}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$. What is the torque about the origin?
explanation:
Here, $\vec{r}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$.
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
Torque, $\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$
$ = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k}\\1&{ - 2}&1\\5&2&{ - 5}\end{array}} \right]$
$ = \hat i(10 - 2) - \hat j( - 5 - 5) + \hat k(2 - ( - 10)$
$ = 8\hat i + 10\hat j + 12\hat k$

question:
A rod of $l\,m$ long is acted upon by a couple as shown in figure. The moment of couple is $\tau Nm$. If the force at each end of the rod is $F$, then magnitude of each force is
$\left(\sin 30^{\circ}=\cos 60^{\circ}=0.5\right)$
explanation:

$\therefore$ Force acting on each end

question: Find the torque about the origin when a force of $3\hat j\,N$ acts on a particle whose position vector is $2\,km$.
explanation:
$\vec F = 3\hat jN,\,\,\vec r = 2\hat k\,\,$
$\,\vec \tau = \vec r \times \vec F = 2\hat k \times 3\hat j{\rm{ }} = 6(\hat k \times \hat j)$
$ = 6( - \hat i)$
$\vec \tau {\rm{ }} = - 6\hat iNm$

question: The moment of the force, $\vec{F}=4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}$ at $(2,0,-3)$, about the point $(2,-2,-2)$, is given by
explanation:
Given that $\vec{F}=4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}$
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{r}_{i j}=(2 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})-(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \\& =2 \hat{j}-k\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Moment of force $=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$
$\begin{aligned}& \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\0 & 2 & -1 \\4 & 5 & -6\end{array}\right| \\& =\hat{i}(-12+5)-\hat{j}(0+4)+\hat{k}(0-8) \\& =-7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}\end{aligned}$

question:
Angular momentum of a body depends on axis of measurement.
Torque acting on a body depends on axis of measurement
explanation:
$\bar{L}=\bar{r} \times \bar{P}, \vec{\tau}=\bar{r} \times \bar{P}$
So both $\bar{L}$ and $\vec{\tau}$ depend on axis about which they are measured.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Measurement of Angular Momentum of a Particle

question:
A lever system is shown in the figure below.
${F_1}$ is usually some weight to be lifted and force
${F_2}$ is effort applied to lift the load. Choose the correct statements.
I. The ratio $\frac{{{F_1}}}{{{F_2}}}$ is equal to mechanical advantage, i.e., $MA = \frac{{{F_1}}}{{{F_2}}} = \frac{{{d_2}}}{{{d_1}}}$.
II. If the effort arm ${d_2}$ is larger than the load arm, then a small effort can be used to lift a large load.
III. At equilibrium load arm $ \times $ effort arm $ = $ load $ \times $ effort.
explanation:
Statements I and II are correct but statements III is incorrect since
At equilibrium,
Load arm $\times$ Load $=$ Effort $\times$ Effort arm
$F_{1} d_{1}=F_{2} d_{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}=\dfrac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}$
The ratio of $\dfrac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}$ is called mechanical advantage.
subject: Physics
chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
subtopic: Equilibrium of a Rigid Body

question: A particle of mass $m$ is moving in $y z$ - plane with a uniform velocity $v$ with its trajectory running parallel to +ve $y$-axis and intersecting z-axis at $z = a$. The change in its angular momentum about the origin as it bounces elastically from a wall at $y = $ constant is
explanation:
Linear momentum of particle before colliding $=m \vec{v}=m v e_{y}$
Linear momentum of particle after it bounces
$=-m \vec{v}=-m v e_{y}$
Change in linear momentum,
$\Delta \vec{p}=-m \vec{v}-(m \vec{v})=-2 m v e_{y}$
Change in angular momentum $\Delta L=\vec{r} \times \Delta \vec{p}$, where
$\begin{aligned}\vec{r} & =\left(y e_{y}+a e_{z}\right) \\& =\left(y e_{y}+a e_{z}\right) \times\left(-2 m v e_{y}\right)=2 m v a e_{x}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Measurement of Angular Momentum of a Particle

question: A particle of mass $3\,kg$ moves along a straight line $4 y-3 x=2$ (where $x$ and $y$ are in metre), with constant velocity $5\,m/s$. The magnitude of angular momentum about the origin is
explanation:
$4 y-3 x=2$
$\begin{aligned}& y=\dfrac{3}{4} x+\dfrac{1}{2} \\& \theta=37^{\circ}\end{aligned}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951339/preptracker/questions/q_34857_explanation_1764951338723.png The angular momentum about origin is
$L = mv\cos {\rm{ }}37^\circ \times \frac{1}{2} = 3 \times 5\cos \,\,37^\circ {\rm{ }} \times \frac{1}{2}$
$ = 6kg{m^2}\,{s^{ - 1}}$

question: A uniform cube of mass $M$ and side $a$ is placed on a frictionless horizontal surface. A vertical force $F$ is applied to edge as shown in figure. If $F=\dfrac{M g}{4}$ then find the distance of normal force line from point $O$.
explanation:
Let $x$ be the distance of $N$ from point $O$. When $F=\dfrac{M g}{4}$ the equilibrium equations are
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_08_02_0167e8c06dfa23edb050g-27.jpg?height=277&width=166&top_left_y=2171&top_left_x=535
$\sum \tau_{O}=0 \Rightarrow \tau_{F}+\tau_{N}=\tau_{M g}$
$Fa + Nx = Mg\left( {\frac{a}{2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\sum F_{y}=0 \Rightarrow F+N=M g$
$\frac{{Mg}}{4} + N = Mg \Rightarrow N = \frac{{3Mg}}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq's (1) & (2)
$x=\dfrac{a}{3}$
So the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: Calculate the force $F$ that is applied horizontally at the axle of the wheel which is necessary to raise the wheel over the obstacle of height $0.4\,\,m$. Radius of wheel is $1\,\,m$ and mass $ = 10\,kg$. $F$ is
explanation:

$F \times 0.6=100 \times 0.8$
or $F=\dfrac{100 \times 0.8}{0.6} N=\dfrac{800}{6} N=133.3 N$

question: A force $\vec{F}=4 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is acting at a point $\vec{r}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$. The torque acting about a point $\vec{r}_{2}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$ is
explanation:
Position vector of the point at which force is acting
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{r}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \quad \vec{r}_{2}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} \\& \overrightarrow{r_{12}}=\overrightarrow{r_{1}}-\overrightarrow{r_{2}}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}) \\& =-2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}\end{aligned}$
Now $\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{r_{12}} \times \vec{F}=(-2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}) \times(4 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{\tau}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\-2 & 4 & 6 \\4 & -5 & 3\end{array}\right| \\& =\hat{i}(12+30)-\hat{j}(-6-24)+\hat{k}(10-16) \\& =(42 \hat{i}+30 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}) N-m\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

explanation: The angular momentum of the particle with respect to $P$ is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951337/preptracker/questions/q_34760_explanation_1764951337325.png $\begin{aligned}& L_{p}=m v_{y} R \\& v_{y}=\sqrt{2 g H} \\& R=u T=u \sqrt{\dfrac{2 H}{g}} \\& \Rightarrow L_{p}=m \sqrt{2 g H} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 H}{g}} u=2 m u H\end{aligned}$

question: A projectile of mass $m$ is launched with an initial velocity $u$ making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal as shown in the figure. The projectile moves in the gravitational field of the Earth. The angular momentum of the projectile about the origin when it is at the highest point of its trajectory is
explanation:
$\bar{r}=\dfrac{R}{2} \hat{i}+h_{\max } \hat{j}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{2 g} \hat{i}+\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g} \hat{j} \\& \bar{v}=u \cos \theta \hat{i} \\& \bar{L}=m(\bar{r} \times \bar{v})=\dfrac{-m u^{3} \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}{2 g} \hat{k}\end{aligned}$

question: A vertical disc of mass $5\,kg$ and radius $50\,cm$ rests against a step of height $25\,cm$ as shown in the figure. What minimum horizontal force applied perpendicular to the axle will make the disc to climb the step? Take $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$.
explanation:
From the figure
$y = 50 - 25 = 25 cm$
$x=\sqrt{50^{2}-25^{2}}=25 \sqrt{3}$
$F y=5 g x$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951281/preptracker/questions/q_34611_explanation_1764951281390.png $\Rightarrow F(25)=5 \times 10(25 \sqrt{3})$
$\Rightarrow F=50 \sqrt{3} N$

question: A false balance has equal arms. An object weighs $W_{1}$ when placed in one pan and $W_{2}$ when placed in the other pan. The true weight $W$ of the object is
explanation:
Let true weight of the object be $W$ and weight of right pan is $x$ more than left pan.
Now suppose the object is placed in left pan:
$W = {W_1} + x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Now if the object is placed in right pan.
${W_2} = W + x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2)
$W=\dfrac{W_{1}+W_{2}}{2}$

question: Average torque on a projectile of mass $m$ (initial speed $u$ and angle of projection $\theta$ ) between initial and final positions $P$ and $Q$ about the point of projection is:
explanation:
$\vec{\tau}_{a v} \cdot \Delta t=\Delta \vec{L}$
Here $\Delta t=$ time of flight $==\dfrac{2 u \sin \theta}{g}$
Change in angular momentum about point of projection (initially it is zero)
$|\overrightarrow{\Delta L}|=\left|\vec{L}_{f}-\vec{L}_{i}\right|=\mid(m u \sin \theta)$ Range $-0 \mid$
$=\dfrac{(m u \sin \theta)\left(u^{2} \sin 2 \theta\right)}{g}=\dfrac{m u^{3} \sin \theta \sin 2 \theta}{g}$
Now $\left|\vec{\tau}_{a v}\right|=\left|\dfrac{\overrightarrow{\Delta L}}{\Delta t}\right|=\dfrac{m u^{3} \sin \theta \sin 2 \theta}{g} \times \dfrac{g}{2 u \sin \theta}$
$=\dfrac{m u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform bar of mass $M$ and length $L$ is horizontally suspended from the ceiling by two vertical light cables as shown. Cable $A$ is connected $1 / 4$ th distance from the left end of the bar. Cable $B$ is attached at the far right end of the bar. What is the tension in cable $A$?
explanation:
The bar is in equilibrium, the total torques must sum to zero. Let $T_{A}$ and $T_{B}$ are tensions in the strings. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951237/preptracker/questions/q_34285_explanation_1764951236874.png The net torque about point $O$ is
$\sum \tau=T_{A}\left(\dfrac{3 L}{4}\right)-M g\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)=0$
Therefore
$T_{A}=2 M g / 3$

question: A force of $-F \hat{K}$ acts on $O$, the origin of the coordinate system. The torque about point $(1,-2)$ is:
explanation:
Given that $\vec{F}=-F \hat{K}$
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}) \\& \vec{\tau}=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}=(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j})(-F \hat{K}) \\& =F(\hat{i} \times \hat{k})-F(2 \hat{j} \times \hat{k}) \\& =F(-\hat{j})-F(2 \hat{i})=-F(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j})\end{aligned}$

question: The force $7 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$ acts on a particle whose position vector is $\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$. What is the torque of a given force about the origin?
explanation:
Here, $\vec{r}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$
$\vec{F}=7 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$
Torque, $\quad \vec{\tau}=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{\tau}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\1 & -1 & 1 \\7 & 3 & -5\end{array}\right| \\&=\hat{i}(5-3)+\hat{j}(7-(-5))+\hat{k}(3-(-7)) \\& \text { or } \vec{\tau}=2 \hat{i}+12 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}\end{aligned}$

Here, $\vec F = - F\hat k,{{\vec r}_{PO}} = - \hat i + \hat j{\rm{ }}$
As $\vec \tau = \vec r \times \vec F = ( - \hat i + \hat j) \times ( - F\hat k)$
$ = - F[ - \hat i \times \hat k + \hat j \times \hat k]$
$ = - F[\hat i + \hat j]$

question: Scalar product of two vectors is $2 \sqrt{3}$ and the magnitude of their vector product is equal to $2,$ then the angle (in degrees) between them will be
explanation:
$\vec A \cdot \overrightarrow B = \left| {\overrightarrow A } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow B } \right|\cos \theta = 2\sqrt 3 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\left| {\overrightarrow A } \right| \times \left| {\overrightarrow B } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow A } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow B } \right|\sin \theta = 2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing equation (2) by equation (1) we get,
$\tan \theta = \frac{2}{{2\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
$\therefore \theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right) = 30^\circ $

question: If ${c=\hat{i} \times(a \times \hat{i})+\hat{j} \times(a \times \hat{j})+\hat{k} \times(a \times \hat{k})}$ and ${c=n a}$.  Find  the value of $n$
explanation:
Let ${a=a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k}}$
$\therefore c = \hat i \times (a \times \hat i) + \hat j \times (a \times \hat j) + \hat k(a \times \hat k)$
${ = \hat i \times \left( { - {a_2}\hat k + {a_3}\hat j} \right) + \hat j \times }$
${\left( {{a_1}\hat k - {a_3}\hat i} \right) + \hat k \times \left( { - {a_1}\hat j + {a_2}\hat i} \right)}$
$ = {a_2}\hat j + {a_3}\hat k + {a_1}\hat i + {a_3}\hat k + {a_1}\hat i + {a_2}\hat j$
$ = 2\left( {{a_1}\hat i + {a_2}\hat j + {a_3}\hat k} \right) = 2a$
$\therefore n = 2$

question: The vectors  from origin to the points A and B are $\vec A = 3\hat i - 6\hat j + 2\hat k$ and $\vec B = 2\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k$ respectively. The area of the triangle $OAB$ be
explanation:
Given $\overline {OA} = \vec a = 3\hat i - 6\hat j + 2\hat k\,\,{\rm{and}}$
$\overline {OB} = \vec b = 2\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k$
$\therefore \,\,(\vec a \times \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k}\\3&{ - 6}&2\\2&1&{ - 2}\end{array}} \right|$
$ = (12 - 2)\hat i + (4 + 6)\hat j + (3 + 12)\hat k$
$ = 10\hat i + 10\hat j + 15\hat k$
$ = \sqrt {425} = 5\sqrt {17} $
Area of $\Delta \,OAB = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = \frac{{5\sqrt {17} }}{2}$ sq.unit.

question: What is the unit vector perpendicular to the following vectors $2\hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k$ and $6\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k$ ?
explanation:

$\vec C = \vec A \times \vec B = (2\hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k) \times (6\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k)$
$ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k}\\2&2&{ - 1}\\6&{ - 3}&2\end{array}} \right| = \hat i - 10\hat j - 18\hat k$
Unit vector perpendicular to both $\vec A\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,\vec B$
$ = \frac{{\hat i - 10\hat j - 18\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {{10}^2} + {{18}^2}} }} = \frac{{\hat i - 10\hat j - 18\hat k}}{{5\sqrt {17} }}$

question: If $\overrightarrow A  \cdot \overrightarrow B  = 0$ and $\overrightarrow A  \times \overrightarrow B  = 1$, then ${\vec A}$ and ${\vec B}$ are
explanation:

or $AB\sin \theta = 1$
$AB\sin 90^\circ = 1\,\,{\rm{or}}\,\,AB = 1$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,A = 1\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,B = 1$
So, A and B are perpendicular unit vectors.

question: Three vectors $\vec A,\vec B$ and ${\vec C}$ atisfy the relation $\vec A \cdot \vec B = 0$ and $\vec A \cdot \vec C = 0.$ The vector ${\vec A}$ is parallel to
explanation:
If $\vec A \cdot \vec B = 0,\vec A$ is perpendicular to $\vec B.$
If $\vec A \cdot \vec C = 0,\vec A$is perpendicular to $\vec C.$
So ${\vec A}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec B\,\,and\,\,\vec C.$
Also $\vec B \times \vec C$ is perpendicular to both $\vec B\,\,and\,\,\vec C.$
Hence ${\vec A}$ is parallel to $\vec B \times \vec C.$

question:
A stick of length $\dfrac{10}{3}$ metre is held vertically with one end on a horizontal floor. It is then allowed to fall on the floor. Assuming that the end of the stick in contact with the floor and it does not slip, the speed of the other end of the rod when it hits the floor is
(Take $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
Moment of inertia of the rod about the lower end,
$I{\text{ }} = {I_c} + M{h^2}$
$ = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{{12}} + M{\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{3}$
From conservation of energy,
$\frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2} = Mg\frac{L}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \omega {\text{ }} = \sqrt {\frac{{MgL}}{I}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{3\,g}}{{\,L}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {I = \frac{{M{L^2}}}{3}} \right)$
$\therefore \quad v = \omega L = \sqrt {3gL} $
$ = \sqrt {3 \times 10 \times \frac{{10}}{3}} $
$ = 10\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A force of  $15\,N$ is applied on a cord wrapped around a cylinder of mass $2\,kg.$ The cylinder rolls without slipping on the floor. What is its kinetic energy (in joule) when cylinder has moved by a distance of $3{\rm{/}}5m$?
explanation:
Since $15 < mg$, the cylinder rolls on ground without lifting up.
K.E. $=$ Work done $=$ Force $\times$ displacement
$ = 15 \times \frac{3}{5}$
$\therefore \,K.E.{\rm{ }} = 9\,J$

question:
The ring of radius 1 $m$ and mass 15 $kg$ is rotating about its diameter with angular velocity of
25 rad/$\sec $ Its kinetic energy is
explanation:
Here, radius ${(R)}$ and mass ${(M)}$ of the ring are 1 $m$ and 15 $kg$ respectively.
Angular velocity ${(\omega)=25 {rad} / {sec}}$
Rotational kinetic energy ${(K)}$ of body is given by
$K = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}\quad \quad \quad (1)$
where, ${I=}$ moment of inertia
Moment of inertia of ring rotating about its diameter is given by
$I = \frac{1}{2}M{R^2}\quad \quad \quad (2)$
Substitute eq. (2) in eq. (1), we get
$K = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{1}{2}M{R^2}} \right){\omega ^2} = \frac{1}{4} \times 15{(1)^2} \times {(25)^2}$
$K = 2343.75\,J$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A ring and a disc of different masses are rotating with the same kinetic energy. If we apply a retarding torque $\tau$ on the ring, it stops after completing $n$ revolutions in all. If same torque is applied to the disc, how many revolutions would it complete in all before stopping?
explanation:
Because kinetic energy $K_{R}$ and retarding torque are same, therefore in accordance with the relation
Loss in kinetic energy
$\begin{aligned}& =\text { Work done by torque } \\& =\tau \theta=\tau 2 \pi n\end{aligned}$
So, both the ring and the disc stop after completing equal number of revolutions $n$.

question: A uniform rod of length $l$ is free to rotate in a vertical plane about a fixed horizontal axis through $B$. The rod begins rotating from rest from its unstable equilibrium position. When it has turned through an angle $\theta$, its angular velocity $\omega$ is given by
explanation:
When the rod rotates through an angle $\theta$, the centre of gravity falls through a distance $h$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951681/preptracker/questions/q_36560_explanation_1764951680637.png From $\Delta \,BCG'$
$\cos \theta=\dfrac{(l / 2)-h}{l / 2}$
or $h=\dfrac{l}{2}(1-\cos \theta)$
Decrease in $PE = mg\frac{l}{2}(1 - \cos \theta ){\mkern 1mu} \quad \quad \quad (1)$
The decrease in $P E$ is equal to the kinetic energy of rotation
${(KE)_{{\text{rotational }}}} = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{m{l^2}}}{3}} \right){\omega ^2}\quad \quad (2)$
$\left[ {\because I = \frac{{m{l^2}}}{3}} \right]$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{m{l^2}}}{3}} \right){\omega ^2} = mg\frac{l}{2}(1 - \cos \theta )$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{6g}}{l}} \sin \frac{\theta }{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A rigid rod of length ${l}$ and negligible mass has a ball of mass ${m}$ attached to one end with its other end fixed, to form a pendulum as shown in figure. The pendulum is inverted, with the rod vertically up, and then released. Find the tension in the rod at the lowest point of the trajectory of ball.
explanation:
From conservation of energy :
${\Delta U=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}$
${m g(2 l)=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}}$
${\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{4 g l}=2 \sqrt{g l}}$
At the lowest point, the tension in the rod is,
${T-m g=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}}$
${\Rightarrow T=m g+\dfrac{m}{l}(4 g l)=5 m g}$.

question: A body of mass $1\,kg$ is suspended by a weightless string which passes over a frictionless pulley of mass $2\,kg$ as shown in the figure. The mass is released from a height of ${1.6 m}$ from the ground. With what velocity does it strike the ground?
explanation:
Using mechanical energy conservation ${T E_{i}=T E_{f}}$
${m_{1} g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}}$
${I=\dfrac{m_{2} R^{2}}{2} \times \dfrac{v^{2}}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{M_{2}}{2} v^{2}}$
${m_{1} g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m_{1} v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{m_{2}}{2} v^{2}}$
${1 \times 10 \times 1.6=\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 1\right) v^{2}}$
$v = 4\,m{s^{ - 1}}$.

question:
Assertion: Power associated with torque is product of torque and angular speed of the body about the axis of rotation.

Reason: Unit of torque is $N.m$
explanation:
The power associated with torque in rotational motion is given by the product of torque and angular speed, $\left(P_{r o t}=\tau \omega\right)$ similar to how power in translational motion is given by the product of force and linear velocity $(P=F v)$.
$\Rightarrow P_{r o t}=\tau \omega$
Unit of torque is ${\rm{N}}{\rm{.m}}$
So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: As shown in the figure, a bob of mass m is tied by massless string whose other end portion is wound on a fly wheel (disc) of radius r and mass m. When released from rest the bob starts falling vertically. When it has covered a distance of h, the angular speed of the wheel will be
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951564/preptracker/questions/q_35685_explanation_1764951564299.png Assume that the bob travels down by $h$.
From energy conservation
$mgh = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\omega = \frac{v}{r}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Solving (1) & (2), we get
$\omega=\dfrac{1}{r} \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g h}{3}}$

explanation:

$F r \Delta t=L$
As $F \& \Delta t$ are same
$L_{2}>L_{1} \quad\left(r_{2}>r_{1}\right)$
$K=\dfrac{L^{2}}{2 I}=\dfrac{F^{2} r^{2} \Delta t^{2}}{2 \dfrac{m r^{2}}{2}}=\dfrac{F^{2} \Delta t^{2}}{m}$
$\Rightarrow K_{1}=K_{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A ball of radius $r$ lies at the bottom of a fixed vertical ring of radius $\mathrm{R}$, find the minimum velocity to be given so that the ball completes the loop rolling without slipping
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951525/preptracker/questions/q_35446_explanation_1764951525196.png $\begin{aligned}\dfrac{1}{2} m v_{b o t t}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{\text {bott }}^{2}=m g 2(R-r)+ & \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{\text {top }}^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{\text {top }}^{2} \\\dfrac{7}{5} m v_{\text {bott }}^{2}=4 m g(R-r)+\dfrac{7}{5} m v_{\text {top }}^{2} & =4 m g(R-r) \\& +\dfrac{7}{5}[g(R-r)]\end{aligned}$
or $\quad v_{b o t t}=\sqrt{\dfrac{27}{7} g(R-r)}$

question: A body having a moment of inertia about its axis of rotation equal to $3\,kg\,{m^2}$ is rotating with angular velocity of $3\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$. Kinetic energy of this rotating body is same as that of a body of mass $27\,kg$ moving with velocity $v$. The value of $v$ is
explanation:
Here,
Moment of inertia of the body about its axis of rotation, $I = 3\,kg\,{m^2}$
Angular velocity of rotation, $\omega = 3\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
Kinetic energy of rotation of the body is
${K_R} = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}$
Kinetic energy of body of mass $m( = 27\,kg)$ moving with velocity $v$ is
${K_T} = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2}$
As per question, ${K_R} = {K_T}$
$\therefore \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2} = \frac{1}{2}\,m{v^2}$
$\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times {3^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 27 \times {v^2}\,\,or\,\,{v^2} = 1\,\,or\,\,v = 1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: When a 12000 joule of work is done on a fly wheel, its frequency of rotation increases from $10\,Hz\,\,to\,\,20\,Hz$. The moment of inertia of flywheel about its axis of rotation is $\left(\pi^{2}=10\right)$
explanation:
Given, work done on the flywheel $W = $ $12000\,J$,
initial frequency ${f_1} = 10\,Hz$,
and final frequency ${f_2} = 20\,Hz$.
Initial angular velocity,
${\omega _1} = 2\pi {f_1} = 2\pi \times 10 = 20\pi \,rad/s.$
Final angular velocity,
${\omega _2} = 2\pi {f_2} = 2\pi \times 20 = 40\pi \,rad/s$
If I is the moment of inertia of the flywheel, then using work - energy theorem,
work done on the flywheel $=$ change in its rotational kinetic energy
$ \Rightarrow W = \frac{1}{2}I\left( {\omega _2^2 - \omega _1^2} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 12000 = \frac{1}{2}I\left( {1600{\pi ^2} - 400{\pi ^2}} \right)$
$ = \frac{1}{2}I(1200 \times 10)$
$ \Rightarrow I = \frac{{12000 \times 2}}{{12000}} = 2kg{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question: Three objects, $A$ : (a solid sphere), $B$ : (a thin circular disc) and $C$ : (a circular ring), each have the same mass $M$ and radius $R$. They all spin with the same angular speed $\omega$ about their own symmetry axes. The amounts of work $(W)$ required to bring them to rest, would satisfy the relation
explanation:
Rotational $K\,E\,K = \frac{1}{2}I{\omega ^2}$
$\begin{aligned}& K_{A}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{2}{5} m r^{2} \times \omega^{2} \\& K_{B}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} \times \omega^{2} \\& K_{C}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times m r^{2} \times \omega^{2} \\& K_{A} < K_{B} < K_{C}\end{aligned}$
Work done to bring them to rest $=\Delta K$ $W_{A} < W_{B} < W_{C}$

question: A rope is wound around a hollow cylinder of mass $3\,kg$ and radius $40\,cm$. What is the angular acceleration of the cylinder if the rope is pulled with a force of $30\,N?$
explanation:
$m = 3\,kg,r = 40\,cm = 40 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m,F = 30\,N$
Moment of inertia of hollow cylinder about its axis
$ = m{r^2} = 3\,kg \times {(0.4)^2}{m^2} = 0.48\,kg \,{m^2}$
The torque is given by, $t=I \alpha$
where $I=$ moment of inertia, $\alpha=$ angular acceleration
In the given case, $\tau=r F$, as the force is acting perpendicularly to the radial vector.
$\therefore \alpha = \frac{\tau }{I} = \frac{{Fr}}{{m{r^2}}} = \frac{F}{{mr}}{\rm{ }} = \frac{{30}}{{3 \times 40 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$
$ = \frac{{30 \times 100}}{{3 \times 40}}$
$\alpha = 25rad\,{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A solid sphere of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and ratio $R$ is rotating about its diameter. A solid cylinder of the same mass and same radius is also rotating about its geometrical axis with an angular speed twice that of the sphere. The ratio of their kinetic energies of rotation $\left( {{E_{{\rm{sphere }}}}/{E_{{\rm{cylinder }}}}} \right)$ will be
explanation:
$\dfrac{E_{\text {Sphere }}}{E_{\text {Cylinder }}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} I_{S} \omega_{S}^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} I_{C} \omega_{C}^{2}}=\dfrac{I_{S} \omega_{S}^{2}}{I_{C} \omega_{C}^{2}}$
here, $I_{S}=\dfrac{2}{5} m r^{2}, I_{C}=\dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}, \omega_{C}=2 \omega_{S}$
$\dfrac{E_{\text {Sphere }}}{E_{\text {Cylinder }}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{2}{5} m R^{2} \times \omega_{S}^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} m R^{2} \times\left(2 \omega_{S}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{4}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{4}=\dfrac{1}{5}$
subject: Physics

question: Two rotating bodies $A$ and $B$ of masess $m$ and $2\,m$ with moments of inertia ${I_A}$ and ${I_B}\left( {{I_B} > {I_A}} \right)$ have equal kinetic energy of rotation. If ${L_A}$ and  ${L_B}$  be their angular momentam respectively, then
explanation:
Here, $m_{A}=m, m_{B}=2 m$
Both bodies $A$ and $B$ have equal kinetic energy of rotation
${k_A} = {k_B} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{I_A}\omega _A^2 = \frac{1}{2}{I_B}\omega _B^2 \Rightarrow \frac{{\omega _A^2}}{{\omega _B^2}} = \frac{{{I_B}}}{{{I_A}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Ratio of angular momenta,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{L_{A}}{L_{B}}=\dfrac{I_{A} \omega_{A}}{I_{B} \omega_{B}}=\dfrac{I_{A}}{I_{B}} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{B}}{I_{A}}} \quad \text { [Using eqn (1)] } \\& =\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{A}}{I_{B}}} < 1 \quad\left(\because I_{B}>I_{A}\right)
\therefore \quad L_{B}>L_{A}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A cord is wound round the circumference of wheel of radius $r$. The axis of the wheel is horizontal and moment of inertia about it is $I.{\rm{ }}A$ weight $mg$ is attached to the end of the cord and falls from the rest. After falling through a distance $h$, the angular velocity of the wheel will be
explanation:
From the conservation of energy
$m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
As the cord does not slip $v=r \omega$
$ \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{2mgh}}{{m{r^2} + m{k^2}}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{2mgh}}{{m{r^2} + I}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2mgh}}{{I + m{r^2}}}} $.
subject: Physics

question: The given figure shows a force ${\vec F_1}$ acting on a particle of a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis. The particle describes a circular path with centre $C$ on the axis. The correct option is
explanation:
The displacement of the body is the small arc length given by $d s_{1}=r d \theta$
The small work done by the force ${F_1}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& d W_{1}=F_{1} d s_{1}\left(\cos \phi_{1}\right)=F_{1}\left(r_{1} d \theta\right) \cos \left(90-\alpha_{1}\right) \\& =F_{1}\left(r_{1} d \theta\right) \sin \alpha_{1}\end{aligned}$
The magnitude of the torque produced by the force ${F_1}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& \vec{\tau}_{1}=\vec{r}_{1} \times \vec{F}_{1} \\& \tau_{1}=r_{1} F_{1} \sin \left(\alpha_{1}\right)\end{aligned}$
So the correct option is (4)

question: A uniform solid disc of radius $R$ and mass $\mathrm{M}$ is free to rotate on a frictionless pivot through a point on its rim. If the disc is released from rest in the position shown in the figure the speed of lowest point on the disc in the dashed position is
explanation:
From the parallel-axis theorem,
$I=I_{C M}+M D^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}+M R^{2}=\dfrac{3}{2} M R^{2}$
The pivot point is fixed, so the kinetic energy is entirely rotational around the pivot. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956878/preptracker/questions/q_34840_explanation_1764956877843.png From conservation of energy
$MgR = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{3}{2}M{R^2}} \right){\omega ^2}$
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{4g}}{{3R}}} $
At the lowest point on the rim, $v=2 R \omega=4 \sqrt{\dfrac{R g}{3}}$

question:

A. For translational equilibrium

explanation:

Rotational kinetic energy
$K . E=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}(I \omega) \omega=\dfrac{1}{2} L \omega$
Moment of inertia of a body $=M k^{2}$
So the correct option is (4)

question: If the angular momentum of a rotating body about a fixed axis is increased by $10 \%$. Its kinetic energy will be increased by
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}& K=\dfrac{L^{2}}{2 I} \Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{L_{1}^{2}}{L_{2}^{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{100}{110}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{100}{121} \\& K_{2}=1.21 K_{1}\end{aligned}$
$\%$ change in $K . E$ is $\dfrac{K_{2}-K_{1}}{K} \times 100=21 \%$
Increase in kinetic energy $=21 \%$

question: A constant power is supplied to a rotating disc. The relationship between the angular velocity $(\omega)$ of the disc and number of rotations $(n)$ made by the disc is governed by
explanation:
Power $=\tau \omega$
$\Rightarrow P=I \alpha \omega$
$\Rightarrow \alpha \omega=K \Rightarrow \omega \dfrac{d \omega}{d \theta} \cdot \omega=K$
$\int_{0}^{\omega} \omega^{2} d \omega=K \int_{0}^{\theta} \theta \Rightarrow \dfrac{\omega^{3}}{3}=K \theta \Rightarrow \omega^{3}=3 K .2 \pi n$
$\Rightarrow \omega \alpha n^{1 / 3}$
subject: Physics

explanation:

$1500=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2.4 \times 625 \times t_{o}^{2}$
$t_{o}=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~s}$

question: The moment of inertia of a body about a given axis is $1.2\,kg - {m^2}$. Initially, the body is at rest. In order to produce a rotational kinetic energy of $1500\,J$, and angular acceleration of $25\,rad{s^{ - 2}}$ must be applied about that axis for a duration of
explanation:
As, $I = 1.2\,kg - {m^2},E = 1500\,J$
$\alpha = 25{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} rad{s^{ - 2}},{\omega _0} = 0,t = ?$
Now, $E_{r}=\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{2{E_r}}}{I}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 1500}}{{1.2}}} = 50\,\,rad{s^{ - 1}}$
From, $\omega=\omega_{0}+\alpha t$
$50 = 0 + 25t \Rightarrow t = 2\,s$
